comments.commentBody:
Are these yams grown in the US?  Or are they Imported from other poorer countries making them more expensive for the locals there?

Why not focus on local recipes with local ingredients?

@June Jon

Because some of us enjoy the adventure of looking for and trying new things. This sounds delicious to me!
there are plenty of recipes with "local" ingredients and local of course varies even within the US. Ignore the recipes that don't hold your interest.

Africans I knew back when I was in college would typically make fufu with instant mashed potatoes, kosher potato starch, and a whole lot of elbow grease. It was a nice stand in with thinner soups, but I was pleasantly surprised when I had the opportunity to taste a more authentic version made with yucca root flour.

I would be very interested in having Yewande provide some guidance on making swallows from the tropical Ulu breadfruit. I have incorporated it into many different dishes from savory to sweet desserts. NYT times recently published a recipe featuring Ulu: Mashed Ulu with Passion Fruit Chutney. I did make it per recipe and enjoyed it.
It’s great to see this getting some attention, but, at least in Malawi, the corn flour variation is nsima, not nsmia. —Peace Corps Malawi Alum

So happy to see this here! 
Looking forward to a recipe for sukuma wiki to go with it (hint hint)!
How does step 5 work on an induction burner?
Do you pour soup around this and eat it like a dumpling, or break it off and eay it with your hands, dipping in soup?
Typically, you break it off and eat it with your hands and you dip it in whatever sauces you have or use it to pick up meat or vegetables in the sauces.
If you can find it, use whole grain white corn flour. The flavor is more complex and it’s healthier than the more processed white flour.
Super cool
So cool to see this recipe in the NYT!
To my surprise, I found masa arepa in the Mexican section at the local Aldi market.

I worked for USAID in Kenya one summer when I was in graduate school.  I was a 24-year-old American woman.  The young Kenyan secretaries very kindly befriended me and they were amazed that I had never made ugali.  “How will you get a husband?!” one of them asked.
In Guatemala we also cook ripe plantains in a similar way, but with a stick of cinnamon and no salt. Makes a light dessert, an afternoon snack, or just comfort food.
Don’t you mean yellow plantains? Green plantains are not at all sweet.
Peeling a plantain is not an easy task for newcomers. Some instructions (“See tip”?) would be nice.

I learned this from a Puerto Rican chef. Hold the plaintain vertically against a chopping board.  Using the tip of the knife, slash through the skin lengthwise.  Do the same on the other end.(watch your fingers). Then just peel the two pieces of skin off!  It works when the plaintain is soft.

cut ends off and then cut the peel lengthwise (use an old knife, it will get super sticky/dirty!). AT SINK, UNDER COLD, RUNNING WATER: peel from the center of the cut, getting fingers underneath the peel and lifting. 

doing it under running water makes all the difference!
@David Morris Yewande Komolafe walks us through cutting a plantain on this video for another recipe, it helped me a lot @3:05 https://youtu.be/6sH5rMK0nSo?is=XZ8c7sQOmkFOT2zW

Recipe is poorly written.  Admittedly, I could have read through it a couple of times.  Unclear if garlic should simply be mashed or also chopped.  Pay attention to the 'chopped' vs 'minced' cilantro and red onions.  The chopped items go in the sofrito, the minced cilantro goes in the sour cream and the minced onion is used at the end with 1/2 of the chopped tomatoes to finish the dish.  Don't bother buying a medley of mushrooms - the taste of the mushrooms is lost in the sofrito.

This uncomplicated recipe is not “poorly written” and even seasoned cooks understand the need to take the time to read and reread unfamiliar recipe before beginning. 

Recipe says “smashed” garlic. If more detailed instruction is needed, consider investing just a little time learning basic terms and techniques - this will reduce expectation that all recipes be written at beginner’s level, and increase the confidence to tackle a vast world of great but sparsely detailed recipes. Good luck!
Jean, did you make the recipe? How was it?
There are no fresh plantains available in my rural area; I managed to find frozen ones but they would be unstuffable.  Would bananas work adequately?

Bananas do not hold up very well when cooked unless they are green and a green banana would not add the hint of sweet that a plantain would give.  I could imagine this working with a variety of sweet potatoes or a delicata squash which has that hint of sweetness.

A sharp paring knife, piercing the "skin" at the top and pulling sagittally to the bottom, should do the trick.   Sometimes you need to that in two different locations on the plantain. Tops 2 seconds.

The recommended mushrooms are NOT interchangeable: They're vastly different in texture & taste. And, it would be a huge waste of money to make this recipe with any of the suggested mushrooms, since there are many competing flavors in the recipe. Morels are expensive (and seasonal). Maitake,  although it can be cultivated, is also pricey. Shiitake is more expensive than baby bellas, which are nothing more than white button mushrooms grown in different conditions (same species-Agaricus bisporus).

Your message is confusing to me.  Are you saying don't mix and match?  The recommended mushrooms are wide range of choices.  Shitake and Maitake are both rather cheap in asian grocery stores for the amount you get compared to other items.  Morels are expensive regardless because they are few adn far between.   Can you clarify what you were advising?
This was a lot of work and did not satisfy my plantain craving, as the taste was completely overwhelmed by the toppings, especially tomato.
Why is the oil divided?
@Jean If you put it into "Cooking Mode" you can quite easily determine where the chopped vs. minced ingredients go.

I found this recipe too hard to follow.
Step 6 “add the sofrito“
What is sofrito? It’s not in the ingredient list and it’s not explained.
@B It is explained. Read Step 4 carefully and you’ll get it!
Step 4 says “Make a sofrito by finely chopping…”.
Step 4 describes how to combine the chopped bell pepper, chile, garlic, the chopped onion and chopped cilantro into the sofrito.
I can't eat tomatoes or tomato paste. Any suggestion for a substituion?
interesting idea, but lots of prep time.
I added a can of pinto beans for a little more protein, and I used greek yoyo instead of sour cream for the sauce. So good! My plantains were easy to peel because they were quite soft.
My family can't do heat at any level.  Can I just leave the scotch bonnet out?
try to get large plantains if possible. the larger ones were easier to stuff. I ended up just pouring the stuffing over the small ones, it works.

I ended up using four super large plantains and had no tomato paste and neglected to get a full pound of mushrooms. Still after filling the plantains I could not see them. I think I'd call the recipe tomato and mushroom stuffed plantains and use six of them.
Soooo good. A lot of work but so many delightful tastes in this dish!!

To peel a plantain, cut the ends off, then cut shallowly along one of the ridges. With oiled hands, start at the ridge and begin separating the skin from the flesh with your thumbs in an outward motion.

title: Ìlá (Okra Soup With Shrimp and Greens) Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: What a lovely read, a paean to okra. I have not made this yet but as soon as my little plant in the garden has a yield, I plan to. Thank you, Yewande Komolafe, for brightening my morning.

Lovely write-up, and the soup sounds fantastic. Unfortunately there is zero chance I could find the three less common ingredients in my area, and no possible substitutes are presented, even though only one tablespoon of each ingredient is needed for the recipe.
@Al B. All 3 were available through Amazon.

@Jackiei 
I hate having to buy even my food, from Amazon. I don’t want Amazon in every facet of my life. I’ll try some local stores for those exotic ingredients and agree that the chef would be required to provide substitutions!  And I live in Brooklyn, NYC with lots of options.

Folks, I have just made this dish precisely as instructed.  I am an experienced, 66 year old cook who has a broad pallet and have eaten all kinds of from many different styles and cuisines.  

I live in Houston, Texas, where we have a thriving food culture from all around the world.  I purchased the dried ground crawfish and the fermented locust beans from a food market called Wazobia African Market.  Both products were fresh and stored in refrigeration.

Just want to let you know that the fermented locust beans are quite pungent.  As is the ground crawfish, but not as much so.  As it turns out, the flavor of the dish when cooked retains the pungent odor, and the taste is not pleasant to either me or my partner. (We both love okra in many forms!)  We have chosen not to eat it.  I am sure it is well loved by many, and that is great.  I simply want to say if the aroma of the special ingredients is not pleasing to you, I expect the flavors will not be as well.

@Steve Gardner I have lived along the Gulf Coast for many many years and have a great affection for okra, shrimp, and all the related dishes. I have typically been instructed to cook the okra first to remove the okra slime or at least reduce it. I went with the instructions on this one to possibly see how it’s done in another part of the world and I will say the slime level is really really intense. I’m sure I can dilute it with stock or something like that but a better cook than me might make a suggestion about cooking the okra first to at least reduce the slime content.

I made this dish yesterday with the ingredients I could find. I’m based in a small town in the Netherlands, so I couldn’t find fermented locust bean or crayfish powder. However, a previous commenter suggested subbing locust bean with red miso. I used the white miso I had at hand and found shrimp paste in an international market. I added goat meat and chicken thighs to mine, and it came out wonderfully. I only wish I added more okra, but that’s a personal preference. I will definitely make this again and hope to see similar recipes like this in the future.

It warms my heart to see this here as it’s a weekly stable on our table. Most African stores and Costcos in some places will stock the locust beans and palm oil, I however find you can replace the locust beans with anything that gives an umami flavor.
Wish I could make this but too many esoteric ingredients to bother. Sorry.
I would like to make this but even with the tip, some of these ingredients are just not that easy to get locally.
@MarkB you will have to order online!

After some tinkering, I have come up with an approximation for dawadawa. it is not authentic or perfect, but it is close.
1/2 teaspoon red miso
1/4 teaspoon mushroom powder
1/8 teaspoon gochujang
No palm oil . Kills the orangutans in Indonesia . Find a sub.
I detest okra — what’s a good substition?
Why would you want to make an okra soup if you don't like okra? The article explains the importance of okra to the soup.
Vichyssoise?
This sounds great, but I have questions. Do you cut in one inch pieces and then chop it? I do not have a food processor. Can frozen okra be used?

For anyone having a hard time finding ingredients, the Iru you can get from Burlap & Barrel online…the crayfish…maybe substitute with shrimp? And palm oil you can get anywhere online.  Hope that’s helpful.
The accompanying article said that frozen okra was an acceptable, if inferior, option.  I find much frozen okra to be woody.  Is there a brand that the author or other readers prefer?
If you happen to live in Texas, the grocery store chain HEB has great HEB-brand frozen okra.
Flavor was OKish. I knew okra was slimy but this was REALLY slimy.

title: Fish and Lentils With Sauce Rof Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Salty and incredible. My wife and I talked about how we enjoyed this dish more than a string of recent takeout nights. This was a o good that it prompted this, my first, NYT comment!

Perfect and well received by my 11 year old. Used red lentils and barramundi, one scotch bonnet pierced and dropped in the lentils while they cooked, then fished out prior to serving. The fish needed an extra 5 minutes. Served over rice.

This dish was absolutely delicious! I had a lot of lovely spring herbs from my CSA so I riffed on the Sauce Rof a bit with chives and cilantro, rather than parsley, and string beans instead of spinach at the end. Otherwise, I followed Yewande's technique for lentils and fish which was so, so yummy. So great, I can't wait to make this again.

Yewande recommends using a food processor to make the rof, or if not, then chopping by hand. I thought I’d try it with a blender, and while the texture was perfectly fine, the raw sauce was far too astringent. Learn from my mistake! A gentle chop up would have been better. 

Nonetheless, the rof that got cooked worked well and the lentils were divine. I’ll definitely try this one again!

@Dana
I was thinking it that too!  I like my sauces pretty saucy, so chopped doesn’t work that well for me. Was thinking of part blended and part hand chopped?  Maybe I’ll just try to get pretty fine with the good processor. I’m curious why the blended sauce would be sharper.

I used French lentils. Lentils with fish are great. This was delicious with multi layered flavors. The lentils pack a real protein punch. I had it for dinner followed by multiple lunches at room temperature. I’ll definitely make it again.

This sounds delicious, and I look forward to making it. I have to say though, at least in northern Senegal where I live, no one would ever serve fish with lentils, or sprinkle uncooked rof on top of anything. To make the rof more authentic, substitute lime for lemon (there are no lemons in Senegal), and add a little bit of green bell pepper and crushed green bell pepper seeds too.

Excellent! Making it as spicy as you can handle it - makes it even more complex. I marinated the fish for about 4 hours and I’ve reserved some of the delicious rof to use on something else - the sky is the limit for its uses. I added some cilantro to the rof and used 2 scotch bonnet peppers. I used 2 Wegman’s steamable lentil packets and TJs frozen bronzini fillets. Microwaved the lentil packets then put them in the pot with oil and 1.5 cups of water and followed the rest of the recipe. Used spinach and mustard greens. So good.

Fish  (cod) and black lentils cooked perfectly. I marinated the fish for a few hours and soaked the lentils in that time. Looked beautiful but it lacked flavor. I had halved the jalapeno so that might have been it but if I were to make this again I would add cilantro to the mix of herbs. I like that this was a complete dinner with the lentils and spinach in the same pot as the fish. Timing was right for me. Just a bit too  mild...
Followed instructions to a T and came out with burned lentils and undercooked fish. Would cook the lentils for less time before adding the fish and probably add a bit more water at the jump.
@Nina C some of my lentils burned a little, too

In general—delicious. And. It’s summer!! It was a hot evening here. Guests outside. Me inside over a hot stove? No thank you. So. I simmered the soaked lentils with water in the same way I do for lentil salads I make. I skipped the extra oil. (Went outside and enjoyed a glass of wine with my friends.) When they (the lentils) still had a nice bite to them (resistance), I drained them and put them in a wide serving platter and added the rof. Then I put the marinated cod drizzled with EVOO in the oven (in a deep pan uncovered) 375? That’s what I chose—Not sure that’s the best option. But the cod was juicy.  About 18 minutes for 3.5 lbs.  check it to make sure it breaks apart with a fork. Added spinach at last minute and covered. Watch those pan handles coming out of the oven!!! Served with zucchini and shallots, roasted peppers, naan, tzatziki…I love the “confetti” of rof. It’s celebratory.
This sauce rof really resembles green seasoning from Trinidad and Tobago (and other Caribbean locales as well). Delightful!
Delicious , fresh, and a bit salty. Repurposes well (and decreases the salinity) as a soup by simmering in water and adding a bit more spinach!

My spouse had a heart attack last month so we have been searching for intensely-flavored heart-healthy food that makes him happy. This rof sauce is all that. 

I make the lentils separately, the rice in the rice cooker and the rof sauce all on its own. Those three ingredients live in the refrigerator until we are ready to throw them together with fish, chicken, tofu or all by themselves. Or, I mix some of the rof sauce with Greek yogurt for variety. I use Chico blonde rice.

This is one of the most delicious dishes I’ve ever made! I would serve this to my most discerning guests. That sauce—think I would call it a salsa, though—is everything. I used Serrano peppers and seeded one of them, and the heat was on the just detectable. For my fish, I used tilapia, and instead of spinach, I used Swiss chard, because that’s what I had home. While the chard was good, spinach or Lacinato kale would be better. My only small problem was with the lentils: some of them got a little crispy on the bottom as the fish was finishing cooking. I think I’d use 3 cups of water next time or just pay attention and add water when they look dry. I also didn’t add as much salt. I didn’t salt the fish and I didn’t add the salt to the greens before they wilted, and I can’t imagine needing the extra salt from those 2 steps. I served roasted potatoes alongside, which was perfect, if perhaps unconventional.
So good, healthy and easy; will definitely make repeatedly and share.  Served over rice.  Recommend not drinking while preparing this dish though as it doubled my prep / cook time.  Oops
has the nutritional information link been removed from recipes at NYT Cooking?  I no longer see it . I previously selected certain recipes based on nutritional information supplied.
How much is a “bunch “ of parsley? A bunch here in MI is quite a hefty amount. In Europe the bunch would be much smaller.

I don’t think I’ve ever seen parsley sold in a bunch in Europe. I’m in Germany and I simply tell the green grocer how much I want and that’s what I get. Just use your taste and common sense as a guide on how much to use.

My husband loved it. Made with barramundi, black lentils, and Serrano chili, because neither of us likes much heat. My herb garden produces a prodigious amount of tarragon. Any thoughts on whether it would work in this recipe?

Used corvina and otherwise followed the recipe closely, using more garlic and lemon juice. I think the cook times will be highly variable based on the size of the skillet/pan/pot so just watch your lentils for their change in texture. It was fantastic.

Ridiculously delicious! Flavorful, hearty, healthy. Adaptable (I only had a half bunch of scallions on hand, lowered the heat by using only one jalapeño, and I’m not certain if I used French lentils or just the smallest brownish lentils I could find). Came out perfect anyway! I served it with some crusty, whole grain bread. Amazing!!! Even told my fish guy at the store about it (we exchange our favorite fish recipes). Just fabulous!
Despite the unfamiliar ingredients and prep method, this was a first time winner! Fearful of the heat, I used a fresh Anaheim chili, will try a roasted version next time. Yummy!

i doubled the lentils and coinciding water bc i had was 2lbs of fish (cod) 
soaked the lentils for hours! before cooking
i used 2 large jalapeños, id use a 3rd next time 
used more garlic, great
added more salt after plating than i’d hoped, loved the acid of the lemon to finish
really a beautiful dish! fresh, bright, green and beautifully comfort food, but elevated and 
de-li-cious!!! 
i had baby zucchini to use so i added them with the lentils prior to adding fish
i’d add more spinach next time, or any other greens that needed using -felt very elegant lol not sure why, but really loved preparing and! eating this dish thank you!

This idea is good, but the recipe and instructions are not good. I used black lentils and this came out FAR too soupy. My fish (16 oz of cod) took several minutes longer to cook than listed.

Other issues:
* the salting is imprecise and relies on too much salt in the sauce rof vs properly salting the other components
* marinating and cooking the sauce rof loses most of its flavor -- it's much better as a topping
* there's really no reason to add 1/4 C oil to the lentils or to soak the lentils
@K I was wondering about that 1/4 cup oil for the lentils! Next time I’ll omit
This dish was fantastic! My family loved it. It was also very easy to make. I will surely be making it again.

I loved this—and that’s saying a lot because I’m a hard sell on fish. This was full of flavor and I will make it again—frequently!

I followed the recipe fairly closely. I don’t have Scotch bonnets, so I used a hot red pepper. The biggest change was that I cut up onions, which I fried and then added chopped parsley and a chopped tomato before adding the lentils. The tomato gave a good texture and tasted good.
tilapia and black lentils, jalapeno, hand chopped the rof. Served over brown rice. An excellent, healthy meal.

I love how protein packed and delicious this is. Search for Monterey Bay's "Super Green List" if you want to find the most sustainable fish to use in this recipe (US-based).

I try to avoid salmon, tuna, and shrimp due to their overfishing and environmental impacts. I wish NYT had more recipes like this for other more sustainable seafood and fish instead of focusing on those big 3!

Crazy delicious. Especially the sauce rof cooked with the lentils. Halved the recipe for solo dining, and used one Serrano pepper in the sauce. Will definitely make again. Used sprouted green lentils, and the cook time was much shorter. Approx 2:1 ratio of water to sprouted lentils, for saucy lentils. Used defrosted snapper fillets. Can imagine that using fresh fish of choice would only kick this up another level.

So delicious! I modified the herbs in the sauce - added cilantro, mint, basil, dill, parsley- great way to use up fridge herbs. Add some halved cherry tomatoes to the lentils before layering in the fish- adds some acidity and color! Yum!
Has anyone tried this with salmon? For those who like salmon, would it be an ok substitute?

I’m wondering the same, if salmon fillet would be an ok substitute for white fish fillet? I’m sure the sauce and the salmon would be delicious but I’m not sure about the salmon with the lentils… 
Feedback is welcome please!
Would brown lentils work?

Brown lentils cook faster and break down more readily than French or black lentils. If you don't mind a mushier texture/look to the dish (it may make the whole thing more "grainy" in general, a bit like half-smashed beans) and having to guesstimate the lentil cook times (less than what's stated here)... in addition, imo the "lentil" taste permeates more, since it's broken down throughout - if all that sounds good (or a fair tradeoff for a quicker dish), then yes, brown lentils can be subbed.
I would love to try this recipe but I don’t care for parsley. What would be a suggested sub here? Could I try 50/50 parsley and cilantro? Would a whole bunch of cilantro be too strong?
@bakebake.   I just made this for dinner and used half parsley and half cilantro.  We loved that combination - I hope you will as well
What might the result be with 1/2 of the oil?  I struggle with the nutritional content of this with fat grams higher than both carb and protein grams.

@Nancy   I'm a dietitian. The first amount of fat (being 31 g), is the total amount of fat. The rest of the fat is just a breakdown into those different types. This means that there is more carbohydrate at 34 g and more protein at 37 g than there is fat. Also, the fat is mostly mono and polyunsaturated fat, which are the heart healthy types of fat. This is a very healthy recipe. I hope this helps!
Looks delicious.  Can this be done with a slow cooker that has a sauté/simmer function?
Has anyone tried this recipe with fresh trout?
Recipe says to serve with the reserved 1/4C sauce. Is the sauce to be heated?
Recipe says to serve with the reserved 1/4C sauce. Is the sauce to be heated?
So so delicious!  This will happen on the regular.

This was absolutely delectable and even convinced my lentil-skeptical husband and son that lentils could be delicious! Made exactly as written, deseeded a large serrano pepper, used halibut for the fish.

I used red lentils and tilapia. I ended up cooking tilapia filets from Trader Joe’s for about 15mins and it was well cooked without being overdone. The “sauce” was fantastic! Lemony and spicy and herby, mwah!
Absolutely delicious!
Delicious!  I followed the recipe exactly with sea bass.  We loved it and I’ll definitely make it again!
Panfried whiting and served it atop the lentil mix criwned with the salsa and it was terrific and very healthy

I wanted to make half the recipe - I was fine until I got to the lemon.  The full recipe calls for a whole lemon, with all of it zested; then it’s halved, with 1 half juiced and the other split into wedges.  But the scaled down version is confusing to follow - it calls for 1/2 a lemon, and I’m assuming you zest the half, then halve the half, juice one of the quarters and split the other into 2 eighths for serving - but that’s really not the way it reads.  Can someone clarify it?

Recommend! I made this last night with fresh haddock. I loved it, while my husband enjoyed but thought it needed more oomph. 
I don’t enjoy super spicy dishes, so i used one jalapeño, deseeded, not chopped up. Other than this, i followed the recipe exactly.

Lovely and delicious dish! Our fish was mahi mahi we caught yesterday! I added half a cup of basmati rice to the lentils before adding the fish fillets
Also as per comments added more heat from home grown hot Serrano chilies. 
I didn’t have spinach but grow dinosaur or Tuscan kale which I blanched and added to the skillet 
Delicious!

This was a delicious recipe. The first time I cooked it, I had some salmon that I grilled gently instead of cooking it with the lentils. Still put the sauce on, but added spinach to the lentils. It was really good.

The second time, I used cod and followed directions . Also delicious. I used a shallot instead of two of the 6 garlic cloves. I found both times the lentils were a tiny bit too salty. So I didn’t salt the fish after marinating it. I will reduce the salt for next time.

I didn’t have spinach, but I did have a bunch of small collards from my CSA box. To accommodate their longer cooking time, I removed their tough stems, chopped them up, and simmered them in with the lentils. Required a little extra liquid but otherwise followed the recipe. Delicious! And honestly, the lentils and collards simmered with the sauce rof would’ve made for a tasty vegetarian meal on their own!
A delicious and healthy dish, with tilapia, and I replaced the spinach with fresh chard, stems and previously cooked leaf and added it in the last few minutes. I would like to try sole next time.
Wow!! So delicious.  A home run.  And I was sweating a bit from the heat, which is always fun!

This was fabulous! I used haddock and jalapeño for the heat. Will definitely make again, so flavorful. I used pardina lentils from Goya, which are nutty and hold their shape. The lentils cooked much faster than the recipe so I just made adjustments as I cooked. But overall this was quick and easy (after a fair bit of chopping) and I can see trying different greens and chiles next time.

This was so good! I used cod & jalapeños. Next time I will leave seeds in jalapeños to add a bit more heat. I added dill to the food processor because I had some on hand. Otherwise I followed the recipe. I can’t recommend this recipe enough!
Delicious!  I used cod, and two seeded habaneros and extra garlic for sauce rof. Just the right amount of heat.  I could have eaten the whole batch of lentils myself - they are that good!
On a scale of 1 to 10 our spice tolerance is a 1. Can someone suggest a substitute?
@Cooker I would use a green bell pepper, then

Delicious and easy! I have been wanting to try more of Yewande’s recipes since I read an article about her. I loved how this recipe used the sauce rof in three different ways (fish marinade, to cook the lentils and as a fresh sauce at the end)- genius and makes is easy while punching a ton of flavor. Also healthy. Will be trying more of her recipes!

Made these last weekend with 8 T butter and added 1 cup of raisins. My wife judged them the best cookies ever and requested a new batch for her mah jong group. 

After a day or two a few minutes in a hot oven restores the exceptional initial crispiness.
Made with cod. Very good and will make again. The French green lentils I used too twice as long to cook as recipe timing. Next time I will start cooking lentils as first step.
I am wondering if I could use freshwater fish? crappie,catfish,bass?
Why not olive oil? Those neutral oils are so highly processed..
Wanted to love this. So many great ingredients combined in a way that should have been outstanding and we found it totally lacking flavor. Not a hit in this house.
As vegans, we used slabs of pressed tofu instead of fish. For herbs, we used everything we had in our garden including thyme, cilantro, mint, and chopped garlic scapes. Great recipe!!!
@Kateroo ... I like your approach - simplified the whole recipe andI like a bite to my lentils.  I'll give it a try soon

Made as instructed, using tilapia and 2 scotch bonnets, which I didn't seed because I like heat.  The fish and lentils were perfectly cooked, and the dish certainly "interesting" -- but I'm unsure whether I liked it enough to make it again.  Perhaps it was too green-tasting for me; if I repeat, I'll add some warming spice like cinnamon or cumin.
This was delicious!! I used basil instead of parsley, the lentils dried out but I just added a small splash of water. Yum!

I just used whatever herbs there were in the garden (dill, mint, cilantro, tarragon, and garlic chives) and 2 Serrano's, which was just-tolerable spicy, and served it with some sturdy whole wheat sourdough bread, toasted. Delish!

Such a surprise!  Low effort and delicious

We use 3 Fresno chilis and no green onions (had none). It was so very good
This was phenomenal.
This dish is super yummy!!!!!

This is a really good recipe.  The only thing I'd do differently is soak the lentils overnight or for longer than the recipe suggests.  I used a couple medium/large jalapeños since I couldn't find the Scotch bonnet chiles.  All the flavors came together to create a special dish we will make again.
Made this tonight, and it was delicious.  I used chopped arugula from the garden for the greens since that was what I had, and it worked fine.
Reasonably fast prep, easy to cook, tasted delightful.
Tastes good but more like a fish stew. Too much water since I soaked them first. Agree with others- not spicy enough.
Alright, I'll use some paid time off to make these and replenish my kitchen on the fourth day.

"Jeera" is the Hindustani name for cumin. In the 1800s, the British brought Indian and Chinese indentured laborers to the Caribbean; indenture was one level above slavery, which had been abolished in Britain in 1834. Caribbean dishes such as roti or dal puri are of Indian origin; Chinese immigrants contributed soy sauce and stir-frying.
For just 1 potato, microwaving the cubes for 6-8 mins is simpler.
Zesting the orange rather than adding/removing the peel avoids the bitter pith.

I haven't tried this recipe but it sounds like someone took the filling from Jamaica, the puff pastry crust from Haiti, and added a little Trinidadian/Guyanese heat to a patty.  This patty is a food metaphor of Flatbush Avenue!
I can’t stop thinking about Sweeney Todd.

The recipe sounds amazing, I can't wait to try it!  I love Patties of all types and origins!  It will be easier with the puff pastry. Great job of putting together your take on the restaurant's delicious dish. 
Just a clarification for the first comment- Cumin or Jeera is an annual herb grown to harvest the cumin seed, which is commonly used as a spice in Asian, Mexican, Indian, and Middle Eastern recipes. Cumin is native to the Mediterranean. Seeds found in Syria have been dated back to the second millennium BC and there are many references to cumin in Biblical text of the Old and New Testaments. It was introduced to the Americas by Spanish and Portuguese colonist. Today the plant is commercially grown in the Indian subcontinent, Northern Africa, Mexico, and Chile. So it has been around for awhile, and used by many cultures.
The potato cooked as described in the recipe is to use the potato water to add moisture and the natural starches to thicken the mixture. Boiling produces a better starchy water than microwaving.

I'll be cooking this, this weekend, and adding some peas and carrots!

I agree that this recipe, while yielding a tasty meat pie, is a lot of effort and mess for the results. I don't think I'll make these again, but they were tasty. And I went rogue and dipped them in yellow mustard, like a knish (sorry if that offends).

The instructions to handle the dough in the article are perfect and ought to be part of the recipe. "Gently unravel the sheet with conviction, and it will glide while maintaining its composure. Stretch the dough into a rectangle. If it begins to tear, you’ve gone too far. (Don’t worry. It’s endlessly fixable: Trim the edge to press the scraps into any tears.)"
Delicious recipe - loved the technique with the puff pastry! They came out perfect!
After reading Step 9  I decided to not try it

I am going to the store to get the ingredients and I want to share a tip. Whenever I have a recipe with 5 or more spices and seasonings that I may not have on hand or have a few but not all - I go to the grocery store with bulk spices and I create the blend right there in one bag. I put the code for the spice that is in the mid-range cost of all of them. That part of the recipe is done before I get home. This is definitely one of those recipes!
These look delicious, but I think I'll just use the filling for a regular pot pie and save some time.

NYT Cooking, which steps in the recipe fall into the 20-minute prep versus the 2+ hour cooking time? I know this has been discussed in the comments of other recipes, but I’d like to hear the answer from NYT.  To me, prep is everything until you slide the dish into the oven. Cooking time is how long that dish spends in the oven.

I assume you mean the filling: "Take 2 pastry slices out of the fridge at a time, lightly flour them and roll each into a 4-inch round. Brush the surface of one round with the egg wash and place ¼ cup (58 to 60 grams) in the center."
Made this with Beyond Meat and it is lovely. I made the filling the day before and finished it the next day. Served with sliced mango on the side and cilantro mint chutney sauce. Yummy.
Why does this recipe and so many others list KOSHER salt as an ingredient? There is no difference between kosher and non-kosher salt.

Kosher salt is produced through a specific process called raking, where saltwater is evaporated to create large, flaky crystals, while regular salt is refined and processed to produce smaller, uniform crystals.
@Alan Feingold table salt and sea salt are 2x as salty as kosher salt. So adjust your salt down if not using kosher salt
I will def be making this. But I will use a turmeric British hot water pastry crust and turn it into a “pie”. BAM!!!

This was a fun and delicious project.  I didn't see any other comments from people who live in places where we are thrilled to be able to get Pepperidge Farm puff pastry so don't complain about not being able to get Dafour, so on our behalf I will say, PF worked great! I used one box/two sheets, and cut each into 10 pieces, which worked out perfectly for the amount of filling.  Used a habanero (with seeds) instead of Scotch bonnet, and it seemed like a good amount of spice, if you're ok with spice. Takes some time, but all good!  And pretty!

The calls for ground lamb or beef.
Why not ground goat, like in the original?

No reason why not.. For that matter, you could use ground chicken, turkey, pork, or even a vegan version with chopped and sauteed extra-firm tofu - it's really the spices and pastry that make the dish. Goat is ubiquitous in the Caribbean (one of Jamaica's national dishes is Curry Goat)- but harder to obtain in the US, and the premium you pay here isn't worth it.

The orange peel: is it just stripped off in large pieces  and added? shouldn't it be removed before adding the potatoes, then? Or is it shredded or chopped into small dice? Or is the orange peel just grated while the orange is still whole?
Large pieces.  It comes out with the bay leaf and thyme sprigs at the end of Step 5
Do you not brush the outer surface of the patty with egg wash once you've sealed them together with the filling inside?
Could you make these on a whole sheet of puff pastry and cover with the 2nd?  Cut into rectangles to serve?
@Cathy  Don't let anyone tell you that you can't. But, please, prepare the recipe as written, just once, before you change it up so you have some expectation of what the creator intended.

From the peeling and chopping to the food processing and ultimately, the cooking, this turned out to be a very involved 2-day job for this yardie.  They turned out well, but the sweat equity required means that I'd readily seek out store-bought options here in the city before attempting this recipe again.

Grew up on these. Every Caribbean island has a version of this. In Trinidad, they’re called beef pies. This filling sounds wonderful, but for less fuss, no potato (potato is Jamaican beef patty), season with salt, garlic, onion, any hot pepper, parsley, thyme, oregano, or an Italian spice mix. Cilantro, even. That simple. And don’t overstuff the filling. The joy is the buttery flakiness of the pastry against the savory earthiness of spicy beef. You can use any ground meat.
After you roll and cut the pinwheels of puff pastry, do you lay them flat to roll them into circles? That would seem to ruin the puff part of the puff pastry. Please clarify. Thanks.

@Hans I'm not sure what you mean by "flat," but I stood them up so that if you looked at them from above, you would see the spiral, and then rolled them into 4" circles.  They puffed when baked and were lovely.
Made 1/2 batch.  Used 1 sheet of puff pastry, rolled out into a large rectangle.  Cut into 12” rectangular pieces, making 6 patties.
This works very well as a vegetarian/vegan version substituting the meat for a plant based crumble. Reduce the amount of liquids in order to make it too mushy.

What is the role of the írù?  What does it taste like?  Even in the cooks' paradise of San Francisco, I haven't found any and likely won't stock it.  Would you provide a substitution recommendation?  I'd probably just leave it out but I suspect it might be included for texture and flavor.

@Shelley an article that collected several African recipes suggested Chinese fermented black beans, which I can get in my local Asian grocery, for this ingredient in another recipe. I might try this with those.
You can get iru from Burlap and Barrel.

Expensive, complicated, with many hard to find, one might even say rare, ingredients. Given our current economy ($117.26 for a tank of gas today in Northern California) combined with the daily terror of living in the USA, affordable, accessible, nurturing recipes are what this reader hungers for.

What a beautiful, inspired dish. I loved reading the recipe and learning about the flavours—at least three new to me. I feel as though I can almost taste it. Looking forward to trying it next time in Houston at ChòpnBlọk!
I am farly certain I do not want to mix by hand anything that contains scotch bonnet chiles.... !!!!

This recipe sounds delicious.  Almost all the comments are kvetching, and I can’t begin the guess why.  There’s not that many unusual ingredients, and the vegetables that make up the bulk of the ingredients are easy to find and relatively inexpensive.  I’m going to make it without the smoked hake, and I’m going to sub fermented black beans for the locust beans.

title: Spiced Coconut Tapioca Pudding With Fruit Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Very strong on the lemongrass front. I didn’t mind it, certainly a savoury-leaning desert.
this recipe is in the throwdown category... will anyone ever make it?
@Eatingathomebutwhy I will by the end of the weekend. I have all the ingredients including lemongrass growing out front that survived the Florida freezes. But not tonight. Rest time.
ME. I have tapioca pearls and made a dark chocolate pudding yesterday. Will def try this one too.
I wonder if I could cook a few hours before guests then rewarm? Or would it go gluggy? Prefer not to cook dessert “live” after a glass of wine or two :)

Ah cool - 1 world! We have literally the same recipe in Austria! (minus all-spice and dry milk powder) The handling is the same. Here they‘re called „Buchteln“ and are also served with butter and honey (the traditional christmas eve version) or topped with vanilla custard. I‘ll absolutely try this recipe!
Can you share eggless version of these pull apart bread

title: Mọin-Mọin for a Gathering (Savory Bean Pudding) Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody:
Made this today using black-eyed peas, dried crayfish, and smoked whitefish. I used parchment paper instead of banana leaves because I didn’t have the time this week to go to the right grocery store, and substituted Thai chilies for Scotch bonnet because that’s what I had on hand.
Followed directions almost exactly otherwise, but felt like the end result was a bit more watery and not quite as firm as I expected or hoped.

Thank you so much for bringing back memories of this delicious, nutritious dish. I was a Peace Corps volunteer in Cameroon in the late 1970s. I ate this dish at least weekly for two years and it provided me vegetables and protein in an otherwise limited and monotonous diet. While I’m sure there are many variations, the ingredients in this recipe matches quite closely to how it was prepared for me nearly 50 years ago.

Highly adaptable. The essentials are red palm oil and some kind of oily smoked fish. I used a variety of greens -- kale, radish greens, pea shoots, and spinach -- all from the local CSA.  I happened to have smoked alewives on hand, but mackerel or sardines would likely work here as well.

Similar to many dishes of stewed greens and smoked or dried fish found across West and Central Africa. As with the previous commenter, it brought back many fond memories of time spent in that part of the world.

title: Poulet DG (Cameroonian Chicken and Plantains) Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: What are the odds? I was just searching to see if there was a restaurant in NYC where I could have this. I first tried this in Marseille two years ago and it was delicious. I went back everyday while I was in town.
If you cannot find plantains in your grocery store, Trader Joe’s has frozen cooked ripe plantains.
@Marissa, would you still sauté/fry to caramelize?  It does seem a good way of avoiding using a cup and a half of oil…
@EOregon, I would try a sauté in a small amount of butter….thise caramelized edges are SO good. Personally I would avoid the deep fry. BRB headed to Trader Joe’s
@EOregon  I love the ones from TD's. I just put them in my air fryer, and they crisp up nicely. Would definitely do that for this recipe to save a step.

This looks delicious! I was a Peace Corps volunteer in Cameroon but never got to try this dish. The only thing missing is the Maggi seasoning. I can't imagine they would cook this in Cameroon without a dash of Maggi.
@Dan hi there. Not certain, but I think the Better than Bouillon subs for Maggi.

@Dan when were you there?  I was also a PCV in Cameroun and don’t remember having this dish.  I cannot wait to try it.  I have had my share of chicken in peanut sauce there and here and in other African countries, never as good here as there (except on the campus once at University of Cape Town which brought me right back to
Maroua.
@Maxine @dan me too! Rpcv Maroua here! Thus was a special occasion dish. Made it with Maggi and some piedmont and. Chefs kiss. Perfect. Cameroon me manque beaucoups.

I know Yewande is overcoming some severe physical limitations. This recipe proves the most important asset in cooking is not your hands but your imagination , taste buds and power of observation. Bravo, Maestro Yewande!

@Mark
Ditto - Bravo Yewande!

If I follow these directions, I'm ending the recipe at the point where I'm crushing tomatoes in an open can. Because the next step is a trip to the ER for stitches! Seriously, get another bowl dirty and be safe!
(I do plan to make this, but I just had to comment on that. Even with a large can, I'm clumsy and have big hands, so I won't be doing that.)

@Lauren Good call on the potential tomato massacre. To save a trip to the ER, I take a pair of cooking scissors and cut up the tomatoes in the can. If I miss any, I can always go after them in the pot!
seems odd to add skin on chicken to the liquid without first searing the chicken

I just happened to have all these ingredients in my refrigerator when this recipe came out. It was delicious! My only suggestion is with how to handle the plantains. My plantains were very ripe so I sautéed them in about two tablespoons of oil which browned them perfectly. I put them in with the vegetables as directed and felt like they broke down a little too much. Next time I will wait to add them with about 5 minutes left to cook on the dish. My wife doesn’t eat chicken so I took out some sauce and veggies and added it to some baked tofu for her which she enjoyed even though it wasn’t purely vegetarian.

The flavor is incredible. It’s worth it to make a double or expanded batch … all those ingredients for only 4-6 servings didn’t seem efficient from a cost or time perspective. I doubled the plantains and most vegetables including the chile. Also used 2.75 lbs skinless boneless thighs to get more meat out of it and still fit it into my Dutch oven. I did not need to double the canned tomatoes but I spent a little less time reducing everything (just did it all on the stove).-

Thrilled to see Yewande bring another African classic to NYT! I’m a vegetarian so this is Tofu DG for me… but seriously—Cameroonian food is incredible, and this is a standout. Put some Makossa on and dedicate a Saturday to this!
If you cannot find ripe plantains, Trader Joe’s has frozen cooked ripe plantains.
@AMN, you might try 116th Street in Harlem or Crown Heights in Brooklyn. La Savane is quite good, and Africa Kine is excellent.

Beautiful flavors and easily adapted for milder heat by changing out the pepper type or amount. Having ripe enough plantains is so important and can be challenging to find. Using frozen ripened plantains to even sweet potato medallions would help provide the sweet contrast. The next time I cook this dish I would definitely brown the chicken since most don’t enjoy boiled chicken skin.

This version is vibrant, balanced, and deeply satisfying. The layers of aromatics, peppers, and gentle heat create a sauce that feels both comforting and celebratory. It’s approachable enough for a weeknight yet impressive enough for guests. A beautifully written and thoughtfully developed recipe that captures the spirit of the dish. I did shortcut by using purchased maduros and cooking everything on the stove and with that - this could easily be a worknight, weeknight meal.

oh this is GOOD! Favours are even better as it sits! I don't have anything to compare it to as I have never had this before but figured id try it since I had everything to make it! only things I swapped was habanero powder instead of fresh and dried herbs as well. super good! I wish I could have this out at a restaurant! any recommendations for Toronto recommended!

I should have read the comments before making this. I have easy access to plantains, but no experience cooking with them. I'm pretty sure the ones I chose were not ripe enough. The Trader Joe's frozen plantain tip is definitely one I will use next time! It will save the frying mess.
Can I marinate this for more than 12 hours or will that make the chicken too mushy? Thx.
Followed every step. . took all day!  Good results!  Love the plantains but not sure if I'll make again.  Took all day!
One of the best NYT chicken recipes I've made. Worth all the extra effort if you have time. I used carrots and yellow squash for the veg.
Is there a reason that you couldn’t toss the plantain coins in a bit of oil and fry in a cast-iron frying pan?
I don't like chicken thighs. Can one use breasts? If so, what are the modifications?
@IDM Normally it is a whole chicken that has been butchered. I would just recommend bone-in
Soooooo I don't think grocery stores near me in NH have plantains.  Can I substitute bananas?

@Eileen no no no. Please don’t 😀
Thus is really good even without the plantains
Do you get sweet plantains or the non-sweet?
Sweet
@Lauren that was the first thing I thought of when reading this recipe. I also have large hands

Lots of ingredients. Lots of prep time. Just good, not amazing. Didn't use Better than Bouillon and that was probably why it was just good. If I made again I'd probably use Worcestershire or fish sauce. It wasn't very spicy. Would have added more heat if I make again. It wasn't obvious where to add the bouquet garni or the bay leaves in. I added with the liquid with the chicken towards the end. I probably would have rated it lower but I like that it is a flavor profile I've never tried before.

One review!!?? Dang, what's up..??
Makes me nervous.. probably because it takes almost 6-8 hours to cook..??
Looks AWESOME!!

One note - the recipe isn’t clear on what you do with the bouquet garnis one the chicken has marinated. It goes in with the reserved marinade when you fry it. I waited until after frying the marinade to add it but you could do it in the fry step as well. The aromas from this dish are awesome

This sounds fantastic but the calorie count gives this GLP1 guy serious pause. I'm curious if air frying the plantains would help reduce the calories? Otherwise the recipe doesn't look too bad on that issue.

I think that the nutritional information might be counting ALL of the frying oil as part of the recipe. You aren't actually going to be ingesting all that oil. Also I think brushing the plantains with oil and air frying would probably be fine.
@dan a well stocked Latin grocery has maggi, or definitely online!

Delicious and hearty! Not a quick weeknight meal, but don’t be intimated by the time listed—most of that is for marinading. I used dried herbs because my grocery was out of fresh, and also swapped the habanero with a jalapeño for someone sensitive to heat, and it was a crowd pleaser.

I made the dish and my picky kids really liked it, it's sort of like an African spiced chicken cacciatore. I did brown the thighs first as that missing instruction seemed odd to me.  With the amount of sauté time, you may want to put your bouquet garni in some cheesecloth instead of just tying it if you want to be able to fish it out at the end.  Recipe does take a fair amount of time to cook so plan accordingly.

I've got a lot of problems with this recipe, and now you're gonna hear about it. Way too many steps involved. The marinade is too thick to really envelope and soak in. Skip the marinating completely, just start cooking and use skinless, boneless thigh meat, already cut up. The plantains get lost in there. I'd recommend using eggplant instead in the dish and serve plantains on the side. And if you like spicy, add at least 4 hot peppers. Following the recipe was tasty, but not worth the effort.
As good as it sounds! I saw this and had to make it right away. The plantains take it to another level. It’s not a quick meal, but definitely worth the effort.
Could coconut oil be used instead of the vegetable oil?

This is absolutely delicious! I used bone-in chicken breasts, and left out the chicken bouillon paste (I didn't have any, and was too lazy to go to the grocery store to get a new jar). I added some green beans with the carrots, peppers, and plantains. Very hearty meal served over steamed rice and sautéed Swiss chard.
My plantains cooked in less than half the time to a blackened state so watch carefully, try medium to medium-low heat once the oil is hot, and check after 1-2 min

I loved this!  because of another comment I turned the chicken skin side up during the last cooking step so they were lightly browned.  I happened to be drinking seltzer with coconut essence, and I think a sprinkle of unsweetened coconut might be a nice addition for next time- and there will be a next time!
Made as directed—delish! My two alterations for next time: brown the chicken before adding and buy crushed tomatoes.
This was good but not great. I'm not sure it was worth all the effort compared to a nice chicken tray bake.

Lots of steps but SO worth it. This is probably one of the best things I’ve ever cooked. Made it for dinner on a Monday but will absolutely serve it to guests one of these days. The layers of flavor are incredible. And I don’t even remotely like bell peppers. 

Made a few adjustments as I went along: 
- Seared the chicken before cooking the marinade
- Added a jalapeño and a serrano to the chile and carrot because we like SPICE in this house 
- Added an extra plantain 
- Used a ton of rosemary, oregano and sage for my bouquet garni because that’s what I had in my yard

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 45 minutes, plus at least 12 hours’ resting

comments.commentBody: If you haven’t read the accompanying story, then you must read it right now. Then bake this Cake Komolafe (thank you, Andrea in Richmond). Bake it as a tribute to a courageous warrior of a woman who is a role model for all of us in life and in the kitchen! Yewande, you’re an inspiration! The power of food to heal has now taken on new meaning. 🙏 Vaughn Vreeland, maybe you could feature this recipe (with a link to the backstory) in an upcoming edition of Bake Time?

@JJ
Thanks so much, inspired to bake. I look forward to a link, as well.

@JJ I won’t make this cake, but I’ve been hooked on her next-level butternut squash soup for months!  This article is truly inspiring. And to digress from food, it points to the need for further research into sickle cell and other diseases.

@Amy K.I was an ER nurse in the mid 70’s. The only treatment for sickle cell was pain meds and we treated many. I became a Nurse Anesthetist and luckily did not see this awful outcome. The best thing I have heard is the gene therapy given to a woman with sickle cell that early on seemed to work. I have not heard since and hope it is at least a beginning of a cure.
What an incredible story! In my house this will be called Cake Komolafe.
I believe this could also include a “tag” as gluten-free.

@Brent- yes it’s GF and making it is a tribute to a courageous woman and chef, this story brought tears to my eyes. It will be an honor to bake this cake in my home in tribute. This recipe is a gift to us all.

Yewande, I am so moved by your story, so sorry for the unimaginable pain you've endured, and so grateful that you are finding your way back to creativity with fierce spirit and determination.   Blessings to you and your family.
An incredible story of resiliency and hope. Thank you so much for sharing your story.  This recipe looks amazing.
My favorite item at Radio Bakery!!! So excited to make it, an the accompaniment to a beautiful beautiful article. Thank you for making this

Ended up here from the very moving story, am a huge fan of the radio bakery corn cake, happened to have all the ingredients already so gave a whirl. 
I do think turbinado sugar or potentially something similar helps w the structure of the meringue. It is much fluffier and sturdier than meringues I’ve made with white sugar which I suspect helps it hold up to all the things that get mixed in. 
I used frozen sour cherries and bobs red mill medium coarse corn meal. I rested in the fridge for ~24 hours. 
The cake did not turn out great for me though. I ended up keeping it in the oven 10-15 minutes longer but it was still rather underbaked and the visual cues she describes in the recipe (including my cake tester) were reached. I am an avid baker and very familiar w the ways my oven misbehaves, so I don’t think my oven temp is wildly off. I also found the cornmeal to be far too gritty in the end for my taste. Maybe this is because it wasn’t fully baked? Or perhaps my cornmeal was too coarse- I suspect medium varies quite a lot.
It wasn’t fully baked delicious but texture all over the place (part too soft raw cake, part too crunchy raw corn meal)
@Madie - Since we're already taking out and dirtying the Cuisinart, would it make sense to grind the cornmeal a little finer?

I had the same experience! I followed the instructions exactly (including a 48 hour rest and visual baking cues). When I turned it out of the pan, several areas were dry and stuck to the pan and several other spots were basically raw. It's also both too sweet and too salty - I won't make again.
@Madie Did you rest the batter?

I know no one actually thinks you could prep a cake in 15 minutes as this recipe says- but if you add up all the time guidelines for browning butter, pulsing sugar, and mixing, it’s at least 40 minutes.  I wish there was some vague level of commitment to reality on the time estimates that these recipes give.
@Lilith 100% agree. As much as I love the NYT cooking app, the times are criminally underestimated. I’ve read recipes where it says to caramelize onions in 5-7 minutes. In what universe?

Having read the recipe and all the comments, I am wondering if the reason the cakes have come out rather coarse from the cornmeal is that the bakers are putting their cakes in the oven too soon.  As I read Yolanda‘s recipe and directions she suggests that the baker refrigerate the batter for up to THREE days. It seems to me that three days would help moisturize that cornmeal, especially if you are using, as she suggests a coarse cornmeal. I am 80 and don’t bake much anymore but I do love reading recipes. My granddaughter comes over and is the 16 year-old Baker in our house right now. She loves cooking and baking with her granddad and myself. I am the overseer much like Yewande and her granddad is the helper and sous chef. We have a grand messy time!

thank you for continuing on with your passion. I look forward to making this cake. And i will make a lot of hollandaise sauce with the egg yolks ....eggs Benedict or topping for asparagus or artichoke mummy or something else delicious

@Grand
Egg yolks freeze —just stir with 1/4 tsp sugar (thanks to Rose Levy Beranbaum)

@Natasha Yes… and a pinch of salt per yolk for future use in savory dishes. I freeze them in plastic ice cube trays (one yolk per cube). When completely frozen I pop them out and store in zip lock bags clearly marked for sweet or savory. Makes it very easy to incorporate them into future recipes.
@GrandB will pick up meyer lemons for lemon curd.
Bless you for the outlook and the recipes you share!

I have not made the cake yet which looks delicious! I just wish to commend a courageous woman who is fighting back valiantly from a horrible experience. Few people are aware of the “ normal”  difficulties experienced by those afflicted with sickle cell disease and to battle back as Yewande has to continue on with her career and love of creating recipes is amazing!
God Bless you and may we have many more recipes!

Yewande...thank you for the courage and openess it took to share your amazing story so openly...one of my best friends has sickle cell ...my son was in coma following an accident that paralyzed him...they never want to share their stories....so reading each word of your somehow helped me heal. I eagerly support your competence and creativity as a cook....you are brilliant.
I made this cake last night - absolutely delicious! Served with whipped cream. Yewande's recipes are always so good!

What a gem of a recipe! Really surprised that so few ingredients could produce such a rustic yet complex cake with a symphony of texture and flavor. The interior was pillowy soft and the exterior had the crackly crunch of toffee from the caramelization of the turbinado sugar. I agree with @vivien, this cake stands on its own and really doesn’t need the addition of the cherries. Next time I will bake this without the fruit, but will prepare a bowl of macerated berries to pass around the table. I followed the recipe to the letter, using a 30 hour resting/hydration time. I served with unsweetened whipped cream because the turbinado in this recipe provides plenty of sweetness. Bravo! Cake Komolafe is a new family favorite.

I’m not sure I understand why the egg whites need so much air whipped into them if you are just going to let the batter rest for 12 hours or more…seems like it would defeat the purpose. I get that the cornmeal needs a lot of time to moisten, but whipped egg whites seem much more important for a soufflé, than to a corn cake.

I halved the recipe and baked it in a shallow 10 inch cast iron skillet for 35 minutes. The texture is great as others described. But I find this cake too sweet. I wish I had not coated the pan with more sugar, at least. 
I used relatively fine corn meal and let the batter rest for 14 hours. This was sufficient. 
Some readers wondered why the egg whites are first whipped and then butter added, which might deflate the batter. I found this not to be the case. Trust the recipe. 
I wonder if one can reduce the amount of sugar without compromising the consistency of the batter.

This recipe looks great.

Any tips for using stone-ground bloody butcher variety cornmeal and substituting blueberries for the cherries? It's what I have on hand.
Try it. Of course, it won't be a Cake Komolafe.
I cut the sugar by 1/4 cup. Not enough. I am throwing it out.  Cut the sugar by half, or even more. I’d say don’t try this unless you have 8 eggs you have to use up.
If I want to halve the recipe, it's easy to do until I get to the baking pan.  Would a 6-inch cake pan work?
@Alice i have an 8-inch cast iron skillet that should work.

Would really appreciate a gram measurement for the egg whites, as I keep a bag in the freezer that was added to a couple of times over the holidays and I’ll need to defrost the quantity needed. I’ll estimate from online averages in the meantime, but assumed that would be included since the other items are weighed and with the variation in egg sizes, especially among those who raise chickens or buy at farmers markets, where there can be less consistency (and also common in baking recipes). Thanks for a great bake!
Easily looked up. One large egg white = 30 grams.

I am a fan of the radio bakery corn cake. I can see the resemblance but I don’t think this recipe cracks the code for the home baker.  I read through the comments and made the folllowing adjustments- used cornmeal to coat the sides of the skillet and added a layer of parchment paper in the bottom baked slightly higher at 380 for an additional 15 mins. Cherries did not sink. It came out looking like a clafoutis & dislodged beautifully. However it’s still mushy and underbaked in the middle =unsightly from the flip. The edges are deeply burnished and crusty. My take on the problem is the skillet. I frequently bake a hot water cornbread with a coarse ground meal it is a very wet batter and one of the essentials is pouring the batter into a pre-heated skillet. I think the cast iron is not able to reach a high enough temp at 375 for 30 mins to thoroughly bake this dense batter. If I were to do it again- I’d use a thinner dark metal cake plate with the same prep which would allow better heat circulation. I put mine back in the oven at 200 for 45 mins to dry out. Should work but now it looks unfortunate from the handling and not nice enough to serve. Not sure if the cherries help or distract.

The crunchy exterior and creamy interior was created rave reviews. Worried about being underdone, I set the dough on the counter to warm up an hour after its day in the fridge and stirred it before pouring into the pan. I used a 12” cast iron pan lined with parchment buttered and sugared and it popped out easily looking just like her photo. The only variation was using blueberries instead of cherries as I dislike cherries.
Made this as muffins instead of a cake, and they turned out AMAZING! Tastes just like the one from Radio Bakery
Do I need to add the cherries as I don’t like the texture of cooked fruit?
Of course not

Yewande I repeat prevous comments:  You are an inspiration!

Thank you
I will make this Cake Komolafe as a tribute to an incredibly inspiring woman. Thank you for all you do.

Totally concur with @JJ and @Andres in Richmond, VA. Ms. Komolafe is inded a warrior and beacon of light we can all learn a thing or two from, and delight in her amazing resilience and creativity. The Cake Komafe is on the menu!!!

Excellent cake. Made with TJ' turbinado sugar, Bob's Red Mill medium grind cornmeal. Batter rested overnight. Very sweet; served with creme fraiche, which went well. Used TJ's frozen bing cherries - did not add much and could well be omitted.

Something isn’t quite right with this recipe. We made it today and the bake time was almost double the listed time and then batter completely separated from the cornmeal during baking. Perhaps an indication of the recommended coarseness of the cornmeal would be helpful. 

As previous commenters mention, we assumed there should be 1 teaspoon instead of 1 Tablespoon of salt. Even with a tablespoon the cake was moderately salty.

Yewande, thank you for sharing your story. This article and recipe show your resilience in the face of challenges. I also appreciate your note that was originally made with almond flour. As I’m allergic to corn, I will be making the almond version instead. In my family, it was considered a prize to get the cherry with a pit still in it. Seems like a metaphor for life.

"Bake it as a tribute to a courageous warrior of a woman who is a role model for all of us in life and in the kitchen! Yewande, you’re an inspiration!"
My thoughts exactly! I've been a fan of Yewande's for a number of years. Her story brought tears to my eyes. She is an inspration.  I am going to bake it (doubled) as the dessert for a group of 20 this month in tribute to her.

What an inspiration you are! Bravo, bravo. I admire you beyond words. I’m so excited to make this recipe. Thank you for being the woman that you are! Your strength, your willpower, your passion shines. Thank you.
Yewande Komolafe, you amazing human being!  Thank you for sharing your story and this delicious recipe!  You are and always will be my inspiration in the kitchen and in life!

I wanted this to taste so good, and it did, but I wound up with a cake that was uncooked on the inside and too crunchy, like hard candy on the outside. I used Bob's Red Mill Medium Coarse corn meal but it never softened enough and the grains didn't absorb the liquid. As I said, the taste was good -- the brown butter flavor shone through in the uncooked "mush" but other parts were inedible. I want to try again, maybe with corn flour and less or even no sugar in the pan before baking.
this recipe was written very thoroughly. i’ve never been able to make a meringue before but with the precise directions provided, the meringue and the cake came out perfectly.
Made as written.  Not a simple recipe but it came out beautifully. Texture and sweetness remind me of pecan pie.

While my batter was resting, I read every single comment on this recipe and became anxious my cake would turn out badly. It was absolutely delicious: tender on the inside with a chewy, caramel-like exterior.

Taking into account the comments that said the cake was too sweet, at Step 8, I coated the sides of the pan with cornmeal instead of sugar and omitted the final sprinkle of sugar on top. I also think the grittiness mentioned by a few people is a deliberate feature, not a flaw.

Anyone tried replacing the egg whites with liquid egg whites from a carton? I did, and my egg whites didn’t whip enough to be a merengue….and the bottom! sunk in a couple of places. Also, the butter separated from the butter and fried the top and remained in a shallow pool on top of the cake…was this all because I used carton egg whites??

Preparation takes a bit of work (longer than the recipe suggests) but the outcome is an interestingly textured and tasty cake. I used carton liquid egg whites and cane sugar to skip the food processor step. Bob’s red mill medium ground cornmeal. Rested for 32 hours. Baked for 40 min. Result was a deep golden brown top with a heterogenous texture inside, some chewy (reminiscent of meringue) parts and some cornbread texture parts. The cornmeal was clearly detected when chewing but we liked it. It was sweeter than expected so I agree with the comment to use cornmeal to coat the pan instead of more sugar. There was also absolutely no way that it would have popped out of the pan after baking. Super stuck on so served directly from the cast iron. With ice cream! Would make again.

I wanted to love this, but it’s just…okay. The cake is way too sweet, and despite (accidentally) baking it for slightly longer than I was supposed to, it’s underbaked; almost like a corn pudding rather than a corn cake. I let it rest for three days so the texture of the corn was nice. And the sugar around the crust was a nice addition for taste and crunch. But my goodness it’s borderline disgustingly sweet. I still love Yewande though and this is the first of her recipes that didn’t deliver for me.

Sadly I agreed. I had a small slice and still felt woozy after from the intense sweetness. It was somehow both bland and insanely sweet at the same time. Recipe also had an annoying need for a million bowls, plus a blender, standing mixer, etc. 

Beautiful story and so many of Yewande's recipes are revelatory. I wish I could soften my stance but this one is a skipper. Texture, mess and unappealing over-sweetness were three strikes too many.

Has anyone attempted this in a smaller baking dish (like a muffin tin or the mini pans Radio Bakery uses)? the crispy edges are my favorite part of radio’s brown butter corn cake, so I wonder if this recipe would work in a smaller dish to try to replicate those edges all around

@Smaller baking dish? Great idea! I’m going to try this. Several factors contribute to successfully making those crispy edges, one being the material of the pan. This recipe specifies either cast-iron or carbon steel for the baking vessel. I have a Lodge cast iron “wedge” baking pan. I use it for corn bread and scones. Lodge cast also sells muffin pans and small cake pans (7 small cakes per pan). I would imagine any of these would produce the result you seek. Not sure that a standard aluminum muffin tin will provide the optimal crunch you seek.

I absolutely love the accompanying story. But, I won’t be baking this cake again. 

I baked until golden brown and until my toothpick came out clean,only for my cake to be underbaked still in the middle. The crunchy edges were yummy, but also a little too sweet for my preferences. My biggest nopes were that the recipe uses so many egg whites + has many steps, when you factor in the blending the sugar.

Thank you, Yewande, for this delectable, well balanced recipe that accompanies your story of strength and resilience, showing that creativity and perseverance will out. I'm going to make this cake again with the sour cherries I picked and froze last year, offering  to serve it when my courageous daughter comes to visit next week.
I wish she had explained why the turbinado sugar is important.  Its expensive and I don't stock it in my pantry.  Should I forget about making this recipe or will regular sugar be acceptable?
My first package was a splurge I thought I couldn't afford.  Go for it, you won't regret it.
@Marie If you’re near a Trader Joe’s, you can get turbinado sugar there. Don’t recall it being expensive
It’s also nice for topping muffins or quick breads. Plus it’s good in coffee if you like adding sugar to coffee.
Is the resting for 12 hours needed to soften the cornmeal? I’m perplexed. (and really want to make the full recipe in one go!) Any wisdom or short cut tips?

I believe you're right and it's to give the cornmeal time to absorb water and thus soften and cook more quickly. If you have your heart set on doing this without a resting period, you could use more finely ground cornmeal but a great deal of the charm and flavor of the Radio Bakery cornmeal cake comes from the distinctly textured coarse cornmeal. You might also need to gauge the consistency of the batter before baking and adjust with a small amount of milk.
Would dried tart montmorency cherries, plumped in hot water ( w or w/o sugar & brandy) serve as a substitute for the fresh cherries?
@bren the recipe calls for frozen cherries, not fresh. Dried probably wouldn’t work because the moisture ratio would be off.
Wow! Cutting 2 sticks of butter and 2 cups of sugar with 1.25 cups of grain.
Hi, I love the sound of this recipe but I dislike cherries very much. What would you consider a substitution? Thank you so much for your help.
@Siobhan Isaacs cran or blue

‘Scooped into individual portions’ is in the intro, not in the instruction, and who is the Chef doing the scooping?  Does one need to read the article, forever attached to the recipe, to decipher the instructions, which seem to perplex with so many minutes of inflation and deflation.  I will make this, but this writing is confusing.
@MigueXo that is a reference to the inspo cake not this recipe.
Friends, what are your suggestions for using up 8 egg yolks?

@MaryLynn  Easiest option is to freeze them for future use. You’ll see my instructions for how to do this if you scroll down to my reply to @Natasha. Since I freeze the yolks individually, I can defrost the exact number I need for any given recipe. Here’s my plan: make home-made mayo (Kenji Lopez Alt recipe on the Serious Eats website which uses an immersion blender and takes 5 minutes); Bearnaise sauce (Sam Sifton recipe); Strawberry Jam Bars with Cardamom (another super Chef Komolafe recipe); Lemon Curd (several great recipes on NYT Cooking); I will use the lemon curd in Melissa Clark’s Lemon Poppyseed bars; Soft & Chewy Sugar Cookies (Sam Seneviratne recipe). Any recipe that leaves you with 8 extra egg yolks is like a gift that keeps on giving!
Carbonara!
Baked custard!

I was thrilled when I realized I already had turbinado sugar (I get mine at Trader Joe’s) and cornmeal so I made this today. It’s hanging out on the cooling rack and my partner and I can’t stop taking bites as we walk through the kitchen. I refrigerated it for 3.5 hours (I know, but we really needed a pick me up today) and omitted the cherries. I used a 10in cast iron skillet and ended up baking it for 45 minutes. The edges are incredibly chewy and crispy and I love the texture the sugar adds. The center is soft and gooey in the best way. I doubt this will make it through the weekend! Thank you for sharing your story and this recipe, I look forward to trying the Radio Bakery corn cake the next time we’re in NYC.
These came out really greasy and undercooked, even after an extra 15 minutes in the oven. Not sure what I should have done differently

Wonderful story, and I felt inspired to try making this, but after following the instructions exactly I ended up with an underbaked, too sweet, overly gritty cake which I threw out. I wanted to like it but it’s a ton of effort for a weird result which some may like but it’s definitely not what I expected. I usually love cornmeal and my oven isn’t cool, so I don’t think it’s me… maybe this just isn’t for me! But fair warning.

In case anyone is having trouble figuring out what to do with the egg yolks, David Leibowitz chocolate ice cream uses five yolks, and I usually add a sixth for more richness. Then you're down to two spare yolks.
Baked custard, and lemon curd come to mind....

I just read your story, Yewande. I have long followed you and now do so with a new connection and admiration. I will continue to make your recipes in honor of my deep respect for you! And because I love them. :)
Any thoughts on making this in a Bundt pan?
Does this really need 1 tablespoon of salt??
Definitely not. I made this with 1 tbsp and it has a very salty flavor, to the point that I’m probably not going to eat any of the cake. The cornmeal was very gritty. Pretty disappointing overall

@Sharon Yes, if you are following the recipe and using Diamond Crystal Kosher Salt then 1 tbsp is correct. If you are using regular table salt, you would use half that amount. The 2 types of salt have vastly different volumes because of the variation in crystal size. Diamond Crystal Kosher Salt is preferred by most cooks and bakers because it has much larger, flakier crystals, making it easier to control the amount of salt in a recipe. If you prefer to use regular table salt, then you would use half the amount specified in the recipe, in this case 1/2 tbsp.
Can anyone tell me if I can sub Demerara for the turbinado?? TIA😊

@Cate I wondered the same thing myself. Demerara is a stickier (wetter) crystal with a more molasses forward flavor profile. Ultimately, I decided to bake the recipe as written, with turbinado, because I was concerned that the additional stickiness in the Demerara might impact on the quality of the bake, AND I trust Ms Komolafe’s very carefully developed recipes. Nonetheless, I think this could be delicious with Demerara! If you decide to move forward with this baking experiment, PLEASE let us know how it comes out.
@JJ will do, and thanks!
What can be done to reduce the sugar ingredients ( 7 teaspoons per serving) while retaining the qualities of the cake?  Any advice ?
Easy, but I didn't like the flavor. Probably just my taste. Won't make again.
Didn't care for it. After all the steps and processes and waiting time, it tasted like, uh, sugar.
What is it about turbinado sugar that so many NYT recipes consider it de rigueur? I can't find it in our area and I need a reason to order it online.
@Jefftry coconut sugar.

@Jeff I encourage you to take the plunge and discover for yourself why turbinado sugar is so frequently recommended by bakers. It’s minimally processed raw cane sugar with the distinctive carmel/maple taste that’s characteristic of raw sugar cane. Beyond baking, it’s also fabulous in a cup of tea or your morning coffee (if a teaspoon of sugar is your thing). Try it! I bet you’ll use it up faster than you imagined.

I followed the recipe exactly and it came out… weird. It’s really, really weird. Nothing like the picture (sliceable). Way too much butter (so oily!), gritty (cornmeal sticks in your teeth), the cherries fully disappeared, and then it fell apart.
