I used Bob’s Red Mill medium coarse corn meal and refrigerated it for 36 hours. Anyway, now I need to figure out what to do with these egg yolks so they’re not wasted too.
I used coconut sugar instead of turbinado. Quickly became fine and added rich flavor not unlike turbinado. Delicious result.

This was a disaster, and I'm sad about it. I'm an experienced baker. I let it sit for 12 hours. I did use aquafaba instead of eggs, so maybe that caused it? When I took it out of the oven well past the recommended time, it was a wet mess with butter pooled on top. (Earlier -- wetter, still with butter pooled on top.)  I let it sit for awhile, because it clearly wasn't ready to be flipped. When I returned to it, the edges had completely hardened and now this THING is affixed to my skillet.

Love the flavors-the browned butter lends a delicious caramel flavor.  The cornmeal creates a gritty texture that I like but others may not.  I rested it for 3 days but still gritty.  My chief complaint with this recipe is that it is entirely too sweet.  I woud like to repeat this recipe and reduce the sugar by a fourth (1.5 cups) and a half (1 cup).  It also was not fully baked even though toothpick trick indicated it was.  It stuck to the pan.

Delicious! Does not need the last bit of sugar added on top. I used sour cherries instead of sweet because that’s what I had, and I liked them a lot as a counterpoint to the very sweet cake. I’ll probably add 50% more next time. 

In a 10” pan took longer to bake than expected but was fine.
With all the extra sugar and butter, mine came out like a Carmel bread pudding. Fun baking project but probably won’t make it again

Feel like this is unnecessarily complicated. Just make a financier with cornmeal + some almond flour, and it tastes pretty much identical to Radio's without the whipping, overnight rest, sugar crust, etc.

This is a really good recipe.  Some might find it a little fussy but I find it worth the effort as the result is incredibly close to the Radio Bakery cake I've had a few times and loved.  I baked these in a Le Creuset mini-loaf style muffin pan (holds 8), and baked them for 20 minutes.  They had great color and were still tender inside. Worth the planning ahead and ingredient prep!  Plus, the leftover yolks are a great excuse to make a curd.

Had the corn cake at Radio Bakery and came here to replicate it. This is great, it’s special, for simple ingredients, the flavors are amazing, and will make again. The cherries I had reservations about but they add something special. If you are using a 10” cast iron, be prepared to cook it for longer. My oven temp might be off, but I think a thicker cake needs more time.
Has anyone tried using blue cornmeal instead?

Speaking as a Capetonian, this recipe is missing the yellow rice with raisins and cinnamon, and should always be served with sliced ripe banana, dried coconut and chutney (preferably Mrs Balls Chutney). Recipe in “South African Cooking in the USA”
Please, no chicken! This dish should be beef or lamb. I grew up with it and it will not be bobotie if you use chicken. Preferred dried fruit is sultanas. Mrs. Balls Chutney is good with this.
what does, “may taste elusive” mean?
What is a 'mist of onion"?
Both wonderful questions

A thick egg topping resembling a frittata isn’t traditional- way too many eggs. Half that amount would suffice. 
Mrs Balls chutney is imperative- both inside the Bobotie mixture and for serving as a sambal accompaniment. Coconut & banana & chopped tomato/red onion mix is our family favorite. 
I don’t use Garam Marsala or chili - I 
use a mild curry powder ( Smart & Final have a good mild curry powder perfect for Cape Malay dishes - not spicy but imparting delicious flavor)

Bobotie is supposed to be a dish that uses whatever leftover meat you have. I really dislike ground beef, so we usually use ground pork, but turkey would also be fine. I’ve even made it with bison - it’s always delicious, and my American husband’s favorite thing to eat.

The egg custard here is way too thick. Part of the issue is the use of heavy cream. Way too fancy for this humble dish, and completely unnecessary. The egg custard should be thin and light, with pieces of browned meat poking through.
@MarthaliI I had it in South Africa at a friend’s home. Delicious.

@Marthali I have a recipe in the Time-Life Africa cookbook, and it only uses 2 eggs and a cup of milk (left over from soaking bread) for the custard. I made it and it looked exactly as you described. A thin custard with bits of meat poking through.

Not authentic bobotie, but i thought id give it a try and it was tasty.  Apricots were not a plus.  Meat some what dry and for the first time ever in my long life of making bobotie most of the custard sank into the meat.  Do yourselves a favor and get a real south African recipe with raisins, apricot jam, curry power and a dash of vinegar.  Sometimes I add slivered almonds for crunch.
I have used raisins instead of cranberries or apricots (depends on my pantry) and sliced almonds in this recipe, as well as cinnamon. It is delicious.
My recipe from the 70s in Africa, made it into a meat lof. Yummy!
I tried it with dried apricots and beef. Delicious. Will make again.

Had this in South Africa last summer and loved it. Came home and made it using lamb. At the game park where we had it, they used kudu. Delicious either way. Used a really deep chutney and it comes out yummy.
Could you suggest an alternative to the cream to make this lactose free?

@Lisa Hi. I have a Time-Life recipe published 1970 and for a similar sized bobotie it only uses 2 eggs and a cup of milk for the custard. So drop the cream, use half the eggs and a cup of almond or oat milk and you will be fine. It’s _supposed_ to be a thin custard.

Thank you. In the South African movie Blitz Patrollie there was a plate of food I could not identify; one of the characters complains that another character ate all the meat out of his dish. What he was eating sort of looked like --but wasn't-- mac& cheese. I think this might have been it.

Made this, I held back on the fruit (I used yellow raisins) but should have gone for the full cup. The sweet flavor breaks up the intense savoriness.
Also, it was much more of a meat pie than I was expecting; I was hoping for South African quiche but it was more like shepherd's pie.  
Next time I will dial back the meat, substitute barley or rice some of the meat, or use stew beef instead of chop meat.
Sorry! I initially did not see all of the comments and they were very helpful.
You leave the bay leaves in after sautéing ? In other recipes they are usually removed before serving. Not sure it would be pleasant to bite into a whole bay leaf. ????

Considered an iconic South African recipe, Bobotie almost always includes a slice or two of crustless white bread soaked in milk as a binder to keep the minced meat mixture moist and tender. Squeeze the milk out of the bread and use the leftover milk (topped up) for the egg topping (no cream) and 3 eggs maximum. Spicing the meat varies by recipe but should always include curry powder and a little lemon juice, chutney, apricot jam, dried fruit and perhaps slivered almonds. Best to look for an authentic South African recipe to experience its amazing flavors! Done right, it’s delicious and enjoyed by non-south Africans too.

I agree with readers who say there are too many eggs in the recipe. I use 2. Also, there’s never cream in bobotie. One trick that I would add is from my ancient cookbook. Lemon leaves, rolled into tubes and pressed down into the mixture before baking, impart a wonderful extra flavour.
Way too eggy... it actually kinda resembles moussaka, with a lovely topping - not eggy throughout.

No, no, no…. What is this blasphemy?
Beef or lamb, NO heavy cream, and how many eggs???
Do yourself a favour and look up an authentic bobotie recipe. It’s served on rice and coupled with sliced bananas, unsweetened coconut and Mrs Balls Chutney, if possible, or another mango chutney, if not. And the dried fruit is sultanas, nothing more fancy than that is needed!

Have had Bobotie all my life starting in Zimbabwe, now cooked by me in California.  Laurens  van der Post's African Cooking (NY: Time Life Books) has an excellent recipe p 40 (book p 139) with lean ground lamb, fresh lemon juice, chopped tart apple (like Granny Smith) seedless raisins, and blanched almonds; don't forget tucking in fresh orange leaves (or bay leaves).  Book version adds half hard boiled eggs within meat.  Newer recipe with ground beef is in The African Times Cookbook (Penguin).
I've read somewhere that cream is very low lactose.  But possibly you could lactose free half-and-half.

The dish was cooked by Malay chefs for the Dutch farmers and became part of both the Cape Malay and Boerekos cooking tradition but the origins of this dish can be traced back to the spice cooking tradition of Medieval Europe, not Indonesia or Malaysia. It was often made on Mondays from the leftover Sunday leg of mutton roast.
Fun fact - the dried shrimp is listed as optional. That means you can leave it out if you don't like it!
Based on personal taste, could I substitute the dried shrimp and crayfish with anchovies?

I had Red Red frequently when I was doing some work in Ghana and loved it, so was excited about trying the recipe and I have to say, it definitely took me back. However . . . I made some substitutions based on what I had in my pantry -- Japanese dried crabs and Korean shrimp powder instead of the dried and ground shrimp called for in the recipe -- and maybe because of that, while the taste was authentic, it smelled like something had died in my kitchen.
Delicious. Made a vegetarian version with a little mushroom based “fish sauce” instead of crayfish. Served with brown rice.
Made it vegetarian using dulse, lightly toasted then crumbled.  May or may not taste authentic but did taste good.
can you substitute dried with frozen crayfish?

Delicious! I skipped the optional dried fish (vegetarians). Beans were black-eyed peas from Rancho Gordo. I may have done something wrong - I didn't find this any sort of big "commitment" as warned in the header. I made and cooked down the base as per Part 1. For Part 2, I sautéed an onion, then added it plus the beans to the cooked base still in its pot. I really didn't need to - and couldn't have - cooked it down much beyond heating the beans through. So so good! Had it w fresh WW sourdough.
Is there a good vegan/vegetarian substitute for the shrimp? If you don’t add a protein, other than the beans, do you just skip steps 5 and 6?

Good question!  I made this for a vegan guest and found that, without the dried fish ingredients, it was very bland.  In my comment above, you’ll see what I tried instead:  Tofu skins for texture, soy sauce (skins soak up so well) for umami and salt; chopped celery and garlic for texture and more flavor.  If you couldn’t find tofu skins or didn’t want the trouble of sauteeing them, a vegan chicken/ fish product would probably work, too, for protein, flavor, and salt.
Could you use a splash of fish sauce instead of the dried shrimp?
Anyone know how to grind the whole shrimp? (Dry blender?) Or is this a separate purchase?
@TSC I think it is a separate purchase.

Made this for a vegan guest, so obviously- no dried fish.  But as I looked at it, there’s so much of that, it’s not going to be truly optional.  So it wasn’t.  Vegan part of the stew showed potential, but it was missing:  bulk, umami, and other flavors.  So I rigged up a sautee of:  chopped celery, 3 cloves garlic (last), and soaked, chopped tofu skins.  Taking a deep breath, I added soy sauce the skins soaked up.  Fusion- and it was good.

title: Roasted Kelewele (Spiced Plantains) With Crispy Shallots and Herbs Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: oh my.  Just the sound of the recipe sounds delicious.  I cannot wait to try it.  My mom used to make plantain fritters and chips (out of very thinly grated plantains with the skin on them) when we were kids growing up in South India.  Was the most wonderful thing to eat during a rainy day.
@CN what a lovely comment to share!  Hope this recipe gives you & yours as much enjoyment.

@CN I love the idea of grating plantain for fritters! Im really curious to hear more about the inclusion of plantain *skin* in them. I know the skin is thin, but does it not taste similar to banana skin, with its dry alum-y bitterness?

I am wondering about the quantity of orange juice.  With no prior context for making this type of recipe, I followed things pretty closely, but instinctually the 1-1/4 OJ+zest (which was just 2 oranges, not 4) seemed too much liquid.  Ultimately that seemed correct and I ended up decanting most of it at the end so that the final broil could really caramelize and crisp the plantains.

Overall this was still delicious and will make again, but will reduce the juice at least 50%, and maybe bake uncovered a bit longer?

Wondering of others’ experience/thought on this matter, or if it really IS supposed to have so much juice.
@Victor 4 medium/small cava navels worked ok for me - it wasn’t runny and got lots of good caramelization under the broiler
I would think the plaintains will be too mushy- I'm going to try it but cook for less time. Also, can I substitute something for the peanut butter- peanut and almond allergy in the family?
I’ve never made this, but I feel like sunflower seed butter or tahini would make good substitutes for peanut butter. Best luck!

I made this almost exactly as written, and would happily make again with several changes:

There's far too much OJ and zest; I would reduce by a quarter to even 50 percent. The orange flavor overpowered the very tasty peanut-miso sauce. 

I used maybe 3 tablespoons of the 6 called for, which is used to drizzle over the plantains at intervals. It seemed fine that way.

This is insanely good! The process is like a spa ritual: plantains steam in a citrus bath, with a miso-soy face face mask on. They come out perfectly relaxed and tender to the bite. 

To address the comment about the quantity of citrus liquid: before broiling, I let them rest for a few minutes to air-dry and further absorb the sauce, and this seemed to do the trick.

My family and I really liked this dish! I halved the amount of oranges as another person commented as we aren't huge fans of powerful citrus flavors in main dishes, and it was delicious! The only downside of this is the number of dishes and the time it took to make.

I have used dozens of recipes from NYT cooking, always eager to try something new and exciting and different. I was so psyched to try this, unfortunately, not a fan at all. Followed recipe to a t and the flavor profile is not for me. Something about the orange juice and the nut butter glaze, is just off. Oh well, at least I tried it!
I will continue to experiment with all of the other hundreds of great recipes on here though!!!

This is delicious.  I made a half size recipe and cut back a bit more on the orange juice (per the other comments I read) to about 1/2 cup vs 5/8 cup.   I prepared everything ahead of time mid day, and let it sit before the broiler steps, which others recommended might help the plantains soak up any excess juiciness.  It worked! Paired it with black beans as suggested and a large fresh salad from our home lettuce tower and our microgreens growing tray.  Hubby and I agree it’s a winner!  Thanks so much.
This is delicious! I only used 3 plantains and missed all the OJ tips so it was a bit more wet than crispy, but still excellent. Served with avocado; will probably add a salad next time. A winner!
I made this with a few modifications. I got a jar of pasta sauce then boiled some spaghetti. I cover the pasta with the sauce and served it with some crusty bread. Picky family loved it.
Cook pasta till al dente than another 10 to 12 minutes? Won't it be mushy?
Where's the lemongrass (one of the supposed "primary ingredients") in the recipe?
Its interesting to me that most of the comments are negative about this recipe. We cooked it this evening and it was phenomenal. Thanks for the recipe.
Where is the lemongrass in the recipe?
There is nothing Caribbean about this recipe except the name and use of coconut milk and hot peppers

I know both salt mackerel and shrimp rundown. Love them both. I've had them with rice, had them with pasta. But this recipe seems strange. It leaves out usual ingredients, adds odd substitutions, and apparently completely overcooks the starch. Rundown on pasta is delicious. I won't try this, however, because of the cooking times, even though it might be delicious. I'll stick with preparing the rundown more traditionally and serving it over pasta. Still, it's great to see rundown profiled!

This seems very odd.  Seafood and lemongrass are mentioned among the primary elements, but there is no lemongrass.  I guess the anchovies are the seafood.  And you cook the pasta on medium-high for 10-12 minutes past al dente!  That's an awfully long time.

I'm guessing the lime was substituted for lemongrass, for those who can't find it in their local market. And the cooking time in steps 5 and 6 are just a breakdown of the 10-12 minutes from step 4, which makes for a more reasonable pasta cooking time.
Nothing in the writing suggests steps 5 and 6 are a breakdown of step 4. Not as I read it, at least. Quite the opposite.

Dry pasta softens much more slowly in a sauce than it does in water. I haven't made this recipe and can't vouch for it, but twice the normal time is pretty realistic depending on how thick the sauce is.
Where is everyone seeing lemongrass in the recipe? I don’t see it.
They must have edited the recipe and description to remove any reference to lemongrass, unless I'm blind. I don't see it anywhere but in the comments.

This sounded weird in theory, but it was phenomenal. The flavors all complemented each other, nothing was overpowering. I went light on the habanero for my kids and we all loved it. The sauce is creamy, the flavors are bright, and since it’s all rough chopping, it’s a super easy weeknight meal. I’m not ashamed to say I threw some chicken nuggets in the toaster oven and we had them on the side for some protein on a busy school night. Bonus: it’s made the kids’ lists of approved leftovers to send for school lunches.

I was apprehensive to attempt this recipe, however knowing that it was authored by Yewande Komolate, it seemed a reasonably low risk. I was right! It is delicious. It definitely contains an odd assortment of ingredients, not usual for a Western palate. In spite of this, or more likely because of it, the dish was very tasty. I did cook the pasta separately and added al dente to the sauce, then cooked it all for another 5 minutes. This will definitely make it into our regular rotation. Yummy!!!

Not really Caribbean unless it’s a mixup of various islands plus SE Asia.  To the best of my knowledge, we don’t use lemongrass, at least not in Jamaica, not even sure if most of us from the Caribbean know what anchovies are.  
My mom’s recipe is tomatoes, coconut milk, onions, hot pepper, garlic, thyme if wanted.  Cook until sauce separates, it will look curdled.  Add seafood at the end. We can also add tropical root vegetables instead of the pasta.

I found this surprisingly tasty.  I did cook the pasta separately (8 oz for 2 people) as I was worried about overcooking it . I also added a head of broccoli cut into florets towards the end and took both off the heat when al dente. I used 1/3 of the anchovies and cooked the tomatoes a bit before adding half the coconut milk and reducing until thick and adding the pasta.  I did not add cheese as that seemed strange. The broccoli made it and the addition of shrimp would be great as well!

Apart from the lemongrass and no-seafood-but-anchovies issues, this seems like a fascinating recipe. Sort of terrified by the prospect of adding parmesan to a seafood dish though. I'll probably make this with salmon tonight and throw some lemongrass stalks in the sauce when the pasta gets added.

I put in 1/2 of the anchovies and it was still to much for us.  The next day I tried the leftovers and the anchovy flavor was overbearing.  My wife likes anchovies and she couldn't even do it.    Also, I did think that it was to starchy after cooking the pasta in the sauce.  Overall, I'd probably not make this one again.  It was an easy cook though, so maybe some personal tweaks would make fun dish.

Those who are complaining about the pasta cooking time without having made this (or similar recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce): Use the recommended pasta shapes, which are sturdier, and follow the cooking times/temperatures, and the pasta and sauce come out with a great consistency. I cannot speak to the authenticity of the recipe, but the cooking technique works fine. The only thing I will say for recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce, such as this one, is that the pasta tends to start sticking to the bottom of the pot towards the end, so stir and keep an eye on that.

I love Yewande.  She understands coconut milk in a spiritual way.  Reading her recipes makes me want to hang out with her. This dish satisfies a desire for tomato sauce that cheers up this insane cold weather in a fresh but also warming way.
Naysayers- you just like complaining.
I never have everything exactly on hand and she still leads me to  favorites that stay on rotation. 
This is now on the playlist.
 Jarred minced ginger and some fish sauce (to my taste-no anchovies) were my only substitutions. So yummy and easy. 

Even the picky eaters were happy.  For protein we paired with a jar of jalapeño oil tonnino and served a yogurty citrus salad on the side.

I was looking forward to making this dish based on the recipe description however we found it to be quite underwhelming and meh. Followed the recipe as written (used a habanero vs a scotch bonnet pepper) and didn’t think it had much flavor at all. The coconut milk overwhelmed the elements that should have provided more depth of flavor. We think the recipe has possibilities with revisions but felt it was a lot work for little return on the effort.

This was bad and not in a good way.

The anchovies rendered it really bitter. And the flavors never came together. I don't know what I would do to improve it except not make it.

Family members stopped eating after a couple of bites and we scrambled to put together another makeshift dinner.

I share the confusion raised in many of the comments about the presence/absence of lemongrass, but I'm more puzzled by the thought of making the sauce with the 4 cups of water plus the can of coconut milk. That sounds like a lot of runny sauce to me, not at all like the illustration.

I made this following the recipe except a couple less anchovies and shortening the pasta cooking time. My husband and I both did not like the flavor; all the notes just did not come together well. Quite labor intensive, but sadly, down the disposal it went.

Made it as instructed, was bland so added lemongrass paste and red pepper flakes and still bland. Cooked the pasta 10 mins ("cooks in 14") then just a few minutes in the sauce and it was falling apart. The people who said the anchovies were overwhelming...we had the opposite issuse. 8 anchovies and no flavor.
I added lemongrass and made it with pasta (cooked seperately) it was ok. I added shrimp to the leftover sauce and served it with rice the next day...much better!

I cooked the pasta separately (by accident!) and added some of the pasta water to the coconut milk  mixture. My husband hates anchovies so skipped those. Did add some arctic char (what we had in the freezer) for a delightful fish stew. Lime was good enough, but I did wonder about the lemongrass...
What can be substituted for the anchovies (vegetarian here)?
A better recipe!
Capers plus/and/or a dash of vegetarian Worcestershire sauce (available at Whole Foods and other stores).
Sauce is yummy but cooking pasta in it?  I’m at 15 minutes and my pasta is still hard.  Not al dente.  So I’m adding more water to keep it going … we’ll see!

I bought some lovely lobster ravioli for the holidays. I don't love cream-only pasta sauces... much prefer anything with tomato in it. Hoping this recipe will be the gift that keeps on giving. Anyone tried it with such yet?
Surprisingly flavorless for all of the very flavorful ingredients. Was pretty disappointed.

I remember many years ago being served a dish on the Atlantic coast of Nicaragua that was called rondon. It took me a while to realize the name came from "rundown". It was a stew with root vegetables and fish, and a coconut milk sauce that would "run down". Very different from what is being proposed here. But this dish does sound good. My impulse would be to cook the pasta separately.
Crazy pasta technique - better the next day.

Made this, it wasn’t quite what I was hoping for as I was tasting so I modified…ok, added cream and so so so much parm. Next time would make with stock instead of water. My picky 8 year old loved it though. Go figure.

my first comment on Cooking -- this recipe was DELICIOUS.  I'm vegetarian, subbed out anchovies for capers.  And controlled the heat using jalapenos, and then in the when checking seasoning, I stirred some sambal olek.  It was a perfect heat. But i do want to try it with scotch bonnet next time for funsies.   Next time, I'll also try to add protein into it -- maybe garbanzos, or blend some soft tofu into the sauce.
Delicious! Made as written with bow tie pasta and turned out perfect.
are we all eating the same thing? This was really really good. I didn’t have anchovies so I stirred in some miso paste at the end. Probably a total bastardization of the dish, but it’s very tasty!

@CC how much miso did you use? I’d like to try this recipe with miso in stead of anchovies. I made it twice with anchovies (the amount listed) and loved it! But i would like to have a vegetarian option too.
So much for Italian/Thai fusion. My husband said, “This has to be one of the three worst things you’ve ever made.” I agree.

Followed the recipe very closely. The results were edible but I didn’t think the recipe has a focus. What is the central flavor being celebrated? I have not spent much time on the Caribbean islands but I wonder if dried rigatoni, Pecorino Romano cheese, tomato paste and anchovies would be widely available for use by local cooks.

I made this as directed and it really hit the spot for the porridge I was craving. It’s like a cross between the cream of wheat from my childhood and a barely-sweetened cornmeal muffin. I’m adding this to the rotation of savory weekend breakfasts.

Adding the cornmeal this way, it's tricky to avoid lumps. But if you bring most of the water to a boil, combine the cornmeal with the rest of the water cold, and stir that in quickly, it won't lump (stir till it comes back to boil and starts to thicken). Hold back 1-1/2 c cold water and mix with the 1-1/2 c cornmeal just as the other water boils (i.e., not too far ahead of time).

This was our breakfast every morning in the south of Johannesburg before running to the municipal bus to go to school. Other additions are: Ground biltong mixed in; white pepper and more butter is fabulous; a mashed tin of sardines into the "pap" is still a sibling's  favorite!

This is called mealie pap in Douty Africa and is the staple food (without the luxury items you include). It can be made like this as porridge or with less water to be something like the consistency of mashed potato and eaten with meat and gravy (saus).
Could this recipe be altered to make it in an Instant Pot?
Not really as the maize needs to soften and swell but a quicker method can be attempted in the microwave with repeated stirring until cooked and thickened.

@Deborah W maybe not, but I’ve made something similar overnight in the crockpot. You can find a lot of overnight crockpot cheese grits recipes that actually use polenta, so if you don’t add cheese I think it would basically be this.

In the American Heartland, we call this dish grits.  Can be made sweet or savory.  A good source of the cornmeal is McEwenAndSons.com, where they grow their corn organically and everything they sell is also gluten-free.

@Michele I agree! I order all of my grits and cornmeal from McEwan and Sons. I used to cram bags of their products in my luggage when flying home from BHM and then realized I could just have everything delivered instead.
Can this be made in an Instant Pot?
Can the cornmeal be baked as in the NYT No-Stir Polenta recipe?  Or does the coarseness of the grind make a difference when cooked on the stovetop vs. baked?

This was absolutely delicious with all the recommended additions for serving, however the cleanup of the pot is very difficult as the porridge sticks badly.  I'm still scraping without much success.  Sadly, due to this technicality, I won't be inclined to make this again.
If you let it sit with water for a few hours, it'll lift off easily. I make this (without the sugar) as polenta all the time, and cleanup is easy if you don't try to clean it up immediately.
Can this be refrigerated and reheated?
How well would this save as a make-ahead breakfast?
How about in a rice cooker?
Sounds like. real close cousin of grits to me
Could use oatmeal instead of cornmeal?
Yes you can.
Is there a reason NOT to use a fine grater or a zester for the orange rind?

What is the juice used for?
I do not see it mentioned again
@Patti I hope someone answers your question.
@Patti Travaglio Yes, please clarify what otherwise sounds like an interesting recipe.
@Patti Travaglio. Exactly

I’ve eaten something similar to this in Morocco. You can buy the nuts in the store. But what I liked was each nut had a specific flavor. So I would make as listed, but have some nuts fennel, some nuts, cumin, some nuts the other flavors. Then have them all together in a bowl but this way as you eat one at a time you get different very specific flavors
Why not just save your fingers and zest the orange instead of cutting and mincing?

I assume the egg whites make sure the very interesting combination of flavourings here stick to the nuts rather than kiss them briefly and pool at the bottom of the bowl.  Thank you for sharing this tip.  I've never seen it before.  Great for parties where guest list and dietary restrictions are nailed down.  Curious to know if any intrepid cook out there has tried this with aquafaba before I waste a bowl of nuts being people pleaser?
@CNR I have used whipped aquafaba for a different spiced nut recipe in the past and it worked fine.
What vegan alternative would work for this recipe?
Try Oat milk
is there an egg free way to make this? would aquafaba or psyllium husk powder or tapioca starch work?

@curious
Someone in the comments above wrote they had success with using aquafaba instead. I might try it with a small portion of nuts first.

I added sugar to the egg whites—probably about 2 T. I also used about a teaspoon of salt, total, maybe a bit more. Without the sugar & plenty of salt, I think the mix would taste pretty bland. I can barely taste the sugar, but the overall flavor punch is nice and bright. Next time I'll add more pepper too. It's a good nut mix for a party.

I tried this before realizing that there was no instruction for the orange juice. So I made it without, and it lacked flavor. I tossed the finished nut mix in the orange juice and returned it to the oven, same settings as before, followed by a 20 minutes convect at 200F (dehydration). Better, but still not great. If folks want to give this a try, then I suggest mixing the orange juice with the spices and orange zest and follow the rest as written, possibly finishing w a dehydration step.
There is no orange juice in this recipe--just zest from the peel.

The tip is unclear.

Are you briefly whisking the egg whites before adding the cream of tartar?  If so, why not just add this to the steps?

And if not...are you quickly whisking egg whites and cream of tartar together by hand before putting the mixture in the mixer for the long mix?  Again, if this is the case, why not just include it in the steps?

It looks like the egg whites will be whipped to almost stiff, so add the cream of tartar once you have them whipped up some, this is similar to how I make a meringue cookie & the cream of tartar simply stabilizes the stiff egg whites. I think if you add it to the bowl before whipping some, it clumps.
I'm confused. Why not simply zest (aka grate) the zest from the oranges? Or does she want you chop the peels, meaning the zest and with pith?
@Susan Stuck just use the orange part of the orange peel and avoid the white pith as it’s bitter.
Why squeeze the orange juice if it isn’t used? What is the orange juice used for?
Hi, Catherine, the peeled oranges (and their juice) aren't used in this recipe. Save them instead for whatever you'd like! Thank you for flagging. We've adjusted the recipe accordingly.
Won't the zest burn after that length of cooking time?  May consider adding it at the halfway point, or at the end of baking.
Will aquafaba work in place of egg whites?

I think mincing the orange peel instead of grating it gives you a bigger orange pop when you bite into a piece. I enjoyed drinking the fresh-squeezed OJ with the nuts before they cooled. Great combination!
This recipe had promise when I first read it.  Was excited to try but alas is devoid of any flavor.  The egg whites end up with a chalky after taste and I barely register any orange flavor.
The leftover yolks and whole oranges should work well with the cast iron orange cake on this site.
Delicious, unusual, and beautiful.
How far ahead of time can you make these?
I used a grater, zested, and veg peeler, and peeler (or knife) is the best bet.
No mortar and pestle. Anyone try this with ground spices?
I used a zester/grater instead of peeling and the batch came out great:)
I found this nut mixture quite bland. Should I have added more salt? Maybe. There are other flavored nuts I like better. I won't go to this one again.

I doubled the spices and sage and still found the result lacking flavor with an unpleasant dry mouthfeel from the egg whites. I then added smoked paprika and more salt, which helped a lot. Then I finished by tossing with just enough EVO to give the nuts a light gloss. Delicious but a bit messy in the hands. Great on salads or roasted veggies.
@Zoya Kachadurian I made this exactly as written and agree with you. They were delicious as is, but variety in flavor by nut would be extra cool.

Chopping the orange peel was a pain in the tuchus. Next time I’ll use a microplane to zest the peel and it will blend better as well instead of falling off. Also found it necessary to amp up the temp at the end to get the nuts properly roasted…did an additional 5 minutes at 350 convection, but I know all ovens are not created equal.

When I first tried these they were not crunchy and were almost unpleasant - the orange tasted odd & too strong. I'd zested the orange and I wonder if zesting releases too many oils & that's why the recipe calls for mincing? It would be great for the recipe to explain that. I put the nuts back in for about another 20 minutes, raising the heat to 325, and then they were fine, but I don't think they're really worth the trouble. Not as good as I'd hoped.
@EKL I had the same experience and doubled the baking time my first attempt. The second time, I increased the spices and let the nuts cool before testing the crunchiness. Much better!

This was very good.   I wish I had added more salt after it had baked.  I do think the instructions were a little more fiddley than necessary; I put all the seasonings in the small bowl of my food processor and chopped them until very fine.  I had the benefit of a new vegetable peeler and it stripped the orange peel perfectly.
Bust. Like others, found it flavorless.

Love the recipe.  I'm an old holiday mix fan (cereal, nuts, and worchester). I plan to use some healthier ingredients: garbanzos; edamame; etc., and I have a heavy hand with spices.  Looking forward to this.  Any suggestions to the aforementioned bean substitutions?
I have a small bowl of orange juice leftover.  It doesn’t appear to be needed here, am I missing something?
One more question/ comment, the cook time says one hour, but the instructions don’t really get to one hour, so? I did keep it in the oven for nearly an hour.
Unfortunately very bland and not a trivial amount of work. It needs bolder and more contrasting flavors.

I was very disappointed by this recipe. It smells great but tastes like just nuts and seeds. It was significantly more time consuming than the recipe states for prep time. Lastly, the recipe instructs the cook to juice the oranges but does not mention when to add the juice. If it were all added, the mix would be very slushy. I added only some of the juice and had to cook st least 10 minutes more to dry the mix.

@Judy
Many people refer to the juice.  At no time does the recipe direct you to juice the oranges.  After removing the zest it specifically says to use the peeled oranges for something else.  Eat them is my recommendation.

Add 2T of sugar to the egg whites while whipping. Also add a total of 1 teaspoon of salt, the juice of one orange and a drizzle of olive oil over the nuts before baking. This will add flavor for a delicious result.
How long can this mixture be kept and how to best store it?
Not very interesting in the end, rather blend.

I too was disappointed by this recipe. I took the suggestions in the comments about adding a bit of sugar, adding a drizzle of EVOO, and also doubled the spices. They were still bland and not very crisp. I added some orange juice and let it coat the nuts in the final bake since the zest (which was A LOT) was virtually undetectable. A lot of time/effort for not a lot of payoff. I would try something else next time.

I've made this three times now, once using a zester for the orange rind and twice following the instructions to use a knife and avoid the white pith in the rind. Even though I zested as lightly as I could, the knife procedure resulted in a far superior taste, with no bitterness and a clear, bright orange flavor. It takes a little patience but it's worth it.
Lots of work, I took great pains making sure there was no pith on the orange peel. End result, just OK.

Definitely used a grater for the orange rind and used different sides of the grater to mix up the sizes of the grated rind.  Also used a little of the juice.  Added an extra egg white for my second batch to spread and bind the spices more evenly.  A little extra salt helped.
The description mentions that this recipe uses cinnamon but it does not appear in the ingredients list—cloves do. Cinnamon and clove have very different flavor profiles. Can this error be amended?
Thank you for flagging. We've adjusted the description to reflect that the recipe uses cloves, not cinnamon.
Delicious, delicate, crunchy, festive, super easy. We loved these and will make them again!

Easy to make and a hit at an office party. Great flavor and texture. Using waxed paper to store them was key—they’re sticky—but worked very well. Next time I will stir the spices with a spatula well before adding in the coconut. 

I didn’t oil the silpat and had no trouble removing them. I used Crisco in my measuring cup instead of coconut oil, as that’s what I had on hand. Worked fine.
Brown sugar?
These are rich and big - I cut the size down from 1/4 cup to half that. Also I liked them on the more toasty side so let them cook a few minutes longer.
Where do I find the large coconut chips?
@Wendy I buy them at Trader Joe’s.
I have purchased unsweetened coconut flakes at Trader Joe's.
Trader Joes sells these, as do many supermarkets and natural foods markets. Check the baking aisle or the bulk bins.
Would coconut sugar be used rather than traditional sugar?
Can you substitute maple syrup for the honey for vegans?
@Cari D wanted to ask the same thing.

What a waste of good ingredients. The result was a jaw breaking concoction that would only delight a dentists pockets. They smelled delicious but I could not in good conscience even gift them as they were a sure path to cracked teeth.
Followed the recipe to the letter.  Found these to be too sweet.  The flavors didn’t really work together.  Texture is off-putting.  Don’t bother.

Next time - more than 15. The 1/4 cup loosely packed makes them big and they spread. I scooshed them back together while they were cooling. But next time I would make smaller ones - 1/2 the size, twice as many. Very sweet and tasty. Though my wife said to add chocolate chips!

title: Gúgúrú àti Epà (Sweet-Salty Popcorn and Peanuts) Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Just made this, and it was so good! It’s been years since I made popcorn on the stovetop. My first attempt resulted in unpopped kernels in burned sugar. I think I dropped the heat too low after adding the kernels and spices, and then burned everything trying to get back to the right temp. For the next two batches, I used a pan with a glass lid so I could see what was going on. Did not turn down the heat as much after adding the kernels and spices, and they starting popping within a minute. After that, vigorous shaking side to side and up and down. No unpopped kernels and nothing burned. I increased the sugar, salt and smoked paprika a bit on my third batch. I’ll be making this again soon!
The text says "smoked paprika" but the ingredient list says "sweet paprika."
Smoked paprika (pimenton) can be either sweet (dulce) or hot (picante).  If you want a smoked flavor to your popcorn then pick whichever spice level you prefer.

@Susan Apparently it is smoked paprika that enhances the smokey flavor traditionally achieved by roasting the corn over embers in Nigeria, so smoked paprika is probably what’s intended.  Thx for pointing out the discrepancy.

I made this tonight. We have a Whirley-Pop stovetop popcorn pot, so I adapted timings for that. It was tasty! My paprika is a blend of 2:1 spicy smoked and sweet smoked Spanish paprika (from a bulk food store). Because there’s so little of it (1/4 tsp) I don’t know that the variety makes a major difference - use what you prefer. It also isn’t overly sweet (not like commercial kettle corn). One note: step 3 says to “break up any clusters” but there were no clusters at all. I’d be interested to know what other whirley-pop users experience. Next time I make this I might mix the popcorn with the sugar-spice-salt mix before putting it in the pot, to see if that makes a difference. (Also, I only had 1/2 cup peanuts in the pantry so added 1/2 cup toasted pecans - yum!)

@Tricia J
Happy holidays Tricia & all! So glad you posted this! I love using my WhirlyPop, it’s so simple and the popcorn pops perfectly.  I generally use olive oil, what type did you use?
@carla I use coconut oil. WhirlyPop wonderful
@carla I probably used grape seed or canola for the popcorn

Two things:
If you have a wok with a lid, it makes the perfect vessel for stovetop popcorn.
and
Try using Lyle's Golden Syrup for the sweetener, it has such a rich delicious flavor. 
Merry Christmas world!

I found that it didn’t pop as well when I turned my heat to low. (I have an induction cooktop, if that makes a difference.) so I made it again, keeping the heat medium-high. It worked much better. Nice taste!
@Calli We have an induction cooktop as well. Does it scratch when shaking the pot?

@SKG A tip for induction stove users: put a piece of parchment paper between the pot and the glass. The heat goes through but there is nothing rubbing on the glass when you shake the pot. I just did this and it worked out fine on my rangetop. :-)
Can you make this with all ready made popcorn?
@MLB no you would not be able to make this recipe from ready-made popcorn because the coating is added to the kernels in the oil and popped together.
Has anyone tried this in a silicone microwave popper?

@bonnie taffer 
I have the same question!

@bonnie taffer

I tried it in mine! Unfortunately the sugar burner and stuck to a few pieces. The rest of the popcorn was great. We wound up remaking it on the stove and it was worth the extra effort! Maybe with a close eye and shaking it occasionally you could avoid the burn in the microwave?

I think it’s  personal preference. It already has sugar, so it’s sweet. The smoked paprika will add the smoky flavor. That’s what I’ll use, because I love it. If you would like it, sweeter, use sweet paprika. Cooks choice.
I used the NYT's suya spice recipe with sugar. That spice mix includes smoked paprika, peanuts and ground ginger. Really easy and delicious!

@vicki Sweet paprika doesn’t have sugar or any other sweetener. Saying it is sweet is a convention meant to convey that it is not spicy (“hot”.) Italian sausage is also described as either hot or sweet, meaning spicy or not spicy.
I've been a fan of the kettle corn recipe so of course I printed off this one. I am thinking double all the ingredients except peanuts, just cause. As in the kettle corn recipe.

I have both smoked and regular paprika, and was confused because the write-up says “smoked paprika” and the ingredients list said “sweet paprika” so I used the regular. What I got isn’t very good. I might try again with the smoked paprika, but so far, this is kind of a blah recipe and a sad waste of peanuts.
made as written, but all the peanuts fall to the bottom. so I ate it with a spoon. I like the mild salt/sweet. nice change up from straight popcorn.
How could you make this with microwave popcorn?
The Genuine Whirley-Pop. Stirs with internal arms, no shaking needed. You’ll never use another pan, pot or (heaven forbid) microwave again.
But it's made out of aluminum, so it won't work on my induction stove (only pots that are ferrous work on induction stoves).  I'm on a quest to find a stainless-steel one! Any ideas?
@Naomi from Pittsburgh They do make a stainless steel version.
Yep. Just joined Team Whirley Pop.
This is different in a tasty, moreish/keep-eating-it kind of way. I would add a pinch of cayenne pepper for some heat.
Great idea! Or maybe a bit of chipotle, for the smoky taste?

@Naomi from Pittsburgh
The notes mention smoked paprika( for the smoky) but the ingredient list says sweet paprika. Spanish Smoked Paprika comes sweet/bittersweet or hot so you can adjust to your heat level. Chipotle would definitely up the smoky heat game!
Wonderful! I tried it with both the smoked paprika and Aleppo pepper. I will be sticking with the Aleppo pepper from now on. Make single batches and combine if more is needed.

I bet in Africa they’re using palm oil to make the popcorn. It’s amazingly tasty and definitely a favorite cooking oil in the African dispora. It’s taste is unique any any recipe is definitely changed for the better by using it.
Do we use sweet paprika or smoked paprika?
@Shera smokey. See my above reply to that same question, when they publish it.

The description says smoked paprika.
The recipe says sweet paprika.
Which is correct?
Sweet paprika in the ingredient list, smoked paprika in the description.  Which is preferred?

@lab
Smoked paprika can be sweet, middle and hot. You can get all 3 smoked in small tins from LaTienda
Quick question: what kind of paprika? The introduction mentions smoked paprika, but the recipe calls for sweet.
Quick and easy. I would double the amount of sugar and spices. Peanuts sink to the bottom. Use a spoon to scoop it out

I might try this in our whirly pop (basically, a pot with the lid having a wire inside and crank on top to keep the kernels and ingredients moving). Seems like this recipe could be made very well in there.
@Emcampbe Yes. Perfect.

I wasn't blown away. The sugar didn't mix in completely, so the corn on the bottom was burned in spite of having stirred it thoroughly. I wanted a more assertive mix of flavors, probably would have increased the proportion of smoked paprika. But all in all, there are easier ways to make a salty/spicy/sweet popcorn and nut mix.
@ Susan L  Microwave popcorn comes in a sealed wrapper, so you would not be able to add the sugar and paprika prior to popping.

Made it using coconut oil (my go-to for stovetop popcorn), and a bit more (smoked) paprika thant the recipe shows. Turned out perfectly cooked and spiced, but passing the bowl during family movie nite, the peanuts all sank to the bottom (sad face).

Love, love, love this recipe. I made a batch, quite quickly, for an impromptu gathering and it was a big hit. My only hint is to *sprinkle* the sugar/salt/paprika mix on top of the kernels and oil for better corn coverage. Cheers!

Also, I’m wondering what would happen if you put the peanuts in at the time of popping.  When I added mine after, they all went straight to the bottom of the bowl.  So I served the popcorn up in individual bowls, and topped it with more peanuts, any advice on that?

I found that the peanuts just fell to the bottom of the bowl and did not integrate with the popcorn. Not sure if this is just the way it is but I felt like maybe something was not right either in the proportions or maybe it was my error in how I made it.

Unquestionably an abject failure. Followed the recipe and helpful comments (using a glass lid atop my Calphalon that’s popped much corn over the years) and still ended up with a burned, acrid mess.

Could be me as I readily veganize recipes, but this season I’ve yet to make anything I’d bring a party, though my spouse eats failures, this was discarded  immediately.
Has anyone made this without peanuts? How is it?
Made this and love it. The peanuts fall to the bottom: serve with a spoon!

I'd love to make this, but as a new induction stove owner, I was wondering if someone could tell me if shaking the pot back and forth will scrape up my stove-top. Or will this recipe work if I lift the pot off the surface and shake it in the air?

cooked on an electric cooktop - absolutely keep the heat constant on medium - high for this recipe to work otherwise sugar just burns and popcorn doesn't pop.  Second batch turned out well but I will add more sugar and salt to the recipe next time...
Sweet and salty is always a treat. Delicious!
This was tasty, made as directed.  Will try the smoked paprika next time, and maybe a little bit more salt.  Would cayenne burn?  A little cayenne might be nice too.
Really good popcorn
There must be some special technique for making this.  I just ended up with salty popcorn and dark sticky blobs on the lid of my pan.  I don't have the patience to try it again.  It was a discard.
This recipe didn’t work for me. I tried it twice. At medium heat, the popcorn doesn’t pop. At medium-high heat, the sugar burns, even with constant shaking of the pot.

I may have diluted the piquancy by upping the amount of popcorn (3/4 cup); I tried to proportionally increase the sugar, smoked paprika, salt, and pinch of cayenne, but the popcorn seemed only mildly seasoned. Also, I think I need the traditional paper cones for serving, since in a serving bowl the peanuts just sink to the bottom. Nonetheless, I stood over the bowl as it cooled and generously helped myself before bringing it to my neighbors’ gathering!

Sounds great.  Thanks for suggesting substitutions, too; appreciate that it might not be quite as good, but there are a couple of ingredients that we cannot have or can’t easily find here.

The article says that “a pressure cooker would also be ideal” for this recipe, but doesn’t give instructions. I’d love some, please.
Would again crab or shrimp paste work as a substitutions .
Would asian fermented black beans and crab or shrimp paste work as substitutions

title: Obẹ̀ Onírù (Designer Stew) With Eggs Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Fermented locust bean? Agege style bread? This might be a delicious recipe but where I live in central Brooklyn I have hard time finding more mundane food items like fennel bulbs, anchovies or fresh semolina Italian bread. Please no more recipes with esoteric, hard to find ingredients.

@Deirdre The recipe gives substitutes for the harder to find items. Sometimes a recipe inspires me to order unusual ingredients online to explore a cuisine I’m unfamiliar with. It’s a great way to discover new tastes.

Thanks for another recipe to use that fermented locust bean I first bought for an earlier recipe by Yewande. I didn't find it near where I live in my section of Brooklyn, but I didn't have to resort to ordering it on line either.

Great recipe! Enjoyed making this and the subs for 2 “rare” items made it very simple to prepare. Enjoyed with eggs as described and our side was cauliflower rice (this time) with very neutral fava beans. Will make this again. The smell and taste of the pepper-rich sauce was amazing. Thank you for this recipe.

title: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: How is it possible that chicken thighs are fully cooked after an at-most 13 minutes??? The recipe says to brown for 7 to 9 minutes, then flip and cook for an additional minute. Then "Cook until the chicken is cooked through, about three minutes." I'm surprised that no one has commented on this yet...
@Meredith S. Plenty of time for boneless thighs. If using bone-in I’d go longer.
Agree. I've never had boneless skinless chicken thighs cook that quickly.

13 minutes is a ton of time for average-thickness boneless/skinless thighs at high heat.  I made this and, having noted your comment, measured temp.  After the initial cooking, it was 168.  After going back in at the end, it got to 185, which is the optimal temp for dark meat.

How has no one cooked and commented on this? It was an umami flavor bomb, wow!! We LOVED it. We followed the recipe to a tee. Served over basmati rice, topped with fresh basil and lime. We will make this again soon! (P.S. the prep took some time and my stove top was a mess but thankfully it was delicious).
@Jess loved it too! (It took me an hour instead of 30 minutes!)
@stephanie s Remember, the time quoted in the recipe starts AFTER you have prepped the ingredients exactly as listed.
@Jess your comment sealed the deal on cooking this and you are 100% correct: umami bomb. It was so savory.

Made exactly as written (don't skimp on the basil). Then my guests and I compared portions with a splash of Apple Cider Vinegar to portions w/o the vinegar. The addition of vinegar unanimously got the thumbs up. Sprinkled 1/8th of a tsp on each serving. Perfect.
@Bobby Nash Ah!  I missed the vinegar suggestion in the notes.  That would have helped!
@Bobby Nash— wish vinegar was in ingredient list and instructions
It is interesting that the recipe calls for boneless/skinless thighs, but the photo appears to show the opposite being used.

@Greg Davids, my husband and I are not fans of chicken thighs, both of us preferring chicken breasts. And I really thought the chicken in the photo looked more like boneless, skinless chicken breasts!

@Greg Davids What is the opposite of chicken thighs? I’m so glad someone finally commented on chicken thighs! Why do so many NYT Cooking recipes call for chicken thighs in their chicken recipes?? They’re awful. All that bone and so little meat. I realize that cooking with bone-in will give the dish more flavor, but chicken breast is so much more satisfying.

@Greg Davids

Fully agree—I assumed I needed bone-in, skin on based on the photos, only to discover I needed the opposite, which I don’t have. I know I can adjust cooking time, etc., to use what I have, but it’s still a bit frustrating.
I’m a simple cook: I see a Yewande Komolafe recipe, I immediately add it to the week’s dinner menu, it turns out (predictably) incredible.

This one is a winner. The interesting combination of ingredients and flavors caught my attention. It definitely delivered. I did not have a habanero so I substituted sambal oelek, and I used dried basil during the simmer instead of fresh basil at the end. I added juice from 1 lime to fill in for the vinegar that is mentioned but not listed in the ingredients. We did not serve it with a starch but I think it would be really good with grits or polenta.
@BH thanks for the sambal oleak suggestion. My chile peppers had gone bad so I resorted to cayenne. Next time I will try your suggestion.

I will def give this a try.  Note to the publisher/chef:  the description of the recipe at the top mentions adding a "splash of vinegar" to the dish, but there is no mention of adding vinegar in the actual recipe.

Just curious.  The description says, "A splash of vinegar lends a tart zing to the sauce," which sounds great.  The recipe doesn't list vinegar but calls for lime wedges . . . guess I'll have to make it and see.
@Timmy I read that as vinegar (and basil) are optional ingredients if you want those flavors
@Timmy -anyone thought to add pickled peppers like the brand named ‘Mama Lil’s’ ? Available in quart size bottles at our Costco?
We made the recipe as directed. It was delicious! Highly recommend you giving it a try.
Any suggestions on making this recipe without anchovies (due to a serious fish allergy)?
The anchovies would give a lot of umami flavor, so some soy sauce or coco amines would give a similar flavor. You can also use powdered mushrooms or concentrated tomato paste to add umami.
They're generally a background note for umami. I'd add some more mushrooms and onions, or maybe a tsp of tomato paste.
Use Marmite or Vegemite!

I cooked this and liked it, but will save the stars until I do it correctly!

Even wrong it was good, but with Holiday Brain it took a couple minutes of "these are the largest anchovies I've ever seen..." to realize I'd just put in 8-10 *sardines*. 

I can imagine when I use the correct amount of the correct tinned fish it will be a lot more balanced! But if you find yourself in a pinch and don't mind bonus umami or like fish, just know it's still good if you goof up and do a ton of sardines.

This is so good, and genuinely easy. Also maybe the first NYT recipe I’ve made where I didn’t need to double/quadruple the seasoning. So much flavor in this, and it really did only take about a half hour to make.

Pairs surprisingly well with Sake
Is the "smooth side" the skinless side?
@JimB no I’d say the smooth side is the skin side. You’re more or less browning the skin and then cooking the rest of it non skin side down so the skin stays crispy.
@JimB it’s the side that the skin used to be on.
Paul, there's no skin....so no skin to 'stay crispy'!
Excellent, I used a jalapeno with the seeds since that is what I had. Also, I baked the thighs at 425 for 20 to 25 minutes before adding them to the mushrooms.
@Jennifer Stell you could use a large  saute pan. Since breaking my wrist it's what I have been using.

Totally agree with the ‘flavor bomb’ description - wow! What an amazing combination of flavors. The prep took a little longer than expected, but it was absolutely worth it and still perfect for a weeknight. I used bone-in thighs, which kept the chicken super juicy, though they did take a bit more time to cook through. And seriously… don’t skip the anchovies!
@Edgar Smith we did have leftovers… and I think the leftovers tasted better than the meal last night!

We loved this last night! I subbed TJ’s harissa paste for the fresh pepper, liked that it allowed me to control the heat (& add more to my own portion). Didn’t have the anchovies so upped the miso a bit. Served over quinoa for a tasty & guilt-free meal.

I made this as written except subbed about 3 Chipotle chilies in adobo (from my frozen stash).   Soooo good.

Then I made it with sweet Italian sausage instead of the chicken and served it over shredded spaghetti squash.... A flavor sensory delight.  My go to sauce from now on.  Add in whatever protein you want!

Meredith S asked how it's possible for chicken to be cooked through at 13/14 minutes; the chicken is boneless and skinless so it flattens out significantly.  Also, it is moved to a plate to rest after 8-10 minutes where it will keep cooking before going back into pan to stew for an additional 3 minutes.  Seems enough time to me, but I always use my own judgement as I go and if in doubt, pull meat thermometer out.

Delicious and easy! Added a bunch of distilled white vinegar during the simmering process towards the end and lots of lime when it came off the heat, this helped with the savory/umami/saltiness of the rest of the ingredients.
how much is "a bunch"?  It's not a great tip when it's this vague!!
Dear Lyd:  when I see a Melissa Clark or Sam Sifton recipe, that gets made immediately.  But I will take your tip and start watching Yewande and surely make this recipe
Had some of the vegetables left after all the chicken was eaten so I pan fried some boneless pork short ribs and added them to the veggies. As good if not better than the chicken batch.
This dish is fantastic. Made as written, although I used different hot peppers. It makes a great savory umami sauce that’s great over any grain. I used farro

Feedback: used large red onion because, why not?  Jalapeno, seeded/deveined: nice bite, not too spicy.  8 anchovies = too many, dominated flavour; 4-5 next time or skip.  1 tbsp miso was plenty.  Did 9 minutes smooth side, 3 minutes after flip. 4-5 minutes more after adding chicken back in.  Added water as sauce was getting a little dry.  Flavour was very good once you got past the slight dominance of the anchovies. No mushrooms; as a small child my father was killed by a large mushroom.

@Squirrel Butler 
I also did not want mushrooms so thanks for posting.

@Squirrel Butler
omg, mine too! That mushroom got around.

For those of us that like chicken thighs cooked until they fall apart, I suggest adding the chicken and it’s accumulated juices back into the pan in the middle of step 4, and transferring to the oven at 400 degrees for approximately 1/2 an hour, or until liquid has reduced to your liking. Worked perfectly, flavours all melded, and you could cut the chicken with a fork.
I'm making this for my special nephew's birthday tomorrow. He loves chicken and almost no other meat. He loves heat and has a great sense of flavor. He's going to love this as much as I do!!
I see vinegar mentioned in the leading paragraph, but not the recipe.  How much to be added, and what kind of vinegar?
Any thoughts on making this without mushrooms? Substitutions?
@Hila. See his recipe for salmon and green beans in red pepper sauce. Sauce looks identical and green beans could solve your concern.
Not a fan of miso. Any suggestions for substitution(s)?

Amazing flavor, will look forward to making it again. We used boneiin skin-on thighs as that’s what we had and didn’t want to debone ourselves.  We put finished it in the oven until the meat was falling off the bone. Delicious.

Four stars.  My intent was to make this as written.  When I opened the jar of roasted red peppers in my ‘frig, it looked like a biology experiment, so I used a fresh red peppers.  I had no scotch bonnet peppers, so I used 1/8t of cayenne for a half batch.  IMO, that was sufficient.  The dish was very good.  I should have put the diced fresh red peppers in with the onion/garlic rather than with the mushrooms.  Something to remember.  I will make this again with roasted red peppers.
Great recipe! we all loved it. Don‘t forget to add the splash of vinegar! It made a good dish perfect.
Yewande is a genius.

This was delicious. In order to keep the ‘spicy’ but tone it down I used a small can of mild chopped green chilies. Worked great for us.
Also, I thought all the cooking on high heat was too much. I got good results with the chicken and the mushroom sauce using a medium to medium high heat. I let the the whole thing simmer on low while the rice cooked.
This was absolutely delicious- used bone in chicken thighs and braised in the sauce for about 20 minutes to cook through.  Love the flavor combination!

I read Yewanda Komolafe’s amazing and courageous article in the NYTimes about her heath challenges. I cooked this delicious dish, thinking of her and all the many recipes she has contributed.  She is inspiring.
Made with chicken breasts. Juicy and delicious. Added more hot peppers.

I dont have a cast iron skillet large enough for this so I used my stainless steel skillet. Next time I will deglaze the pan after browning chicken. Also, I will chop 4 cloves of garlic instead pf leaving cloves smashed. But it is delicious and I will make it again soon! Prep took about 25 min and cooking took 30.

Made this in an Instant pot (10 min @ high pressure) with the following changes to adapt to what I had on hand:
Yellow onion instead of red
Jalapeno instead of habanero
No anchovies
Peanut butter instead of miso
Sauteed two red bell peppers instead of the jar of roasted peppers
1/2 cup water
Added a bunch of chopped kale to give it some color
No basil or cilantro (didn't have any)
Juice of 1/2 a lime

It came out great!

Anchovies and miso, huh? I betcha the original West African recipe has dawadawa/soumbala, an umami packed made from fermented seeds that stinks to high heaven and tastes like Heaven. It can be found in African shops in big cities. 
Or you can make it yourself like I did - a long, extremely labor intensive process but a fun worthwhile project. I used seeds from palo verde trees that are abundant where I live in Tucson Arizona.

@Janet Planet
The same palo verde trees some of us have in Pasadena? How cool!

When cooking boneless skinless thighs, should you leave the thighs rolled up, as they come in the package, or should you unroll them, ( thus having different thicknesses of the same piece)? The photo looks as though the thighs are left rolled up.
Leave rolled or unroll the thighs. "Cook until the chicken is cooked through." It's hard to overcook thighs.
Found the salt. Anchovies!  Smelly little fishies wont come in my house unless i grab someone elses cart by accident.   So 400 mg instead of 1200.
Note this uses BONELESS thighs, thats how it can cook that quickly.

We made this for dinner two nights ago, with two minor changes. We followed the directions up the point of returning the chicken to the pan. At that point, it went into a 275 degree oven for an hour - allowing us to enjoy a relaxed cocktail hour. The dish was delicious - and good enough to serve to company. 
We also made one change in ingredients. It's not easy for us to find habaneros or scotch bonnet peppers so we used 3 peppers from the Thai pepper I've kept going for 3 years.
@Ellen T Agree that this would make a fantastic dinner party dish.
I cut the chicken into cubes to make it more like a stew.

Made last night and followed the recipe close-ish. Fantastic results! Subbed a big squeeze (2 tsp maybe) of anchovy paste for the whole anchovies. Used 8 oz mushrooms and a 12 oz jar of roasted red peppers. Subbed a large hot jalapeño and three ripe serranos for the habanero. The pan was juicy enough without adding broth or water. Served with warm tortillas and walkerswood scotch bonnet hot sauce. Different, and very tasty!

Loved this. Ingredients sound like such an odd combo but came together so well. Instead of just jarred peppers I roasted some red/yellow/red pepper and red onion with a Serrano pepper then chopped fine to add to sauce. Easy weeknight dinner (if you have ingredients) and so yummy! Thanks for sharing.
This was pretty good but I probably won't make it again. Felt it made way too much sauce for the amount of chicken. I mean, a whole jar of peppers??
I mean, maybe your taste buds are not aligned with West African flavors. You do know, do you not, that you could cut the sauce in half next time?

That's good to know, because 1.5 lbs of chicken is never enough for 4 people, but I hate to increase the amount of chicken the first time i make something because then I'm worried there won't be enough sauce.

It’s delicious and worth the work, but as several people said, the prep and cooking take closer to an hour than half an hour. As for sufficiently cooking the thighs in that length of time, they’re boneless. It’s all they need.

We made this exactly as written last night except we were a bit shy on the chicken, and it was absolutely fabulous! I liked having more of the sauce to chicken ratio, so will do that when we make it again. 

There's a lot of prep work, but it comes together rather quickly after that.

I want to make this as it looks good but I have an aversion to the texture of bottled red peppers. Do you think it would be good if I puréed the red peppers  with the stocks. I also may try using puréed sun dried tomatoes??

First time ever commenting. Scandalized that this recipe isn’t viral. I had no peppers whatsoever in a jar or fresh. Swapped for Juanita Piquante peppers and some ancient sun dried tomatoes in oil from the fridge. My thighs had bones. Cooked longer, added water as suggested. Covered a bit at the end. The flavors, the transformation from one thing to another, the forgiving nature of the ingredients because the end transcends the parts. This recipe should be cooked.

I normally go for skin-on, bone-in thighs, but I’m glad I chose the opposite this time. Much easier to eat with the delicious vegetable-y sauce over rice. I didn’t have any chiles so subbed smoky paprika, perfect amount of bite.
@Nancy K. Smoked paprika is a great suggestion! I substituted red pepper flakes instead of the fresh chili, which also worked.

One and done. Starting chicken on high, even for a few minutes never mind 7-9, burned rather than browned that side of the thighs and filled the house with smoke. On the back end, the chicken took much longer than 3 minutes to thoroughly cook. The number of anchovies made the dish too salty. And the cleanup of the stove, despite using a splatter screen, added to the disappointment.
I didn't like the number of anchovies.

@Jess completely! I made this recipe last night and it is delicious but the cook time is way off and I finally just threw it in the oven for 15 minutes at 400 to make sure the chicken was up to temp.  Also, this 15 minutes in the oven transformed the sauce beautifully, making the flavors come together into a rich layered flavor rather than still being able to taste the separate ingredients. I would give this a 5 star rating if the 15 minute bake was added to the instructions.  Loved the flavors!
@Pentachrome I was thinking baking on a bed of rice might be good.  Maybe put rice and equal volume of broth in dutch oven, spoon these ingredients over, cover and bake 30 min?

I used bone in skin on thighs. Once I followed the stove top instructions, I put chicken thighs on top of the mushroom mixture and put in the oven for 15 min on 400 and everything was done perfectly. I served it with brown basmati rice and substituted cilantro for basil because that is what I had.
Can anchovy paste be used in place of fillets?
Yes, I've often used anchovy paste in place of fillets - usually ½ teaspoon = 1 fillet.  I make a lot of recipes that only call for 1-2 fillets, so I find paste more convenient.
I didn’t have Miso so I substitute
Why boneless skinless thighs? Bone in, skin on has more flavor and the skin provides collagen which many of us need and lack. How would you modify the recipe for bone in/skin on?
I fear anchovies. Will this taste fishy? I can finally appreciate the benefits of a few drops of fish sauce, but 8-10 anchovies . . .I’m hoping someone will help me feel brave.

@Laurie-Sue

If you can tolerate fish sauce, you can probably tolerate anchovies, a little better, actually. I suggest using no more than half of the amount called for in the recipe to start with. Give it a try, and tell us what you think!
@Laurie-Sue. Anchovies add a wonderful umami flavor to many dishes. In my opinion, it’s not fishy. It’s hard to describe, but I use them all the time in my cooking.

@Laurie-Sue
Vegetarian here so not a concern. Did give me a giggle!!

@Meredith S. I think it's due to them being boneless and skinless chicken thighs although I also don't think they would be cooked thru in that amount of time. it's strange as the picture appears to be bone in skin on...
I love the sound of this dish; so many umami ingredients!  But I have an issue with peppers, so thinking of 86’ing both the chili and red peppers…any thoughts on this?
@Louise olives ground to a paste, some salt and dried mushrooms would be an option
This was indeed a flavor bomb. But I found it too much of a muchness. I am not fond of food that yells at me. :) Won't be making again.
Miso is a common ingredient in Yoruba?
If I use bone in and skinned thighs how much should I increase cooking?
Cook until the bone-in/skin-on thighs are done to your liking. You could use a meat thermometer to test.
@diasporaco.com. No sheet pan used in this recipe.
@Terry anchovies give a salty musky flavor to foods where they are used as a spice. I would use either an olive or caper paste which have some of those qualities as a sub.

In my experience, habanero peppers retain more heat than Scotch bonnets after seeding (neither is "mild"). For me, the milder red Fresno peppers are hot enough. I found the miso problematic - not bad but a little too present in the final dish, like a visitor from another planet.
any substitution suggestion for the scotch bonnet pepper?  My ulcers would connect by means of another ulcer if I used a scotch bonnet.  Would a jalapeño do?@Jess

Loved this recipe! I had to make a couple of substitutions since I couldn’t find miso or a serrano pepper at the grocery store. 

Instead of miso paste, I used a Kikkoman Instant Tofu Miso Soup packet and picked out the tofu and seaweed before using it, & yes it was tedious. It worked well, especially since a few people mentioned the miso could be overpowering. 

Instead of the serrano pepper, I used a jalapeno pepper.

Great recipe and not very hard… this coming from a hesitant cook.  I followed the recipe exactly, the flavor was great, and the chicken was very moist.  For some reason it was a bit salty. I used everything fresh with the exception of the roasted red peppers (from a jar) which contain a high sodium content, the miso was low sodium.  I followed one viewers suggestion of putting it in a dutch oven.  I think it saved a lot of clean up time. :-)  Next time I’ll put in extra mushrooms.

I didn't have a cast iron skillet, so I had to lower the temps for my pan. I also used two lbs of chicken instead of a pound and a half. Next time, I might use less anchovies, because it was a little too salty for my tastes. I did not add any salt!
I am a totally weenie when it comes to heat, I left out the hot pepper, but left all the rest in, as is, amazing flavor without it.
Pretty straight-forward prep, excellent recipe. Served over basmati, paired with a GSM, and a baguette for sopping sauce. Will cook again.
Delicious recipe, great flavors, but careful with the habanero! I left the seeds in and came out 5-alarm. Next time will take out the seeds and membrane before mincing and should be perfect.

SO good! I used skin-on bone-in thighs and fish sauce (1 Tbsp) instead of anchovies. This meant cooking the chicken longer- an extra 10 minutes at the point where thighs are added back to the pan & then putting them on a sheet pan in a very hot oven to finish completely and re-crisp the skin once the sauce was finished. My chili was hotter than expected so I added a splash of cream as well. I highly suggest squeezing a good amount of lime juice into the sauce before serving. Nothing was left!
This is fantastic exactly as written.

The vinegar is neither in the ingredients list nor the prep steps. Made this having no idea how it would taste but we all liked it. Seemed more Asian than anything due to the miso paste. Is this really a West African recipe or fusion? My wife says it's a keeper but maybe next time she can do all the chopping!
No miso. What .may I substitute? Recipe looks great.

@Wesliextra 
Extra anchovies is what I will use bc I can’t eat  miso. Maybe a dash of soy sauce too?

Cooked it tonight.  Lots of flavor and gooey texture — perfect for a chilly evening.  Could have done without the anchovies…or at least half of them.  A splash of walnut or olive will do.  You might try a handful of oil-packed Greek olives, minced as a substitute.
Photo is of chicken thighs with skin and bones. Recipe says boneless skinless? Is that correct???
You are correct that the recipe says "1 ½ pounds boneless, skinless chicken thighs ." Follow the recipe.

One of the BEST dishes I've ever cooked! Hardly any leftovers, and there are only two of us.
It does take longer than the stated 30 minutes to prepare, but who cares? It was fun and delicious!
I did an adapted version in my instant pot.  TJs was out of anchovies, but it was still GREAT!  Thank you!
This was good. Even better as a leftover, cold, chopped and tossed in a salad with a vinaigrette. Delish for days!
Yes, it tasted good, but it was certainly messy and quite time-consuming.  Multiple serves in freezer- wonder how it will come out.
Last night we made this.  Not a fan, and my husband did not like it either.  Just not tasty and I did not change the recipe at all.
I completely agree! It just did not work for me!

This dish, called a flavor bomb by one writer, was definitely a bomb. I loved the chicken by itself but the sauce was way too much stuff thrown into the pan. I just didn't understand the taste. f you want to define Umani for me, perhaps that's what turned me off.
These are West African flavors. Perhaps that's something you don't like. (You can find a definition for umami on Google or in a dictionary.)

I was really looking forward to this with all of the great reviews and I LOVE mushrooms and spice.  Unfortunately, I thought the 16 oz of roasted red peppers completely overwhelmed tasting anything else in this dish.  Was disappointed :-(

anyone have suggestions for substitutes for onions and garlic? Can’t digest alliums anymore ( it kills me to say, since I love them and my signature dish used to be Beard’s Chicken w 40 cloves of garlic.)
@Ellen B Have you heard of/tried Fodzyme? Without it I have horrible issues with alliums and bananas but that stuff has been life changing. Kinda pricey but it works really well for me.

Really good served on quinoa with chopped banana on the side, and simple spinach. 
I wish garlic were measured in weight instead of cloves, as there are so many sizes and varieties. We used three cloves from the giant Red Russian variety, which seemed about right. 
Bone-in chicken works well.
I’m afraid I didn’t care for this recipe. There were too many different flavors going on and the result was a muddled, overly salty result. Our family won’t make it again.
I didn't have roasted jarred peppers so I used pickled sweet peppers and it was great.

The ingredient list—roasted peppers, mushrooms, anchovies, miso, chile, cumin—promised bold flavor.

Made exactly as written, it was fine but underwhelming. Pleasant, yet lacking the depth and punch I expected—about a 5–6/10. It mainly left me curious to seek out an authentic ọbẹ ata dín dín to see where the adaptation fell short.

Not bad, but not memorable enough to make again given the many better weeknight chicken dishes out

I used a jar of roasted bell pepper/eggplant paste (ajvar), subbed half a tube of anchovy paste (1tbsp) for the anchovies and used two tablespoons of frozen chopped basil instead of fresh basil. It was very good! And not too spicy for my kid since I followed advice and removed seeds from the scotch bonnet pepper.
