This was absolutely delicious. So much umami flavor. Definitely different flavor profile than we normally cook here in the south, and I'm so glad I tried this. Will definitely be making again. Served over left over mashed potatoes & I think it definitely would have been better over rice, and forgot the lime and was out of basil and also think it would have made a big impact as well. Regardless, it was still a 5 out of 5 so can't wait to try again but with the proper finishing and accompaniment.

"One of the best things I've ever eaten" said my husband.  I followed the recipe exactly, except for using canned jalapeño for the chili.  It did take a bit of time for prepping, but once you get the cooking underway, it moves along quickly. The mixture of flavors was unique (for me), and exciting, a umami bomb. We will be having this frequently.  Thank you, Yewande.

I'm still trying to get the recipe to work.  The jarred peppers I used weren't in oil so even drained, they had a some what acid profile. Maybe rinsing would help. And I'd be sure to chop them finely as 2 cups is a lot of pepper for 1.5 pounds of chicken. I added about 1/2 cups of white wine which rounded the flavors out for me. I didn't add the lime or vinegar yet but will see if that improves my lot. I would do skin on too. As an aside, the three cup chicken recipe (bon apetite) is amazing.

@Jess Perfect recipe that I recommend generalizing as an approach to sauce and braising with high heat broil finishing (for crunch and color) as a platform for different flavor and texture combinations. Not surprising given its origins.
- In one week, I used zucchini, eggplant, mushrooms, and cabbage in various combinations. Cabbage + mushroom was the weakest but still edible combination. 
- Fresh oregano most nights but dill for a twist - all worked well. Further accentuation with preserved lemon finish also worked well.
- Simmering the sauce with schmaltz from chicken or pan juices from roasted fish added richness I would lost from an adjacent roasting approach over the single pan one. Why separate? Timing and volume. 
- Lastly, real anchovies and not anchovie paste (too little) or whole dried anchovies (too much) commonly found in ethnic Asian and African stores. Trust me.
Even my picky “I don’t like vegetables or anchovies” family LOVED this! As others suggested, added about 1tbsp sherry vinegar at the end, but otherwise made just as written.
halved miso. added tomato paste a few minutes after the onion, etc. zested some lime at the end while adding the basil. apple vinegar added a nice balance.

Super dish!  It does take more than 3 minutes to cook the chicken thighs as others have commented, so allow more time.  My Scotch bonnet must have been really mild because I added some chili crisp to boost the flavor.  Will definitely make again!

Yes, i found it needed longer to prep and for the chicken to cook than stated here (maybe 10 - 15 additional mins for the chicken). But it all tastes yummy. I only did 1.3 tbsp of miso paste; with the anchovies that's plenty of salt, for me at least.

I loved the flavor. Pretty simple, although it took me an hour... I should have trusted my instincts that skinless chicken thighs seared on high for 7-9 minutes didn't seem right. Left a dry stringy crust after 7 minutes. Next time I'll use more oil, less heat, and more time.

@Elisa. After a first try with taste that was overpowering (too many anchovies, not enough mushrooms), I made another half batch the next day without anchovies and with extra mushrooms (and more chickem), mixed both batches together, and we loved the recipe! I had to add extra liquid both times.
@Meredith S. because boneless chicken cooks pretty quickly. Much more than 15-17 minutes will make it overcooked.
It was okay but not sufficiently delicious to warrant the time and ingredients. The fresh basil and lime were necessary to lift this dish up.

I love NYT recipes and am fond of Yewande's in particular.  But this one didn't wow me.  There were too many flavors -- anchovies, miso, cumin, peppers etc.  It was fine, but not great as I had been expecting based on the 5 stars, which I usually find to be a gold seal guarantee!

Meant to write my review months ago. Made this before the holidays without the miso and it was superb. When I asked my three young adult kids (one who is a vegetarian) what they wanted for Christmas dinner, the only answer that came back was Chicken. Having just had that terrible flu, I was relieved no one wanted a big spread. Made this recipe as written and it pleased everyone including the vegetarian who was ok with just the mushrooms and peppers. The spices give it a great kick for this cold nights! I preferred the first time with no miso, but maybe next time I’ll half the miso to see how that goes.
followed the recipe to T but squeezed the lime over the entire dish at the end, off heat. the medley of flavors was delightful – will make again and again!
Mine definitely did not cook that quickly. We had to finish them off in the microwave for an additional 30 seconds.

Just cooked this following the recipe exactly. The thighs were perfectly cooked in the time allotted, much moister  and tastier than breast fillets. . Also there was ample cooking time for the thigh fillets, the flavours were really good. The recipe is definitely a keeper.
OK Jess! I will give it a try, the review worked. I am always on the look out for SKINNED chicken thighs, my arteries cannot tolerate the skin. Will post after cooking.
Has anyone tried this with bone in thighs and doing steps 5-7 in the oven? If so, any suggestions?
@VicinNY Yes! When I added the chicken broth, I slid the pan in the oven for 25 minutes at 400 degrees F. That worked perfectly.
Can you make this delicious sounding meal in an alternative way? I have a flattop stove and no cast-iron.

Going to try it with local chicken legs instead.  Wonder if I could use my food processor to stand in for getting g the fresh bell peppers to something more resembling the puree state mentioned in the opening?  Not sure how far to take them.
We loved this one! my husband had 2 servings. It's a total flavor bomb, even though I didn't have basil to finish it.
@Meredith S. I guess you forgot to add in the times in steps 3 and 4.
@Peter - Chicken is removed from the pan in step 2 and not added back until step 5.  Therefore, steps 3 & 4 are rest time, not cook time, for the chicken.

Could spicy harissa somehow work here?  I have a jar on the shelf collecting dust.
I think you could easily use 1 or 2 teaspoons (to your taste) of spicy harissa in place of the habanero.
Forgot the vinegar, but still really good.  Served it with broccoli in a balsamic vinegar dressing.

Unfortunately for me, this recipe was big disappointment. With all the ingredients, I expected a flavor bomb but just got something with a vaguely earthy flavor. We made it exactly as directed but just not much flavor. Oh well, that happens sometimes.
This is a mouthwatering dish.  Thanks for sharing this.

The cooking time for the chicken seems too short.  Can anyone say how long it took when they prepared this recipe?  Thanks
@MKann I only had bone-in chicken thighs, so I finished them off in a 400-degree F oven for 25 minutes after I added the chicken broth, instead of waiting it out on the stove. That did the trick!

Why not just follow the recipe? Rather than opine about the cooking time, cook the chicken for the time directed in the recipe, and if the chicken is not done to your liking, cook it for a few more minutes.

Meredith S.:  These are boneless chicken thighs, they cook pretty quickly...

Thought I would love this but we did not. Will not be serving again.

My husband and I were not fans of this dish at all, which surprised me based on the reviews. The texture of the mushrooms combined with the roasted red peppers did not sit well with me and it felt off balance. I felt like the amount of mushrooms/peppers seemed excessive compared to the chicken. We used mostly cremini mushrooms with some oyster and shiitake. We didn’t see the comment to add a splash of vinegar, so maybe that would improve the flavor? Wow, was this good. I went with what I had on hand so I used my dried mushrooms from Italy. I really enjoyed the texture of them. I cut the recipe in half (thanks nyt for the sizing options.) My only regret was using more anchovies than called for because I didn't want to leave 1 lone anchovy in the freezer. Regardless, this was phenomenal.  Served with mushroom risotto and roasted broccoli.  5 stars.

Very tasty dinner (over Basmati rice). Used bone-in, skin-on chicken. Browned it well initially and then cooked it until internal temperature heat 165 F., about 20 minutes.Used Calabrian pepper (bomba) instead of the chile pepper. Easy, attractive, filling, and delicious meal.

This was great! Our family loved it! 

I think I overcooked the mushrooms, though. They were reduced too much. I tried re-hydrating them with chicken broth, but they came out a little too salty (and our family loves salt!). So I'd definitely stick with using 1/4 cup water, and not chicken broth, at the end if you need more liquid. I'd also recommend using low-sodium chicken broth for this recipe. Plenty of flavor and salt still I'm sure.

I made it. It was easy. I enjoyed it, but it is not over the top great as described in many of the comments. I would also not chop the onions and peppers as thoroughly as I did. I used a food processor and they were too finely chopped.
Those who described it as "over the top great" surely thought that it was "over the top great." Lotsa 5 stars out of 5 ratings.

Yum! Will serve over couscous (not rice) next time!
This was fabulous! I had to restrain myself from eating too much. the lime and basil push it from excellent to outstanding.

This did not turn out well for me, my husband and I thought it was bitter. I am not sure what went wrong, as I followed the recipe, using a squeeze of lime at the end as suggested. I won't be making it again.
This is delicious. The combination of flavours did not sound like they should work, but they really do. We loved it.

Chicken will be undercooked at 13 minutes only.  Needs more cooking time I think.
Has anyone left the red peppers out?  They give me heartburn but given the amount, it seems like a necessary part of the recipe.  Please tell me it's not!
This is an absolute winner. Easy to make, amazing aromas, super tasty, and just as good the next day - that is if you don't eat it all.

I'm a little surprise that Everyone Loved This. 675 five star ratings?  It has a kind of strong, unusual flavor (anchovies, plus miso, plus a lot of garlic, plus really lots of peppers) that I expect is not for everyone.  I liked it quite a lot, but not 5 stars.  My husband hated it.  After three bites he said he couldn't stand it and that was that. (He's a moderately adventurous eater.)
Yummy! Saw it. Tried it. LOVED it! This is a delicious dish which my husband, who had dysphagia, can eat! So tasty.

Wow!  I did make a change to the recipe.  I was out of anchovies so I used oil packed sardines and a few drops of fish sauce.  Next time I would be tempted to just make the sauce and leave out the chicken and serve over rice, pasta or polenta.
Is there something I can substitute for the 1 (16-ounce) jar roasted red bell peppers, drained and chopped (2 cups)?
Can one use the very good Korean GOCHUJANG (fermented peppers) for the sauce?

Gochujang will lose the fresh pepper-onion character and gain a distinct Korean fermented sweetness that isn’t Yoruba in profile.  Quick hybrid substitute: Cook ½ cup blended roasted/sautéed red bell peppers and ¼ cup blended sautéed onion in neutral oil (palm or vegetable) with salt to taste, then stir in 1 tbsp gochujang at the end to restore freshness and aroma while keeping the chili base OR use Ají rojo–style pepper paste (Latin markets).

Incredible, but the chicken was not fully cooked based on these guidelines. Once you put it back into the sauce at step 5/6, I'd cover and cook for 10 minutes rather than just 3 more minutes uncovered.
The "guidelines" are: "Cook until the chicken is cooked through." Follow the tip, not the time. Equipment varies; ingredients vary; starting temp of chicken varies.
I really enjoyed the flavors in this, I used a jalapeño because that’s what I had, and I also added a few castelvtrano olives.

Fantastic recipe!!!! I added some dried basil in 10 mins before the end as I didn’t have fresh, used chicken breasts cut in half instead of thighs as I’m not a big fan of chicken thighs, and sprinkled a little apple cider vinegar on the top as others have done (as well as some lime juice as per the recipe). Delicious, absolutely one to make again - the flavours are fab, and quite unlike anything I’ve made before. 10/10

We made this and loved it.  The flavors were so good.  The thighs browned in the time allotted and then when we put them back in at the end they finished cooking in the  juices.  Loved the basil and lime to finish.  Best part is that we have leftovers!
My suggestion would be to find another recipe that is way less work. I followed the recipe to a T and it took a considerable amount of time for a dish that was pleasant but not remarkable.

I have skin on, bone-in chicken thighs.  How long should I cook them in the pan?  Should I remove the skin?
I would remove the skin, but you don't have to. Step 5 says: "Cook until the chicken is cooked through." That's what you should do.

This is a definite keeper. I used 6 medium-sized thighs, a 12 oz jar of roasted red peppers, a jalapeno, and 7 oz of mixed cremini and shiitake mushrooms. The picture is of bone in skin on thighs, but the recipe is with boneless skinless thighs
Delicious and easy to make.  Some of the cooking times listed in the recipe seemed slightly longer for me, but entirely managable.

@Siri using sardines best comment ever

Very good. I cut in half and used bone in thighs as that's what I had. I also used half of a seeded habanero, next time I would use the whole habanero (seeded).

Delicious, we'll add it to the rotation.
The end result was delicious but the mess on my cooktop was pretty bad.  Loved the dish but didn't love the cleanup. Try a pot with higher sides like a cast iron crock pot.

I agree with others: this WAS a umami flavor bomb! However, I also found it a bit too salty with 2tbsp red miso and anchovy paste (which I subbed instead of only anchovy filets). Suggestions for next time: only 1 tbsp miso. Sautée mushrooms after chicken to increase umami from Maillard reaction. Great flavor, chicken very tender and tasty.    Prep did take a bit longer.

Nestled the thighs in the sauce - kept the skin crispy.  Wow!  Great flavor.  Served with rice and had the leftovers a few nights later.  Even better!

to a tee. Added fresh basil at the end. Forgot about the vinegar note. Leftovers will probably be outstanding.

Cooked per recipen using a Habanerochilenannd it was excellent! Boneless chicken thighs will be done within the specified cooking time.

Are we supposed to cook the mushrooms so they release their liquid OR are we to add the broth right away, which seems like it would make it harder for mushrooms to release liquid.  If someone can give some guidance, i'm going to make this over and over!
Sharon, the sequence I followed was to add the mushrooms BEFORE adding the stock.  The whole thing was delicious.  John, New York.
If you use bone in thighs, the cook time will not be right. I put in oven step 6 at 400 degrees for 15 minutes. Came out perfect, did not dry out. Yum
Amazing!!!

This was absolutely incredible, even though I used plum tomatoes cooked to jamminess instead of the bell pepper jar, because that's what I had; everything else as described. Now on our heavy rotation.
Made last night using fresh red peppers. The recipe is definitely a keeper but too salty for my taste. I will rinse the anchovies before I make it next time.

Haven't made this yet, but the instructions for the chili (without seeds for mild) is very misleading. There is little or no heat in the seeds, all the heat is in the flesh and the pith.
Deseeding for mildness is a myth.
If you want your dish to be milder, use half the chili with or without the seeds.
@Malcolm I agree with the de-seeding as not reducing the heat, I just toss the seeds because I’m already in there removing the pith/membrane and the seeds just get in the way.
Any suggestions to replace the jarred bell pepper? Cannot abide bell peppers of any kind - normally sub chopped carrots but the jarred version is throwing me.

Step 7 says to "serve immediately". However, it seems like the type of dish that could be made ahead and reheated. Chicken thighs reheat fine in my experience. If anyone had leftovers, could you comment on how this was reheated.
Used the 1/8 tsp of apple cider vinegar on each portion of chicken as someone else mentioned. Also couldn’t find a scotch bonnet so I used a de-seeded habanero. It’s an umami bomb and a 100% winner!
Halved brussel sprouts replaced mushrooms, works great! And being too lazy I did 't debone the thighs. To fasten the frying I used a burgerpress. Lovely combination though also with variations!

Fantastic recipe BUT....you'll need to cook the chicken a LOT more than 3 minutes after you return it to the skillet.  Also....you can use boneless/skinless as the recipe suggests, but it really works for any chicken variation.

Winner, winner chicken dinner! This was scrumptious. My only small changes were to use anchovy paste instead of tinned, and a long red chili pepper for heat. Boneless, skinless thighs & mixed shiitake, oyster, cremini mushrooms. A fare amount of prep but it came together quickly. (Not sure what the basil is supposed to add, but my husband liked it.) Also, I'm glad I used water and not chicken broth since there's already plenty of salt from the miso. Served with white rice. Two thumbs up!

This was fabulous. One change I made is that after returning the chicken to the pan, I slid the pan into a 350 oven for 7 or 8 minutes as not to have guess when the chicken was cooked through. I’ll be making this again.

This meal came out absolutely delicious & I will definitely make it again. But I have to take issue with the misleading prep time. Ready in 30 minutes? Hardly. I'll admit that I'm an amateur in the kitchen so I tend to double any estimated time a recipe states but the dicing & slicing of all the ingredients took half an hour. This recipe took me 2 hours from start to serve (yes, rank amateur) but it was SO worth it.

Delicious! I used the ingredients I had on hand: 10 oz frozen sliced Shiitake mushrooms, 8 oz jarred roasted red peppers, a partially deseeded jalapeño pepper, entire medium yellow onion, and anchovy paste. I did have red miso, garlic and chicken thighs.

Really delicious dish full of umami flavors! Definitely took me closer to 90 minutes than 30 from start to finish, but I am not the speediest cook! 
I should've let my mushrooms cook a bit longer before adding the broth, I probably gave it 5-7 minutes and think another 3-5 would've been ideal. 
Also from cooking this in a cast iron skillet, I would recommend going half a temp lower depending on hot your stovetop gets. Will definitely cook this again with a few tweaks!

I accidentally left the chicken in the whole time the first time I made this, and having now made it again following the recipe, I like it better with the longer braise — the chicken was falling apart the first time and a tiny bit tough the second.
This is good, but it's really fiddly to serve as a weeknight recipe, and. . . well, a non-weeknight recipe has to be truly exceptional to crack the rotation.
Easy to make and very good. I didn't have anchovies and I used fish sauce and soya sauce instead and so maybe it was maybe also a little less caloric. Next time with anchovies.

This is one of the best preparations of chicken thighs I have tasted. It’s also a great exercise in building a sauce. Umami for days. My husband can’t handle spice right now so I added a teaspoon of sharp paprika instead of the habanero, and I added a dollop of sour cream on the side. Could have added chili at the end but honestly, was just great. I will make this again.
These are delicious (that black pepper pops!) but the cooking time is off. I think 10 minutes might be the halfway point. I did 15 and they’re still doughy.

@Abracadabra
Recipe says 10 min prep; 20 minutes cook.  I assume that’s 20 minutes baking time — about what your comment implies.
@Baba not sure where you get 20 min bake time, Step 6 pretty straightforwardly says to bake for 10 min and cool for 15. But when I try it tomorrow… I’m gonna leave ‘em in longer! :)
@Somewhere it says 20 minute cook time at the top
Just made these. They baked to perfection in 10minutes.  They are very small cookies.  How long would you think they would take to bake?

Followed the directions as written cooking for about 20-25min. Though they looked good and tasted aok doubt will be on the menu again any time soon for all the prep work that goes into making these. Prep work is definitely >10min. Nice change for a sweet potato (which I love) dessert I suppose, may try again if the mood strikes but fam was less than enthused

I made these with purple sweet potatoes from our garden (baked in microwave rather than boiled) and chopped dried cherries. The dough was initially dry so I added another tablespoon milk and it came together. Based on other comments I cooked them for 15 minutes (rotating halfway through) at 350 convection in an oven fitted out with baking stones. They didn’t really puff and taste a little dried out (probably because of the convection setting?) but provide an interesting flavor combo. (And the purple sweet potatoes yielded pretty cookies.)
I have a can of sweet potatoes I want to use up. But they’re packed in syrup. Any thoughts about cutting back on the sugar to compensate? If so, I’m wondering how much. TIA!

@kimz
Wash and drain potatoes

I am curious at the five star rating this recipe enjoys, not because the flavors aren’t promising, but because the dough required so much flour to effectively roll out that the texture and taste were impacted. Once rolled, the transfer was messy. I ended up doing a drop cookie which I then flattened with a tea glass.  

If I attempt this recipe again—and I doubt I will be inclined to do, given the time it took to fuss with the dough—I will roll out the dough on the same parchment I’ll bake with, cut shapes in place, and transfer the entire sheet to the pan. That’s the only way I can envision these working.

The lemon glaze was scrumptious, however. I had to resist the urge to consume it by the spoonful.

@Nicole my experience was different: the dough was initially dry and crumbly (I weighed the ingredients), so I had to add a tablespoon of milk to get it to hold together. Didn’t need much flour to pat and roll out dough. Used a metal bench scraper to cut them into squares.
@Tricia J I really wanted the recipe to work! Maybe I will give it one more try.
I had the same experience. The dough was simply never going to be roll-out-able with gobs and gobs more flour. It was much more the consistency of a drop cookie, which I did.
These cookies are delicious.  I added chopped dates and pecans to the mix.  Cookies are not overly sweet.
Step six says to bake 6 to 7.5 minutes, turn the pan, and bake another 6-7.5 minutes.  That is my interpretation.

I made these Mbatata cookies. They were wonderful. The lemon/sugar glaze pushed them to over-the-top goodness! I used dates as the fruit (as I had them in the house), but I think slightly stronger flavored dry fruit, like cranberries or apricots, would be superlative.
Superlative!! Wonderful distinction - l will make these for sure. They sound utterly splendid ♥️
Life changing… they almost resemble more of a delicate pastry than a cookie to me. Best when warmed up in the microwave for twenty seconds!!

These cookies are absolutely amazing. The dough is extra sticky - be prepared. While rolling them out did take some time, I used extra flour, and it worked perfectly. They are already a hit with my family! My mother asked when I’ll be making them again.
Mine took between 15-20 minutes to reach the golden brown stage. Delicious!
OK, Americans have a tendency to call yams sweet potatoes.  Yams are orange and sweet potatoes are more yellow, right?  Which vegetable is this really made with, or maybe it doesn't matter?

It definitely *does* matter.. sweet potatoes are orange inside, much larger than yams which are very small, by comparsion (and sweet potatoes are called kumera in New Zealand, and pale yellow inside). We cannot attach pics sadly.

Wow are these good! Not too sweet, just the right combination of spices. Perfect with coffee. He used dried cranberries but I can see dates, raisins or others being equally tantalizing. Definitely a recipe to keep!

As written the batter doesn't come out sticky.  It is very granular.  I added a few more tbsp of milk to pull it together and until it was moist enough to work with.  I used a cookie scoop.  Sacrilegious, I suppose, but it works and with less mess.

Flavor if fine.

Tricia J, Lisa's comment is asking about the distinction between orange-fleshed sweet potatoes, colloquially called "yams" here in the U.S., and cream/pale yellow fleshed sweet potatoes--the orange-fleshed sweet potatoes ("yams") are sweeter and moister than the creamy/pale yellow fleshed variety. You are correct that ALL sweet potatoes are of the genus Ipomea. The "true" yams you reference are a completely different genus, Diascorea, and are native to Africa. Genus Ipomea native to S. America

@Brian in that case shouldn’t she have written “sometimes sweet potatoes are called yams.” Because unlike the era when some marketing folks decided to set their product apart by calling them yams (if Martha Stewart’s version of events is to be trusted), actual yams are available in some US markets these days.
Followed the recipe as written. Came out perfect -everyone loved them!!

@Tricia J Lisa's comment correctly states that orange-fleshed sweet potatoes are very often labeled "yams" in grocery stores and markets here in the U.S., especially at Thanksgiving time. And canned orange-fleshed sweet potatoes (the only type of sweet potato I've ever seen canned) are invariably labeled "yams". Yes, "true" yams (Genus Dioscorea) are now available in some cities. True yams are larger and look very different from any type of sweet potato (Genus Ipomoea).
I ended up kneading the dough with my hands in the bowl to incorporate all of the flour. Using a spatula was not working. Dough rolled out well and cookies were a hit.
Can I freeze these puppies?

The total time -- making, baking, cooling, and icing -- is way off. I used homemade mashed sweet potatoes. There is a too "generous" amount of flour used at all the stages. As others have noted, the 2" squares did not rise in the oven. Cooling and glazing were a balancing act to get right with the proper cookware. The end result, though, was delicious and very satisfying -- a cookie that wasn't too sweet, with just the right amount of dried fruit (cranberries), whose taste improved with time.
would these work with canned pumpkin i?

5 stars. I made these on the big snow day since I had baked my stash of sweet potatoes (adding to a previous thread here, my grocery store has always called them yams but recently started calling them sweet potatoes). Definitely would not have worked as rolled cookies without a lot more flour but are perfect as drop cookies. I halved the recipe and got 16 smallish cookies. I put chocolate chips in half and dried cranberries in half. I wonder if they would work as bars.

Sometimes yams (orange, sweeter) are called sweet potatoes, and that’s what I’m thinking is called for here? Classic sweet potatoes are less sweet and light yellow in colour. What’s the consensus here about which type is indicated in the recipe?

@Lisa I think you might have it backwards. True yams are less sweet than sweet potatoes. This recipe is talking about sweet potatoes, from the genus Ipomea (same as morning glories!). There’s a recent blog post on Martha Stewart that talks about yams vs sweet potatoes. Labeling in grocery stores might still mix them up though.

@Lisa, from reading the comments it looks like you can use any variety of sweet potato to make these cookies. If using the orange-fleshed sweet potatoes (often called "yams" in U.S. stores and markets, as you note) you might have to adjust the liquid in the recipe, as orange-fleshed sweet potatoes are moister (and sweeter) than other varieties.
My local super market I shop the most in had a nice surprise.  A bin of Japanese sweet potatoes.  Going to use one of those today and give this recipe a try.  Looking forward to the results.

I didn’t have any trouble handling the dough…
I did use sour cream instead of milk (I wanted to increase the moisture). 
Used dried cherries. Did not need 2 lemons or 2 1/2 cups powdered sugar.

I love these, but they really feel more like scones then cookies to me - I roasted and mashed one small sweet potato - ended up with more like 1 cup, but I think using it all worked well and I didn't need to add any extra liquid to help it come together (also didn't have quite enough brown sugar, so the roasting helped add that bit of sweetness back in)  Either way, a perfect Thanksgiving breakfast!

Excellent. Made with raisins and used 2T Forager cashew/oat/coconut creamer. Did not have enough confectioners sugar on hand to make the glaze, but these cookies are delicious as is. Just the right amount of sweetness. I had extra mashed sweet potatoes and these cookies were a great way to finish them off.
Do you need to add more flour if you use fresh sweet potatoes? My dough is a sticky disaster right now!
@Andreas G. Agree with this and would add that I think the dough needs about 30 minutes of cooling before you work with it.  Altogether I think this is an 1 1/2 hour effort.
I wonder if persimmon could be substituted for sweet potato here. I have a lot of persimmons to use up.

Made my usual vegan and gluten free adjustments and found it easier to squash a ball into the recommended thickness was much easier and faster. Further shaping was done by using a pan which I put on top after. Great way to use use a pre cooked sweet potato for a sweet treat! 

Only used half the amount of brown sugar since my steamed sweet potato is already pretty sweet.

These were great! I used fresh mashed sweet potatoes (kept skins on) and didn’t roll them out and added a bit more baking soda to help them puff a little. I just rolled them into a ball and pressed down to create cylinders. The icing is delish and it’s a very light and festive cookie!
Just pulled these out of the oven.  Great texture but absolutely tasteless.  I used more spice than called for.  I am going to try using hot honey in the glaze to see if that helps.
Followed recipe.  Baked for around 20 minutes to produce a soft, mildly sweet cookie.. They were a big hit at the party I brought them to—curiously people thought they were ginger cookies.

@Dina  Sweet potatos have a strong color and delicate flavor after boiling, vs. baking or roasting.  By using more spice than called for, it may have overwhelmed the sweet potatoes and the spice itself.  It is very easy to use too much spice & end up with a soap flavor.
can these be frozen and if so should they be glazed before or after?

This is a nice, tasty treat. It’s hard to describe these other than to say that they taste simple and pleasant. Making them did take a little bit of care, but I wouldn’t say they were particularly difficult. I made mine with dates and a light drizzle of the lemon glaze. I had no trouble rolling out the dough. The cut squares were solid enough to transport to the pan by hand without falling apart. The spices create a warm flavor.

Confusion #1. Unless sugar is packed down to 3/4 cup level, 600 G of sugar is not 3/4 cup unpacked. 

Confusion #2.  Silicone spatula is useless except for lifting from bottom of bowl, I used a fork to mix ingredients.

Confusion #3.  Baking time in recipe 12 - 15 minutes.  Should of read comments.  Ended up baking 23 minutes and still didn't brown the edges.

Confusion #4.  These are more of a bar than cookie.
Delicious. I added some cardamom as well. Thank you, Yewande.
I used oat flour, going berries and added maple syrup, gran marnier and scotch (for the peat).  was delicious.  Needs to bake longer.

title: Kelewele-Spiced Pork Chops and Pears Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody:
The comments so far are wonderfully entertaining.

See, there’s an actual recipe with instructions that explains why the pork you are looking at in the photo that none of you actually bothered to read past looks the way it does.
Those are the slices of the meat once it is removed from the bone. Each chop has a “sirloin” and a “filet” piece—when you slice those, you will get the rectangular pieces.

Yes. Thats right, they are simply the latter piece of pork, laid flat, and sliced. You can see the size of the slices change as you move down the "sirloin". Wish people wouldnt obsess about this, and instead  read the instructions properly, thenget on and make the recipe!
Okay, so we have 19 comments, many from people who didn’t read it and not one of them from a person who has made the dish?  Thanks for the “help,” everyone.

I really liked the salty-sweet spice mix with the pork chops. But my husband didn’t care so much for the unusual taste. I might could have gotten higher praise from it if I had left off the mint. I loved it. I averaged our ratings.

Five-star recipe. I bypassed the pork chop debate and used a pork tenderloin. I seared it for a few minutes on all sides, then cut it into thick medallions. I seared the flat parts, set the medallions aside and continued the recipe. I used large Canadian Anjou pears and served with a cauliflower mash and green beans with sliced almonds. No mint.
I agree. Those don't look like bone-in pork chops to me. More like sliced pork loin.

Reading this article & accompanying recipe it should clear up the pork chop mystery: https://www.americastestkitchen.com/recipes/14321-pan-seared-thick-cut-bone-in-pork-chops Ive used this method before and made this a few nights ago and the pears and seasonings would have been a great addition!

Made this tonight and it got rave reviews, even from the teenager who normally scrapes the sauce off everything, and the husband who does not like sweet and savory combined. Some adjustments:  made it with boneless pork chops (reduced searing and cooking time accordingly).  Added a pinch of hot pepper to the sauce w the zest and juices because we are like that, and only used one star anise pod because I often find that flavor overwhelming (still shone through). adjustments. Came together under an hour, w/o a lot of fuss.  This recipe is a keeper for sure.

Made this last night (November 16, 2025).  

I found some of the instructions confusing, specifically what was considered the marinade.  So in that respect, I sort of “winged it” (something I frequently do when cooking).  I wound up adding all the ingredients to my large bowl when marinating the meat.  

Found holding the chops on edge to sear the fat caps problematic.  I tried, but don’t think I really got them seared.  And in the end I do not think it made a difference.  I did follow the directions with regards to searing one side five to six minutes and just “kissing” the flip side for a minute. I left the plate with the chops on my stovetop’s warmer section while working with the scallions and pears.

I wish I could include photos here, but I did post them to Instagram and Facebook.  Even though i didn’t follow the instructions to to the letter, the results tasted fantastic. 

A caution to anyone trying this recipe - the chop must be thick!  One of my worries was would the chop taste dried out? It did not!  It was fully cooked but moist.  I’m certain that a thinner chop would have been overdone.
They do look odd.  More like slices from a loaf pan.  perfect rectangles.  Definitely not pork chop-looking.

This was a wonderful, festive dish. I took François Dionne's advice and used a pork tenderloin. The flavors were perfect for the holiday season. I served it with mashed potatoes, butter-braised mustard greens, and a fennel-citrus salad.
The pork chops in the photo are very unusual. My butcher doesn't have anything like them.  Where did you buy them?
Unusual?  How?
They look like slices of meat loaf
@Oaky yeah, I'm not sure either. they look to be sliced boneless loin chop on left, pear on the right
clearly the pork in the picture is not from 2 bone in pork chops. And where is the browning.
Delicious! My husband made this for dinner tonight and we loved it.
Couldc make this with pork tenderloin

To use up vegetables in the fridge, I tosses cubed butternut squash with a little oil and smoked paprika and baked for 15 minutes then added the chard on top with just the water they were rinsed with and baked 10 more minutes. Finish under the broiler on the top rack for 5 minutes. Everything came out perfect and just one pan!

This was terrific! Given some of the other comments I had low expectations but was overwhelmed by my farm box and the oven was on so I tried it - and I am so very glad I did! We eat lots & lots of greens so it's nice to have a preparation that just a bit different.
Added 1/4 of a red onion thinly sliced and a pinch of red chili flakes for extra flavor. Also used red wine salt from Dry Canyon. Quite good
I had high hopes for this chard treatment but it was unpalatable
Absolutely looked fine but actually tasteless.
I drizzled lime juice on it at the end. Really popped!
Broil with rack in highest position?

This is a good use of big amounts of chard.  I'd like to figure out a flavorsome protein so serve on top of it, but I imagine any number of choices would go well.

And I too like red pepper flakes on it.
This was ok. I couldn’t really get that char correctly. Not for me.

title: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody:
Found this while looking for a Mediterranean-ish sheet pan shrimp recipe and was pleased to discover we had everything (except the limes—subbed lemons) and it was DELICIOUS! Served over pearl couscous with a tomato/cucumber/chickpea salad. 

Keep an eye on your kale during the broil: our broiler has both high and low settings and without guidance beyond “broil,” I set it to high and slightly burned the kale by the time the shrimp were done. But once it’s all tossed together with the accoutrements the extra toasty kale just adds to the flavor. Will definitely make this again.
Delicious!  Topped with a little dollop of plain fat free yogurt for additional layer of flavor.
I wanted to love this but found it inedible.
@SL why was it inedible?
What didn't you like?  The mix of flavors or just one flavor?  Or did you have a harissa that was too hot?  If you can explain, it would help a lot of readers.

Flavours are excellent. Delicious.
Instructions on timing were way off for my oven. I used curly kale, done in 5 minutes at 400. Only 1/4 c water in the pan. I added the onion to the marinating shrimp. (Marinated ~1/2 hour)Then scattered the shrimp and onions over top of  the kale and convection roasted rather than broiled it for a further 6-7 minutes until done. The kale would have been badly burned otherwise. Served over pearl couscous.
Wouldn’t the citrus start to cook the shrimp if you marinate for twelve  hours?  Also, 10 minutes to cook shrimp seems excessive!

@Jenny B, yes the marinade will have an affect on “cerviche-ing” the shrimp. I guess it’s a matter of taste and the experience you’re looking to have.  You could completely cerviche the shrimp (eg let it marinade for longer) and then add it on top of the greens after they are cooked - at the same time you add the avocado.  This could make for a refreshing summer-like salad.  Perhaps not the original vision, but could be delicious.  As for length of broiling time, I’m sure the suggested time is just that because it’ll be a mix of your oven, size of shrimp, time of marinading etc.  An eagle eye for doneness is likely helpful.
@Jenny B marinate with everything BUT the lime if you are going to let sit for a long period. Then I squeeze the lime directly before roasting. I use frozen shrimp so I would never just ceviche.
Shrimp cook under a broiler in 3-5 minutes depending on size. Ten minutes is way too long.

Liked the last idea
Wonder what chicken thigh cubes would do to this dish … or roasted eggplant bites ?
@Robert LaRoche: I have family members who suffer from gout, so an alternative to shellfish is welcomed!
Robert LaRoche - This other recipe is similar with chicken thighs: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Potatoes, Arugula and Garlic Yogurt. It's one of my favorite NYT recipes.
@Robert LaRoche, if you’d like to keep the delicacy of seafood you can use cubed salmon: cut skinless salmon fillets into 1 inch cubes and marinade.

Thank you so much to everyone who offered tips on here! So helpful. This recipe called to me but I was unsure how the final product would turn out. It was delicious and unique! Absolutely separate the greens and the shrimp. Marinated overnight as well personally. My tips :: 1. Roast kale with onion (raw onion no thank you) at 400 for about 12 minutes. Toss in the middle and then transfer into the serving bowl once done. 2. On the same pan, do the shrimp under the broiler three minutes per side. 3. Serve over quinoa! Quinoa with seafood is so yum. Didn't have time to grab cilantro so I used mint in a pinch. And absolutely feta and salted avocado. My only thing was the dish cooled down quite quickly so it wasn't really hot ... But that's not too big of a deal. My cousin was saying it would be yummy cold and then I started fantasizing about it as a beach lunch for the summer! A keeper, I'd say. Thank you Yewande for another delish recipe! One of the best chefs of NYT Cooking undoubtedly. xo

Delicious!! I really enjoyed the flavours and it’s totally different from anything I’ve made before. 

I found my chard cooked more quickly than listed, so keep an eye on it. I wasn’t sure if I should low broil or high broil- I believe high broil after making it. 

My harissa was quite hot, so a dollop of Greek yogurt was so good and cooled it down! Also served with pearl couscous, yum!

I am wondering what country this is inspired by? Love the flavours!

Harissa is common to the African side of the Med, particularly Tunisia. I used to make it but sold my regular 60cm oven, replaced by an air fryer - more suitable for someone flying solo. Now I have tubes of concentrated harissa paste. I'll have a go at making a harissa salsa with it and passata. Note that if you use harissa paste concentrate it is searingly hot.
From a gulf coast native: please don’t broil shrimp 3” from the elements for 8-10 minutes!! 4-5 minutes is plenty. Otherwise, a nice, quick and healthy meal.
Can i use dry harissa spice in place of the paste? Should i mix it with oil to coat the shrimp?
@Judi you can mix dry harissa with a little lemon zest and olive oil to make a paste and then use that.
@Melissa thank you!!
Take care if you use harissa paste concentrate [comes in a tube, like tomato concentrate] Searingly hot.

I went totally off piste. Stove top instead of oven, spinach instead of kale or collards, but marinade exactly from recipe for about 4 hours. 

Sautéed 1/2 the onion, then added marinated shrimp and cooked till almost done, then spinach, finished with more red onion, avocado and feta as per recipe. Absolutely a five star dinner!
Would I be able to use spinach in place of the kale? Has anyone tried that?
@Tracy MC I think spinach would cook too quickly and turn bitter.
If you are looking for an excellent, authentic harissa (with delightful variations available), check out your own NY small business offerings — NY Shuk.

I put the kale and shrimp on two separate pans. I roasted the kale on the top rack 10 min, according to the directions. Then I put the kale on the bottom rack, and shrimp on the top rack to broil. I broiled the shrimp on low for 3 min, then turned it up to high for a min and a half. I took the shrimp out, and broiled the kale on low for about 30 seconds, which browned it a little. If I followed this as written, I think the whole dish would have turned to ash.

-Wanted more kale. Would use 12 oz next time. Toss halfway through, it shrinks down quickly. 

-Not a huge fan of raw red onion, though I see it’s place here. Tossed it on the pan along w the shrimp for a 4 min broil. Mellowed the bite, but still had a bit of crunch. Would double. 

-The feta threw me. It seemed out of place. I was wrong!  If you can find sheep or goat milk feta in a block in the brine, it is a creamy, tangy, salty, flavor bomb and it makes the dish.

-Cilantro yes. Avocado optional, IMO. 
-Served over quinoa.

For those commenting that harissa is too hot, there are varying degrees of heat depending on the brand.  A novel idea is to actually taste before adding to your dish so you can adjust. I always taste a new ingredient before using.
Harissa, a seasoning that is currently taking over the palettes of restaurant offerings everywhere. It's not the be all and end all of tastes.
The kale will definitely burn. I made mine separately for just ten minutes, top rack, 400.  Then I used the same pan for the shrimp.
I really should have known better.  Shrimp broiled for 8-10?  I had over cooked shrimp in about 2 min.
I used gojuchang and it was perfect. Agree that the shrimp is partly cooked by lime juice marinade so only a few minutes needed in broiler. Love Yewanda Komolafe recipes!

This is AMAZING, easy & quick!  We used 2-3x the amount of kale and it still didn’t overwhelm shrimp. Mixed onions in to cook w/kale Didn’t  salt kale much b/c feta and shrimp marinade are salty. Did squeeze some lime on kale as an add b/f cooking. We didn’t have harissa so used jarred chili garlic sauce stuff that was in refrig.  Watch kale closely under broiler! We removed from sheet pan when done (the times in this recipe are much too long IMHO) and then cooked shrimp alone for 6-7 mins under broiler. Mixed kale and shrimp together. Served over brown rice. Didn’t want this meal to end! Garnishes, I.e. the feta, avocado, cilantro & red onion (if you like it raw) all serve to make this dish.  Just make it.
I don’t get why you would do this on a sheet pan. I pan fried the shrimp and kale separately and it was brilliant.

Like others, I was leery of the broiling process so I kept the oven on bake mode after adding the shrimp and all turned out great. No burnt chard or overcooked shrimp after 10 minutes at 400. I served it over quinoa, which soaked up the juices nicely.

OMG! This was fabulous. I'm sorry I only made it with 1# of shrimp! I only had harissa sauce and forgot to add the water to the pan with the kale. It was a delicious meal in spite of me with very little effort. Yum, yum.
Never would have put these ingredients together, or cooked using this method, but it worked very well and was delicious. Served over middle-eastern couscous.

Meh, I found this recipe to be just okay. I ate it without much complaint, but I didn't find it "craveable." I think my biggest frustration is with the cooking method. Typically I turn to sheet pan meals because they're nearly hands off, but this meal had to be checked on every five minutes. It was more work than I anticipated.
Phenomenal.  Do not change a thing about this recipe.  Dear Lord, I’m still in afterglow.

I live in an apartment with a 3/4 sized oven and no broiler; so, I've become proficient at workarounds. I kept all the ingredients but cooked them differently. First, I marinated the shrimp for a couple hours. I sautéed chard stems and red onion with black pepper for 7-8 minutes and then added the chard leaves. I added about 1/2 cup of hot water and a lid to hurry the leaf wilting process. Added the shrimp and marinade; when it was time to flip the shrimp, I added cilantro and feta. Served avocado at the table. Delicious.
Has anyone tried this with chicken?

I made this without the harissa (spice) for my toddler and husband and it was a major hit! I even cheated and used garlic powder which I wouldn’t normally do but it was great. Chopped kale and subbed in lemon and dill. Definitely a new staple, it’s surprisingly rich.

I thought this was nice - used Brocolini, squash and carrot instead of Kale which is a devil green not to be eaten ever in my house!  Needed another 5 mins though to cook the veggies.  Not sure the avocado adds much though….

I loved this: the flavors of the marinade are wonderfully bold, and then the feta and onion add just the right notes to give it complexity. I only broiled for 5 minutes and still the greens were way over-cooked, so the next time (and there will be many next times!) I'll try roasting them just for 5-6 minutes and see if that keeps them from getting completely toasted. (The kale was still good crispy and blackened, but I'd like to have some of the leafiness retained.)

This turned out good, but I cooked the greens and the shrimp for less than HALF the time written. If I'd followed the recipe, I would've had a sheet pan full of charcoal. Keep an eye on your ovens, folks.
I agree with other comments, the kale was done before the shrimp but otherwise was great!  I marinated the shrimp in the morning and it had wonderful flavor.

I was nervous about this with the broiling time and worried about making the greens too crisp but it turned out excellent! After adding the shrimp I did 5 minutes at 400 (no broiler) and then another 5 with the broiler on. Everything came out perfect and I can’t wait to make this again!
So delicious and easy!  The kale does tend to get overdone or burned, however we covered with tin foil while broiling the shrimp on high broiling, covering it prevented from getting too crispy.

I made this using 1/2 the directed harissa paste, in case it would end up too spicy, and combined with a dollop of tomato paste; more Swiss chard, and added the red onions at the beginning with the chard. Otherwise followed recipe exactly. And it was…. Meh. I make a lot of NYTimes recipes, love many, but won’t bother with this one again.
This was excellent. I got the mild harissa, but next time will get medium, as I like it a little spicier.  Will definitely put it on the rotation.

I loved this. I made it with spinach and would double the amount of greens next time.
Also, I served it on turmeric pearl couscous and that was yummy. 
Very tasty dish!
Very, very interesting combination of nice ingredients.  A lot of flavor, however, shrimp are overwhelmed by the harissa in my opinion.  I'm going to try it next time with less spice.

Absolutely amazing. Was wondering how the feta and avocado would work - seemed like gilding the lily- but everything melded into an incredible texture. So much umami. Don't skip the lime squeeze when serving. That additional citrus adds the final brightness. Gorgeous dish !
Loved it, but will use 1/2 as much Harissa next time. It was too spicy.
This dish was super spicy (I used Trader Joe's harissa paste). Just FYI, you might want to cut back on harissa depending on what kind you use and your tastebuds!
@Laurel I agree that TJ’s harissa is VERY fiery, and I like very hot. A little goes a long way!

it's fine. 
* the harissa completely overpowered the shrimp flavor. May as well have used tofu. It would be helpful to have a number for the shrimp size (4/6? 8/12?), because I think I should have used a much, MUCH larger one to get the result I had in my imagination.
* the greens got quite crisped in the time listed.
This has absolutely no business being so delicious.

The flavors in this were delicious! The kale was a bit undercooked and tough, though, probably because we had to remove it from the oven while the shrimp cooked, because it was beginning to burn under the broiler. We'd make this recipe again, but we'd try it with chard next time.
Lovely! Will make again. May omit the feta next time, however. It's a nice addition, and delicious with, but perhaps not essential.
Easy and delicious

What a great meal but get ready for spicey! I used Gouchujang instead of harissa. Worked great. Not sure if cutting it back a bit would reduce spiciness or not. Served over basmati rice.
Agree with other comments to really watch the broil. Use low vs high to avoid the risk of burning the kale. 
Also suggest cutting the red onions back unless you are a real fan of them. 
I think next time I make this I will add chickpeas during the broiling phase.  Might be able to sub that for the rice.
We don’t use the word ‘broil’ in Australia so I googled what it meant - I think it means grilled?(but must admit am a bit confused as have seen Americans refer to grilling things also…)

@Anna in Sydney most American ovens if not all, have a setting called “broil” where the top oven element gets very hot and the bottom element stays off. It produces results similar to grilling, but there is no char marks. on the  food as it does not sit directly on a hot metal grill, but rather in a pan. with heat coming from above. Hope that makes sense.

@fauxnomen I am German and have lived in the US, UK and am now in South Africa, so am familiar with all the different measurements, temps and food terms, when I moved to NYC in the 90s I had no idea what cilantro is or what broiling actually means. Most ovens in the UK, Australia and South Africa have the exact same ‘Broil’ function too, it is just called ‘Grill’, and on my Smug the setting is a squiggly line at the top of the square icon.
@Anna in Sydney what we call Grill, the Americans call Broil. When they say grill, we say bbq. So use the grill setting in your oven for this
For gout, 2 Black Cherry Extract, 2800mg daily, has made it possible for my husband to eat shellfish again. Just not every day.
I couldn't find harissa and I subbed with Korean gochujang paste. Turned out wonderfully.

Delicious and love how easy and fast it was to make during the week.  I had shredded green cabbage so added that with the kale.  The spice and flavor was perfect...teenage kids loved it too!  Will definitely be making this again soon.

Followed the recipe faithfully.
Not your typical all in one sheet pan simple meal, but close.  My Harissa paste was a bit spicy, not bad for me but my partner thought it was a bit spicy.  I let it marinate for 3 hours.  When I do this again I’ll add the red onions to the kale during cooking, raw onions are not my fave. I would also use more feta.  
Overall it was great.

Three inches from the top of the oven is way to close.  The kale was burning after just five minutes.  Then, after the shrimp was added and bake was switched to broil, the kitchen smoked up after just a few minutes, resulting in smoke alarms going off.  And I agree, just a few minutes under the broiler was enough to cook the shrimp.  We ate the result, but the kale was burned.
Cut harissa by 1/3 to 1/2, so say the comments.

We loved this dish! I used mild harissa, which worked just fine. Raw red onions don't agree with us so we used pickled red onions, which really were marvelous with the complex flavors of this recipe. A keeper! I definitely could see chicken thighs working in place of the shrimp.
This was just ridiculously easy and good. Our broiler is a nightmare, so I simply set the convection oven to 400. Worked perfectly!

We did not like this recipe.  The kale burned under the broiler, and the cooking time on the shrimp was too long.  If I were to do this again, I would not broil either the kale or the shrimp, but I would instead bake it at 425.  The harissa mixture over the shrimp was quite good.  I think yogurt would have been better than feta.

Only cook the shrimp for one minute each side to avoid them from getting tough. Also I used a combo of kale and red Swiss chard. Delicious! My husband said next time serve with a tortilla!  He loved this dish!!
Interesting flavors and a fun way to cook the chard but so easy to overcook the shrimp.
At a glance, this sounds like it would make an excellent, unconventional taco filling, garnishes included
Used a mild harissa, brand Mina, and it was not too spicy for the kids!
Why the water in the pan? It made the kale mushy instead of crisp and the shrimp watery and mushy. I only added 1/4 cup. Next time I will skip the water.
When I moved the kale to 1 side, I soaked up the excess water with a paper towel.

I loved the shrimp part of this dish, although I would recommend less harissa and to pass on the broiling.  The kale caught on fire during the broiling, but the part that I could salvage was quite good. The leftover shrimp was delicious, but not the kale.

Delicious. Did not broil anything. Convection roasted greens at 375- they became crispy.  Turned up to convection roasted shrimp @400 degrees x4 mins. Did not have feta. Shrimp a tad overdone. Used sour cream to tone down heat. Flavor was delicious. Added some onions to marinade and garnished with cilantro and remaining fresh onions. No avocado bc didn’t have any.Would def make again.
In step 5 are people adding just the shrimp or the marinade also?
I did not add the marinade as I thought it would not let the shrimp get brown edges.
So so so good! Don’t worry about doing shrimp and kale on one pan, as other commenters have. Ten minutes for kale and 4-5 for shrimp and all will be well!
Should have know not to broil shrimp for so long. By the time 6 minutes came along, the shrimp was overdone and smoke alarm was blaring. Kale burnt to a crisp.
First try stems of the kalr were to hard and broiling the dish on high burned the leaves. Turns out better if removing stems and broiling on low.

Made this tonight and completely burned the kale in the first round. If your oven has a low and high broil setting, definitely use the low setting. Thank goodness I had an extra bag of kale in the fridge and started the kale from scratch again. After new kale cooked, this was amazing!! Used pickled red onions, for a slightly softer flavor but otherwise added all the accoutrements called for an it was delicious!! Will absolutely make again.
Does the harissa overpower the flavor of the shrimp? Would love to try but shrimp are expensive!
Has anyone tried with any other protein (no shrimp)?—I would love suggestions!
Nice but not great. And yucky cilantro ruins it.
For those concerned about overcooking the greens in the top rack of the oven,  the recipe says to roast them at 400°, not broil them.
Do you think it would work if you replaced the shrimp with cod or salmon?

I thought this was pretty good—shared it with my teenaged son who had never eaten shrimp before! (His mother is allergic, but is out of town at the moment!). I agree with the broiler comment. I did the first half of the broil on high and then switched to low when I rotated the pan and it ended up being delicious!
The shrimp were awesome, but even better without the feta cheese which tended to dominate the shrimp and it’s seasoning

we don’t know how they got 15 minutes of prep time because it took us an hour
For the amount of work involved, it was not worth it so I gave it a three out of five
This is exceptional - I roasted the onions with Lacinato Kale and omitted avocado (we were out). Otherwise, cooked as directed and couldn't be happier. Will definitely be making this again!
This was terrific!  Except I didn't have harissa, so I made a paste of chili power and marinated Trader Joe's red shrimp for several hours, otherwise followed the recipe exactly.  Delicious!

The flavors were delicious.  I used 7 ounces of blanched and stemmed collard greens, I didn’t have feta and did not use a sub.  I served this on red lentils. I Mixed have off the chopped onion into the greens before baking.

I used a homemade harissa paste using a recipe from Milk Street that had sundried tomatoes and roasted red peppers in it.  I think I would soak the onions in an acid for 5-10 minutes to soften their bite and layer in the toppings 1/2 at a time for appearance.  All in all, it was quite good.

The flavors in this dish are incredible-  I'm not sure I've ever stuffed shrimp into my mouth so quickly! The toppings give this a distinctly latin vibe, with the harissa providing a nice base layer of warmth. Also drizzled some Kefir. Yum! 

I was wary of burning the chard, so I placed the shrimp in the middle of the pan and the greens off to the side. I ended up slightly overcooking the shrimp and the greens never browned, but mixed all together with the toppings it was still delicious.

A great dinner which can be fast unless you make your own harissa like I did. It’s worth it but it would be best to make it the morning of.  We used Kale and overcooked it a bit (we like it well done) and had some fresh Maui fillets so cut them into chunks instead of defrosting shrimp. With the 40 min marinade in the harissa mix it was really good! Used avocado & chopped onion for toppings- husband added some hot sauce as I made the harissa @ medium heat. Yum.
Can Harissa sauce be used instead of harissa paste?
How little faith but needed to use some dinosaur kale - it was amazing!  Never would have expected it to be this delicious.  And really - very easy.

I guess we don't have a great broiler- this ended up so... wet? I poured off water went I added the shrimp, and still although the chard crisped the shrimp were in a lake of harissa water, whisking away all the flavor. Bummer.

Picked this recipe to make because I had some harissa on hand to use and we like all the components. Used kale and served over white rice. It’s a winner. I have a couple of suggestion to the instructions - use the low setting on your broiler as others suggested. I didn’t have jumbo shrimp, so they needed less cooking time. Enjoy!
Loved this recipe, it was excellent! We served it over cous cous and it was 🤌🏼 The only thing I’d change is I’d use more Swiss chard. It really cooks down.

Yummm this was so good!! I added a little honey to my greens and shrimp, and I subbed paprika and chili powder because I didn’t have harissa. I also added some purple cabbage to my greens because I needed to use it up. Ate with sweet potato tortilla chips, so so good! Thanks for the recipe!

This was delicious. Such an unexpected mixture of flavors and textures that led to an exciting dinner. When I make again, I will increase the red onion and feta as they added a lot of dynamic to the meal.

Loved this recipe, especially the slight toasting of the chard. I did this in a steam oven, so if anyone is using one, I used the convection humid setting at 375F, no need for water in the pan with the chard. Fantastic flavours, but my harissa was a little too hot so I would maybe cut it back by a third. Matches perfectly with couscous.
The flavors in this are great.  I found it a little dry. Any suggestions for next time?
A hit. Maybe a bit lighter on the harissa and add some tzaziki or green yogurt to cut back on the dryness of the couscous
A new favorite. Love getting all my favorites from NYT Cooking. Made it with chard. Simply exquisite.
Definitely don’t put the greens too close to the broiler like the recipe says — they literally caught on fire!
A winner!
Made it, liked it - wish it was more saucy. Ideas?

I was preparing to make this when I noted the clearly excessive cooking time for shrimp under a broiler. Then another red flag appeared - marinating shrimp in a lime marinade for hours or even overnight? How can one trust a recipe like this.

Someone else noted the issue with kale - my broiler crisped it almost to inedibility.  I’d modify by baking at 400 for 5 minutes, then moving to the “add shrimp and broil”, and maybe 3 minutes under the broiler, instead of 5-8.

Shoulda known.  But.  I guess charcoal is good for the digestion :)
oh this is good! simple but yummy! I bought TERRIBLE shrimp and it was still good!

So good! We loved the flavors & textures!! Prepared as written except for halving the lime juice & harissa (I added more of both to my plate). No problem with the kale & shrimp turning both 2-3x during the broil. Served with Israeli couscous and, for me, a dollop of plain Greek yogurt. Def on the “let’s do THIS again!” list!
Great recipe! Did not change anything and it was perfect.
Ten minutes for shrimp under a broiler is way too long. Mine turned out rubbery and dried out. Definitely check them sooner, maybe after 3-5 minutes.
very good- but agree cooking time for shrimp is way off.  I cut it down to 3 min per side and still they were overcooked.

This was so interesting and delicious. I really didn't know what to expect because the combination was unusual, but it was amazing. Unfortunately, I could not find Harissa paste, so i used the Harissa sauce that I had and it was still great. I love most of Yewande's recipes and that's why I tried it.

Yikes, kale was burnt and smoking by the time the shrimp was half done, even though it was not directly under the broiler. Next time I’ll use separate pans or remove the kale and add it back just before the shrimp is done. Otherwise was good. Made just for my wife and I…leftovers made yummy harissa shrimp tacos the next night.

A ZERO FOR ME.used a Harissa sauce vs paste as paste seemed so thick. Still added some oil..Did not use a broiling pan and thus shrimp sat in liquid they gave off - started by conv/roast and not enough to crisp them - don't think enough oil added to get any crisp frankly.  Kale will char at 400 even with 1/4c water...MUST to mix it around.  clearly something did not work for me.  Simple grilled shrimp with harissa from Food52 I think was so much better...but that was grilled....

The kale was over cooked, so next time I make this I will start at half of the time. 

But I definitely will be making this recipe again!
Can anyone share a good harrisa recipe?  I love the Cava prepared harissa but it is expensive.

This sounds good, but I mean, sheet pan doesn't always mean easier. This would be so much easier in a frying pan instead of going through all the sheet pan gyrations. Yes, you wouldn't necessarily get the browning from broiling, but you could sear the shrimp first to get your Maillard reaction, remove the shrimp, then cook the greens, then warm the shrimp through. Not to mention having the ability to season the greens with some alliums.
Made this with chunks of halibut instead of shrimp and it was great! Loved the mix of flavors. Will definitely make again.

Very tasty. I was worried about the harissa so cut it in half, but would use all of it next time. Take the note to salt sparingly seriously - I definitely put too much on. 

I suspect many of the people complaining about overcooking the kale haven’t read the recipe properly - make sure to use the water and to cook it for the first ten minutes as described, not under the grill/broiler the whole time.
IF using harissa sauce, use less olive oil.  Harissa sauce=harissa paste plus oil
Absolutely delicious!!!!! Just made the recipe step by step. Yes watch over the kale under broiler. OMG…excellent recipe.
Made this tonight at 1/2 scale for 2 and it was a winner! Served over rice pilaf. Bought the Harissa Paste and now will look for more recipes using it. Thanks for a good meal!

I put a sheet pan of sliced onion (olive-oiled and salted) in with the pan of kale and let them do their thing. Instead of the broiling step, I increased the oven to 425, added the marinated shrimp to the onion and roasted to perfection. Leftovers the next day were fantastic scooped up with a piece of toasted sourdough. Surprisingly, the leftover kale kept its crunch despite being stored in with the shrimp and onions.
Broiled for 4 minutes at 500F (high) with rack at 6" to cook shrimp and 1-2 minutes at the higher level to brown the shrimp and not burn the kale. Served on rice.  Delish!

This had great flavor! I agree with decreasing the broil time for the shrimp. I used rainbow chard for my greens and it was way too watery with the added water to the pan. Next time I will omit the water altogether, as the oil provided enough moisture. It did not need rice or couscous, as it made a fantastic warm salad. Thanks for the recipe :)
We subbed in chicken thighs for shrimp (30 min bake time) and it was absolutely delicious. Will enter our regular rotation!
I love this combo, so healthy!

This recipe is fantastic, easy to make and filled with flavor. I used Mustard Greens instead, substituted fresh mozzarella cheese for the Feta and white onion instead of the red onion and it was great. I also marinated the shrimp in the harissa mix with all of the 6 tablespoons of Olive Oil overnight then used the marinade to massage and roast the Mustard Greens with half of the White Onions. I would suggest just broiling the marinaded shrimp for about 4 to 5 minutes instead of the 8-10 minutes.

Took the ideas from the excellent notes.  Thanks all. 
 Loved the dish but will use hot Harissa next time! I only had medium at home.
Excellent as written.  I served over polenta.

We loved this!  It’s so perfectly balanced with textures, flavors, and colors. The crunchy greens, creamy avocado, spicy shrimp, and tangy cheese. We substituted broccoli greens from our garden instead of kale and it was perfect!
I have not been able to find harissa paste, I have harissa sauce, so I've just used a little bit more. I love this recipe, it's delicious and everyone I have served it to loves it.

I recently purchased a new jar of harissa for another recipe and came upon this dish as another great use for the harissa. It is colorful, showy and delicious. I used baby kale, tossed it with olive oil, and used it as a bed for the shrimp and broiled together— maybe 6-7 minutes, not right under the broiler.

Delicious. Husband and young adult kids loved it. My only deviation was that I did not broil the kale. I took it off the pan before broiling the shrimp so that the kale wouldn’t get too crispy. I served over a bed of basmati rice flavored with coconut milk, onion and garlic powder, lime juice, salt, and pepper.

A great recipe if you know how fast your oven’s broiler cooks. I didn’t realize how quickly the kale would cook- should have taken it out after the first 10 minutes in the oven. It was burning in about one minute under the broiler; my extra large “jumbo” shrimp were done in 5 minutes. I didn’t have feta and used castelvetrano olives for a pop of salt - worked great. I recommend it if you don’t eat dairy or don’t have the feta and don’t want to make another trip to the store. I’ll make this again when I have feta now that I have the timing dialed in.
Doubled the recipe and did shrimp on greens on separate pans, mixed together at end.  So greens were never broiled and remained soft and green.

Good flavors, healthy and easy.  My picky teenager and husband both liked it.  Instead of kale, I mixed spinach with olive oil, salt and pepper, and placed it on top of the shrimp after the shrimp came out of the oven so the spinach wilted a little, then put the spinach and shrimp on top of couscous.  Used Mina Harissa sauce instead of paste.  Cooked large shrimp for 8 to 10 min in 425 oven instead of broil.  Followed recipe for rest.  Don't skimp on avocado, lime and feta to cut the heat!

The flavors were delicious. Next time I will cook the chard until its finished, remove it from the sheet then broil the shrimp separately. I would toss them together at the end. It wouldn't add much time to do this and you wouldn't get overcooked chard.

Delicious and super easy to keep all the ingredients on hand (well, so maybe you have to go buy some kale.) While I followed the recipe to a T the first time I made this, as with almost all NYT recipes, there simply wasn't enough spice.  Next time through I doubled everything spicy - garlic, harissa, etc and it was phenomenal!!!

Made this for the first time tonight and will definitely make again. It was delicious! The only adjustment I made was adding some bokchoy with the kale since I needed to use it up. Such an easy recipe and healthy too!! The toppings are a must. Don't get rid of any of them.
Reminds me of the harissa avocado bowl at Cava. Yum!

This was delicious! I made my own harissa paste but otherwise all else remained the same.   My husband ate it over couscous and I, over red lentils. We had it with roasted butternut squash. The whole dish was quite a bite. Excellent recipe.
Made it without the feta and it was delicious over couscous. Just keep an eye on kale so it doesn’t char

I forgot the zest the first time and didn’t care much for the recipe. I made it again since I had the ingredients and noticed the instruction to include the zest, and that made a huge difference. Would recommend trying a couple of times and make sure the flavors are balancing out! Really enjoyed this the second time around. Also agree with some of the comments about the cook time.
I used gochujang instead of harissa and it was incredible!
use chicken thighs, then grill them before adding to veggies
Can you double this dish and cook it on two different pans?

Excellent flavors, though 10 minutes is way too long to broil shrimp. I had pretty large shrimp, and they were slightly overcooked at 6 minutes. Only other change I made was using baby spinach instead of kale. I just tossed it with the shrimp after taking it out of the oven, and the residual heat was enough to wilt it.

We thought this was wonderful. Fast and easy cleanup. Yum! One could easily make it into a stew by saving the marinade and adding a little broth, simmer and then add the cooked shrimp and greens. Nice with a fresh sourdough.
Not very good.  Sauce tasty but watery. Probably use sauce from ravioli next time.

It was a little spicy as I used spicy Harissa paste, but overall so delicious. Could definitely use extra kale since it shrinks to nothing. Also, next time I would cook the kale less at the beginning because it started to get overcooked (black) during the broiling and I had to take it off the cookie sheet. I will definitely make this again for company that likes spicy, raw onions and cilantro
I normally broil shrimp 6” from broiler, and moved my pan from 3 to 6” halfway through as it had started to burn. May try again at 6” the whole time.

This was very tasty but had far too few greens. I am cooking in what I consider camp situation, poorly stocked grocery so had to make do with Earthbound Power Greens, a mix of baby kale, spinach, and chard—that worked, but I doubled the quantity called for, and we definitely wanted more. I forgot the water but that did not matter. I served it over quinoa, and used honey instead of sugar, as others had suggested. Very good. I will definitely be making this again, especially now that my CSA is delivering lots of greens.

BTW, I used the recalibration tool as I am cooking for just two. I live that feature. Thanks NYTC!

Yum! Made for 2 people, cut harissa down after taste test. Marinated shrimp a few hours. Did in wok with spinach. Wilt spinach, then add shrimp. Served over quinoa with avocado, raw red onion, feta, and amaranth microgreens for color. Definite keeper.

My harissa does not "deepen in flavor once it’s kissed by the heat of a broiler," as the notes suggest. It is HOT. Knowing that my harissa is hotter than many, I reduced the amount called for in the recipe by 25%. The meal was still barely edible, nearly ruined by unbearable heat. I may well make this again -- though I was dubious of the method of making broiled kale, we liked the result -- but if I do, I will use a quarter of the harissa called for. I served it over orzo and would do that again

The cooking time for the shrimp is much too long. I roasted the shrimp (not broiled) for 5 minutes. Also, I used 1 TBSP DEA-brand harissa paste. 1/4 cup seems excessive, but it depends on the brand's heat. On making the dish a second time, doubled the swiss chard, swapped in caramelized onions for the raw red onions, and added chopped dates. The dates and onions went on top of the swiss chard in the oven at the same time as the shrimp. This version gets raves, and it makes terrific tacos!

WeLaJo (who posted 7 months prior to my comment) has the right idea. I followed the same process on the stove top, in a large skillet, and it turned out fine. The longest cooking time was the onions - the shrimp were done in about 3 minutes as they always do - and I also wilted spinach because I'm not a big kale fan. So, it comes together in a flash - easy cleanup - this is the way to go. Thanks, WeLaJo.
My first time cooking with harissa, will not be the last. I used frozen shrimp we keep on hand, but would use fresh for special occasion. I will try this recipe with tofu.

title: Shai Magnan (Sudanese Burned Milk Tea) Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: You know what has cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, licorice (an anise flavor), and cardamom? A pumpkin spice tea bag. Don't stone me! I put forth this suggestion for anyone else who doesn't have beautiful (and expensive) whole spices in their pantry.  1. Heat up a small amount of milk and molasses or brown sugar 2.  Seep pumpkin spice black tea bag in water (as usual) 3. combine + enjoy.
@Hacky McHackface Nice suggestion. You could also use mulling spices if you have some on hand. Just have to add a little cardamom.

@Hacky McHackface Going to have to deeply disagree with this. For starters, the recipe doesn’t call for cinnamon, and that’s only the beginning of the difference between chai spice and pumpkin spice. If you want a cost-effective alternative that more closely hews to this recipe, Trader Joe’s has some excellent spiced chai tea bags. Subbing in pumpkin spice makes this a completely different drink - still a delicious drink, but nothing close to the Sudanese drink that’s described in this recipe.
Can we have the original, genuine article, please?  How do you really make this tea with the burnished milk?  I am not convinced by the molasses.  Thank you.
I'd suggest to brew the tea separately in water. 1 minute is not long enough! and then to add cold milk. no no!
It's not going to taste the same if you don't brew the tea in milk.
even the intro says that traditionally the tea is brewed in water @Afi

Made it as written and it just tasted of molasses - which I do enjoy, but don't want to drink. I had to add more milk, tea and spices to make it drinkable. I'd like a recipe with the milk being cooked down instead of using molasses as a colorant. If you do decide to make this, I recommend using the brown sugar instead of the molasses - or at least half and half. I think the brown sugar would get you closer to the flavor of the caramelized milk.
In Oman thé fabulous KARAK tea is almost the same: a pure addiction! When going back home (France) none of my various attempts to recreate the amazing taste was successful.

I just read the article and then made it. It was heavenly and so comforting. A thicker, warmer, soothing type of drink. It has the body of a good hot chocolate and would be perfect as a morning beverage. I made a half portion and reduced the molasses a tad bc I don’t adore sugar and the flavors were incredible! Thanks for introducing me to this tradition!
RS - re your note " It was heavenly and so comforting.", this was a hit at my house too.

I don't think the shortcut and the classic will taste the same.
