And I don't see how pouring the tea into the reduced, browned milk gently, as the intro says, should involve spattering, if done gently enough, from a spout.

Does milk really turn brown when reduced?
@Ashok think “Dulce de leche,” it’s the sugars that brown.
@harriseve, milk contains lactose, a form of sugar to judge by the "ose" suffix a la sucrose and fructose.
Yeah I’m guessing you heat the last bit of milk before adding it.

Also: Instead of a sieve I used a French press, which makes for far easier drinking as, depending on the type of loose leaf tea you use, you’ll have a lot of “debris” (for lack of a better word) after boiling everything.
@TJ Good idea, because I made a mess pouring this through a sieve.

You know what has cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, licorice (an anise flavor), and cardamom? A pumpkin spice tea bag. Don't stone me! I put forth this suggestion for anyone else who doesn't have beautiful (and expensive) whole spices in their pantry. 1. Heat up a small amount of milk and molasses or brown sugar 2. Seep pumpkin spice black tea bag in water (as usual) 3. combine + enjoy.

I would suggest a Chai tea bag rather than pumpkin spice if you cannot use loose. Also, only a few makers do not use plastic in making their bags, and never buy the tea in nylon pyramid shaped bags.  You can look up the extent of microplastics in almost everything we eat.

This is a rich, delicious, well rounded cup of tea. Brewing the tea in the milk and simmering the milk for so long adds body, and reduces the tannic flavor. I did half maple syrup and half light brown sugar because that's what I had, and it turned out great. To deepen the flavor, I first reduced the maple syrup, and added brown sugar and let that slightly caramelize. Then I added in a little bit of milk first to dissolve the sugars, then added in the rest of the milk and followed as is.

I agree with @R. This is a delicious cup of tea! I admit I also tweaked the recipe a bit based on ingredients I had on hand and to avoid the molasses overpowering the flavor. I used a 2:1 ratio of maple syrup to molasses. I was out of cinnamon sticks so I threw in ground cinnamon (same amount as the ginger) and used freshly grated ginger instead of ground ginger. I will make it again once I get cinnamon sticks!

I scaled this back by 50% for 2 servings and made it exactly as the recipe was written choosing molasses for the sweetener option in the "either / or", whole milk (cow dairy) and Smith Teamaker's Kandy black tea for tea leaves.  It was delicious.  Or as my husband said as he took his first sip, "This is full of flavor!"  High praise from a guy who is not a fan of milk in general and never wants it with afternoon tea.  This spiced milk with tea steeped in hit the spot.   A keeper.

I really enjoyed this tea and it was new to me. I used 2% which split (I think from ginger?) but came back together when I added the other 2cups. I’m also wondering if those who aren’t tasting it aren’t using the right tea? I used the CTC (?) black tea from Patel Bros used in cha - it’s stronger and comes through beautifully (and in fact 4T might be too much of it) so recommend that tea if anyone having trouble tasting it. My husband dislikes molasses and even he liked it!

I spent time in Kenya & East Africa in the early 1980s and fell in love with Chai ya tengawizi, which is made very similar to this Sudanese tea. Key being to boil the tea and spices in the milk (no water) and simmer to reduce the milk in order to get that amazing cooked milk flavor.  Never used molasses, but I'll be trying it this weekend. Nzuri sana.
Seems like this recipe would be the place to use dulce de leche, made from sweetened condensed milk.

I, too, was interested in a more authentic recipe and googled "sudanese burnt milk tea" and found one in a reel on Insta by @theirinjournal.  The method is interesting!  And I now see why spattering can result.  All that said, this recipe reminds me more of the chai I make, which involves simmering plenty of whole spices & pepper in water, then adding tea bags.  This way I can retrieve the spices, freeze them in a ziploc bag, then use them again, adding some fresh ones each time.
I did it! But I used the TikTok video in the NYT article where this recipe is originally featured + the measurements from this recipe. It worked well.
@T.   Please share the video link - I’m a NYT subscriber and can’t find it.   The article has no video (that I could find).

Made this exactly as outlined except that I used ground cloves instead of whole ones and slightly less anise as I had only a little bit left in my spice cabinet. The recipe requires patience but the payoff was wonderful and tasted like a milder version of the chai one gets at Indian restaurants. Looking forward to returning to this tea many times and may occasionally sub in fresh ginger and black pepper if I need an extra kick.
Would dulce de leche or caramel work better than molasses as an approximation of sugar and milk cooked down?
@Ashok Milk does turn brown when cooked down enough.  It caramelizes. I'm going to try this recipe using caramelized condensed milk thinned out with regular milk and see how it goes.

I can’t say I enjoyed this tea. The molasses really did not mimic the flavor of  sugar and was nothing like Hong Kong  or Taiwanese milk tea. It’s also very time consuming to make so why not make milk tea? If you love molasses this might make your day however.
I used cardamom tea from Ahmad Tea. I added the tea once the milk boiled. We thoroughly enjoyed this and it's a nice alternative to chai tea.
Would a better way to caramelize the milk be, like pan dulce, heat condensed milk (in pressure cooker or oven or double boiler)?

I made as described but the milk was so thick it barely passed through the fine mesh sieve. Almost the consistency of a milkshake. Any suggestions? I used whole milk and wonder if 2% would be better.  Flavor was fantastic and I’d like to make again!

Cortney - Re your comment " the milk was so thick it barely passed through the fine mesh sieve", I also used whole milk to make this and it was perfectly pourable though the strainer.  I'm wondering if you used too high a simmer setting for the 8 minute step and maybe reduced it too much?

While not authentic to the original drink, I found the addition of molasses to my chai to be a revelation. I heated/frothed milk with a touch of molasses and poured into my brewed black tea chai. Adds an unexpected, warm and comforting flavor.
I found it heavy in every way, especially on cardamom and molasses. Might be delicious for others, but not for me.

For the first time ever, I had all the ingredients on hand for a NYT recipe. I had to make it. It was delicious, rich and spicy. I used molasses. Can’t say I could taste it. Would using evaporated milk work? I now want to know how to make the original recipe.
Sorry, second comment. This is nothing like pumpkin spice. Not a good substitute. If you do like pumpkin spice, make regular chai with it.
Yummy spices and fun cozy drink but could reduce sweetener by half at least, in my opinion.

Is this supposed to be a hot drink?  If I have 2 cups of milk heated, through step 2, and then pour into that 2 cups of cool or even room temperature milk, isn't the result going to be lukewarm at best?  Did I miss an instruction to warm the second 2 cups of milk?
@Bibi My thoughts exactly. Much as I enjoy molasses, I'd prefer to try my hand at the real thing before I start taking shortcuts.
If the original recipe has caramelized milk, and that means the syrup/or sugar is eliminated, then please publish the recipe.  Eliminating added sugars is my preference.

Frank W - if you'd rather have an original (assume you mean traditional) recipe version for this beverage and not the revised version published here, thanks to the convenience of google or any search engine, you can find several traditional iterations on line to try if you choose not to try this one.
@dimmerswitch I did just that and found no recipes that cooked the milk down to caramelize it. And I worded the search several different ways. The NYT recipe came up first lol!
Does "5 star-anise pods, broken into pieces" mean five full stars separated into ~25 composite pods?  OR Five of the composite pods that make up one star??
Quakers - re your confusion for the instruction ""5 star-anise pods, broken into pieces", it means 5 whole star anise (pods), each one broken into pieces.
Love the combo of spices in this! I used whole milk and felt that 4 cups of it led to a dilute flavor. Next time I would use 3 cups of milk, or add more molasses & spice.
I attempted this twice and the milk curdled each time while I was bringing it to temp/simmer. Anyone else have this issue? Milk is fresh!
@SMD yes, happens to me every time I try this recipe.  Am still trying to figure out why.
I was looking for something different from masala Chai, but this tea tasted so  terrible and bitter despite all the molasses. I had to pour it down the drain.

@Hacky McHackface thank youSO much!  I think I MIGHT have one of those spices in my pantry! and almost didnt save the recipe because,  as much as I would like to say "I'll just go buy some!"... that little sentiment isn't in the budget! BUT, pumpki spice tea bags could very well be! thank you again!
chinese 5 spice powder has a lot of these spices - I often use it as a shortcut
Has anyone tried preparing the milk/spice ahead of time and adding plain milk to (a fourth) of the spiced milk to make just one cup at a time?
Could this work with creamy oat milk?

I enjoyed the "cooked milk" flavor of this but one huge problem is straining out tea leaves/spices. Straining didn't work for me and I tried a few different options. I highly recommend leaving tea in teabags (if you use those) or using disposable paper tea filters (if you use loose tea). Otherwise you might find yourself sputtering on the tea leaves (as I did) since I couldn't filter them out!

title: One-Pan Roasted Vegetables and Polenta Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Why are you folks still promoting the use of aluminum foil? The mining of it is even more polluting than what comes after its discarded or “recycled”.(I use recycling bins whenever possible, aware that this is no guarantee that objects will be recycled. When they are, this consumes large amounts of energy, and produces its own pollution. If one isn’t able to invest in covers for oven-able pots, shallow pans turned upside down or cookie sheet-type pans can protect foods from burning on top.
@mary  Thanks! Sheet pan or cookie sheet over iron skillet works great.
@mary I bought some reuseable foil/parchment. Cleans nicely in the dishwasher.
@mary I didn’t know that foil was so polluting but the information makes me glad I have a matching lid for my cast iron skillet. I use it all the time.

I made this last night, and the benefit of how the recipe is written is that the polenta is cooking while you prep the veggies, so it is actually more time efficient to cook as written.  If your goal is to have crispy veggies, then roasting them separately would be more effective.  We really enjoyed the flavours (especially the zing of the lemon) and would have it again with a variety of root veggies plus sautéed greens I would prep while the rest is in the oven.

I’ve been oven-baking or just microwaving polenta for years. So much simpler. If you make it with canned pumpkin, it’s even more autumnal. Agree that I can’t see why you’d bother to mix veggies into polenta. Roasting them away from all that liquid is what gives them their delicious crispness.
@Nan Dem can you share your proportions for adding pumpkin? How much would you add to the 1 cup of corn meal in this recipe? And do you keep water the same? Thanks!

I make baked polenta all the time and I assure you, two cups water to one cup polenta is a recipe for adobe.  I do four cups water to one cup polenta  and it takes at least 40 minutes to “set” enough before adding mix-ins.
@Letizia It’s not. Works just fine.

It seems like it would be more efficient to roast the veg in a separate pan, put the polenta pan in just 15 minutes before the veg are done, pull both out and combine, and then the broil step. For the non-purists, nuking the sweet potatoes to pre-cook before roasting could shave another 10-15 minutes off. But the dish does sound delicious, and I will make it with my shortcuts!
@K Don’t the clumps of polenta spend 40 minutes in the oven with the vegetables the way the recipe is written? Does it work to take that away?

This is quite tasty and an easy prep. I added some cut green beans (because they needed to be used) and some rosemary (why not?). I also added more Brussels than called for and used goat cheese not feta. Thumsb up. My husband and I had it as our supper with nothing else. We ate almost the entire pan. So serving four people might be a little optimistic.

As someone who loves to roast vegetables, I struggled with this recipe's technique. The vegetables steamed under the foil, and when they went under the broiler, the outer edges of the brussels blackened before the shallots or sweet potatoes got any browning at all. I kept my sweet potato chunks small, but with everything being tossed in the polenta, it didn't seem there was enough surface area to achieve browning. Next time I'd roast the veggies separately.

The best recipe of this kind is Martha Rose Shulman's Roasted Root Vegetables with Polenta. The polenta is cooked separately in a 2 quart baking dish and the vegetables are roasted on a sheet pan at the same time time.  The proportions for the polenta are 1 cup polenta to 1 quart (4 cups) water. The result is foolproof decliciousness. If you line the sheet pan with parchment paper, the cleanup is super easy.

I had some brussels sprouts and polenta to use up and this served. I followed the instructions except for using all of a 4oz goat cheese log rather than just a quarter of a cup. I would happily make it again. My husband had seconds he liked it so much.  Don't compare it to roasted brussels sprouts; it is something different and delicious in its own way. I liked that the recipe didn't require a lot of hands-on time other than chopping the vegetables.

This looks wonderful.  Although this says it is a ‘meal’, it seems like it has a lot of carbs relative to the amount of protein.  Maybe have an egg, a small piece of baked salmon, or some chicken on the side (or some other source of protein if you don’t eat animal products).  I appreciate that nutritional info is provided with the recipes.
I was out of cornmeal but used grits, added a few more minutes to the first cook, and wow it was delicious.  This one will be in my rotation.
Hmmm. This just didn't taste right to us. Way too lemony and sour; we were crazing something more savory.
This sounds delicious, but I cook polenta in the microwave. I will cook this but add two cups of white beans to it for more protein while roasting the vegetables in my convection oven.

I made this tonight as written and the only thing that I would change is to reduce the amount of polenta by 50%.  We like our dishes veggie heavy, and I felt like the veggies got lost in the large amount of polenta.  Otherwise, it was very good.

Made exactly as directed with the exception of using dried rubbed sage instead of fresh. 35 minutes in the oven and 6 under the broiler on "low". Absolutely delicious and everything cooked perfectly. If I'm picking nits, it might be just a bit too lemony with the zest and juice of a whole lemon, next time I might try half a lemon and see how it turns out. Four servings might be a bit optimistic, it's more like two servings and a bit left over. If you wanted to keep it vegan you could leave out the goat cheese but it adds a wonderful richness. This one is going in the regular rotation!

Boo. I made this as instructed. It was a waste of good brussel sprouts. The textures were ok, the polenta at the bottom of my iron skillet was brown and chewy. The lemon and sage were undetectable. The brussel sprouts tasted mushy and cabbagey. Not a fan.

This method seems like a lot of extra work for a simple dish. I often make polenta and veggies as a weeknight meal because it's quick. When potatoes are cut small enough, they cook beautifully in a cast iron skillet with a lid, then remove the lid towards the end to crisp everything up. Or roast the potatoes ahead of time in the toaster oven, cut up and add with more delicate vegetables to crisp in minutes in a skillet. I will however try incorporating the lemon into my version.

Delicious. I am not a fan of sweet potatoes, so I substituted diced delicate squash. Otherwise, I made this exactly the way Yewande suggested. It’s delicious. If you’re expecting creamy polenta (and doubling the liquid  as some suggest) you’re fundamentally changing the dish. The polenta is delicious with the 2-to-1 ratio. It leans chewier and absorbs the flavors in the vegetable concoction. Thank you for the lemon, Yewande. I might reduce the sage next time because that is such a distinctive flavor. But this is a delicious recipe as it is written. Try it that way.

I made this tonight and struggled with it. I had to bake the polenta 5 minutes longer than the recipe listed to get it to set and had to cook the vegetables 10 minutes longer for the potatoes to become tender, but even though the potatoes were done, the shallots were still very undercooked. I would definitely recommend cooking the vegetables separately from the polenta. 

The biggest problem for me though was how dry everything was. I'm not sure how I would fix this.
Followed as written and was excellent!

Can do the polenta to point it holds up the beg, then roast them on top, not mixed in, for more roasty without an extra pan. I have also tossed the beg in enchilada sauce for a different twist. Avoocado atop that and you’re sailing.
@Karen thanks, I was wondering and think I will make it this way. Mixing it all together just doesn’t appeal to me.
I found this a little dry. Next time, I would add roasted cherry tomatoes.
Just not the most sensible recipe. Agree with others that the lemon makes it a little sour.

I made this recipe just as written, in a
cast-iron pan, the only difference was using a few extra brussels sprouts and some additional feta and parsley at the end. It was perfect.

I made this recently and honestly, whatever time saving you get from it is negated by it not being very good, both flavor wise, and textural. at 375 for 30-40 minutes, you get soft, wilty brussels and smushy sweet potato, on top of soft polenta.  Soft on soft on soft.  small dice the sweet potato, and roast everything at 450 while you make stovetop polenta (it's not hard) and in the 20 minutes or so you spend stirring your polenta your veggies get roasty.  Skip the aluminum foil or parchment.

great recipe!  The first time i made this i forgot to add the teaspoon of salt to the veggie bowl and it turned out perfect.  i made it again tonight and added the salt where directed and it was too salty.  next time i make it will cut the amount if salt in half and just season to taste after.  the two teaspoons of salt are too much for this dish.

The family enjoyed it despite me being a novice cook.   The vegetables were roasted well enough after a 6 minute broil and the flavour of the scallions and lemon came through nicely.  2 of them didn't care for the Feta so I  kept it to one side of the pan only.  It was a pain to clean up the pan afterwards though.  Rated it a 4 because its so healthy.
I suggest that you spray your pan or coat it with vegetable oil before adding the polenta. That will allow the food to release easily.
I found this unpleasant. Way too dry and way too much sage.
Added parsnip to the recipe but otherwise made it as written. My polenta was made from red flint corn.

I thought it was delicious as written! Cooking the polenta in this way made for some texture variety; some bites were crunchy with oil and some bites were softer… all of it yummy. I messed up and bought a regular potato, so I just used half of it and added a chopped carrot. Kept it vegan by using Violife feta alternative. I’ll make it again!
Any suggestions for veggies other than brussel sprouts (which I can’t eat)?

I mostly followed the recipe and it was great. An unexpected treat were the crusty pieces of cornmeal that had seared to the bottom of the skillet in the oven. I discovered those as I stirred everything up before broiling. I stirred everything and broiled again to make even more crispy bits.

In the 15 years I've been cooking for my partner, this was hands-down the WORST thing I've ever put on the table. I followed the recipe to the letter and it was awful. None of the flavors (such as they were) melded, putting the skillet under the broiler made the polenta like eating gravel. I was raised to never throw away food, but after a few forkfuls, we tossed the whole thing in the compost pail.

I thought his worked just fine as written . Sure, you can do a lot of things differently, and I’ll probably freelance a bit in future, but roasting the veg separately defeats the purpose of a one pan dinner.
I think Step 4 means to add the veggies to the skillet, not a bowl

Thx for all the tips! I’m giving this a try. I’m very confused about the quantity of brussel sprouts recommended since 6oz, whether you are talking weight or volume, is not anywhere equivalent to 2 cups. I am curious how much people used but perhaps it doesn’t matter?
Would this amount of water actually cook this meal into polenta? I think it might turn into pone,  which is still nice, but not really polenta.
What can you substitute for polenta?
Tasty but not worth the effort.
This is a satisfying meal and easy way to use up fall root veggies. We topped it with a fried egg and Sriracha. It will make a hearty breakfast as well.

The flavors in this are great, but the sweet potatoes took 20 min longer than suggested to cook. I will try it again but will precook the shallots and sweet potatoes to get them a little bit softer faster.

I searched for a good “potluck” dish.  I should have understood that anything requiring a careful watch over a broil and “serve immediately” is not a good potluck dish.  Also, it really doesn’t work.  Much smarter to just make a potent, roast some veggies then serve them together.  Broil is you want but this recipe was a beast to watch over.

title: Salmon and Green Beans in Red Pepper Sauce Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: This is my favorite dish I have ever made from NYT Cooking, and I have been cooking from these recipes for more than six years. I absolutely love how elegant and flavorful this recipe is! Please add more Nigerian and West African recipes like this one!!!!!!!!!!
Is there a substitute I can use for the anchovies?
A few splashes of fish sauce, though you won't "know" there are anchovies as they dissolve into the sauce.
You can also use smashed capers if you want to avoid the fish component.
I was surprised to find I didn't have any anchovies in the house, so I used 1 Tbsp of fish sauce made from anchovies instead. The dish was lovely, but I'll try it with anchovies next time I make it.
Made as written with skinless steelhead, a habanero, and fire roasted jarred red peppers. Used a 12" cast iron skillet. Nice flavors, and the fish held its own against the sauce. I'd make it again.
Sauce was unique and tasty, texture made for comforting meal. My salmon was thinner than photo so a tad overcooked but easy to correct next time. Husband loved it.

Made this for my mother yesterday. I cut the recipe in half and left out the habanero. The miso flavor was very very mild. In the end, mine was saucy  but delicious. I would definitely make this again. It was a hit with my mom, too.

Made this last night as written, and it was delicious. We did add more green beans, as two cups for four people did not sound like enough. We could have doubled the amount.

The flavor was nice, but very subtle. We would definitely make this again, with the addition or more anchovy and miso. Excellent dish.
Don’t be afraid to use fresh red peppers!
@Pierpaolo or yellow or orange (which is what I have at hand just now). It will change the color of the sauce but not the flavor.
@Pierpaolo that’s what I’m planning to do. Did you roast them first?
Finally! A recipe for salmon that isn’t sweet! A+
I made this last night.  It was super easy and very tasty.  We all enjoyed it.  I had leftover sauce and ate with a poached egg for breakfast.  I used a jalapeno instead of the habanero.

Made as written except cooked everything in a 10-inch stainless steel skillet and used half a serrano instead of the habañero/scotch bonnet because I was cooking for a more spice-sensitive friend. It was delish! Served with crusty focaccia and salad.
@Lev is Serrano a less spicy pepper?
@TC compared to scotch bonnet, yes significantly so. you can even do this with a jalapeño, which is less spicy than a serrano. it would go jalapeño -> serrano -> scotch bonnet

My husband and I made this dish today and had several ideas. First, the anchovies overpower the salmon flavor and could be deleted. Second, if you’re not a fishetarian, sub chicken stock for the water added while cooking instead of using anchovies. Finally, avoid reducing the pepper sauce too much so you have enough for all 4 fillets.
Swap the anchovies for capers...

The sauce was fantastic! Great flavors even without the anchovies and the green beans—just did not have these ingredients on hand. Added fresh red pepper and jalapeños from our garden to the food processor to chop fine. Fish was delicious. A definite hit.

I'm cooking for me and my teen, and the teen doesn't do "very spicy". I realize the heat is a fundamental element in the sauce, but does Yewande or someone else have a suggestion that keeps the overall essence but tones the heat down to milder level? Would subbing a jalepeno still result in something similar? Doesn't have to be "no heat". Thanks!

@Alexa
I am going ti try a dash of cayenne, and have Gojugaru flakes on hand for a flavorful hear boost gor the adventurous.
I'd recommend using a stainless steel pan, not cast iron. Simmering liquid, especially if it contains any acidic ingredients, can discolor cast iron.

@Sean Kiely If the cast iron skillet is kept properly oiled, I have found this type of recipe OK.  The depth of "cure" in the skillet is crucial and the skillet needs to be cleaned only by rinsing, scouring with salt if food sticks, rinsing again to remove the salt, then drying with some heat and lightly oiling with a few drops of oil.  I use olive oil (excepting fried recipes cooked in a wok) which seems to work well.

@Sean Kiely   Acidic foods pull a bit of usable iron out of the cast iron. When people gave up cooking with cast iron, we became much more likely to be anemic. My skillets have retained some odors from cooking but no big deal and they’re black so not to worry about stains.
@NYC cooks I grew up on cast iron cooking in the south. It’s good for you.

I was excited to make this recipe after reading the comments but I didn't like it at all. On a positive note, it is a beautiful looking dish but I did not like the taste of the sauce. Very fishy. I tried the red pepper sauce before I added the miso and half the anchovies and the sauce was quite tasty but once the miso and anchovies were added, it was awful and cooking it, didn't help. I cook most of my family's meals from NYTimes but this wasn't good.
@kristiI totally agree. I cook from NYT cooking all of the time and this was just not good at all. My husband thought it was ok- but, I couldn’t finish mine.

This was not our favorite recipe although we are big fans of NYT Cooking in general. We omitted the anchovies and replaced them with a splash of fish sauce as recommended by one commenter. The result was somehow both flavorless and overly salty and fishy.

@Ollie when cooking with fish sauce, I find the dish needs to simmer a little bit to allow the flavors to blossom.  Otherwise the salty/ fishy taste can be off putting.  Give it an additional 5-10 minutes on a simmer, that allows the fish sauce to truly meld into the flavors.  Also the lemon squeeze at the end is essential to bring out the flavor.
Sadly, this was a big disappointment and the first disappointment after many NY TIMES recipes on repeat in my house. My husband finished his, politely. I couldn’t finish mine.

I thought this recipe tasted good enough. However, for the amount of work and ingredients that goes into it, it felt underwhelming. I followed everything to the T and didn’t think the end product had enough depth or excitement. Even though I enjoyed it, I won’t be making it again, wasn’t worth the effort.
Sorry, really disappointing. Made 1/2 recipe but green beans and salmon just didn't come together. Rare miss for NYT Cooking.

Made a few alterations as I'm cooking for one, and not loving leftover salmon. Followed directions until cooking the salmon, then cooked just one portion and froze the rest for the next time. Removed all but one portion of veggies and proceeded per directions. Worked like a charm, except I didn't love the flavor combination. I'll save the salmon for another recipe and have the veggies with something else. They were pretty good, but not with salmon. 3 stars for me.
Ugh, hated this. Way too spicy. I bought the most beautiful salmon filet and this was like smashing it repeatedly over the head with a sledgehammer. All I can say is no, no, no!
How spicy is the dish? I enjoy very hot dishes but my wife has a lower tolerance. Omit the scotch bonnet?
@Ben use a jalapeño for something milder but still with a little heat.
I don’t have a source for miso and don’t know how much I would use it.  Is there a substitution?

@Jane Vallery-Davis, if there’s not an Asian store around, you can order from Amazon, and keep it for a year in the fridge (!!!  Yes, it has a very long shelf life).  You can add a tablespoon to pasta, to chicken noodle soup, to stir fry’s, or to homemade salad dressing.  There really isn’t a substitute; you would just omit it.  The flavor you would be omitting is umami, so you could sub in your choice for that.
@Jane Vallery-Davis Trader Joe’s has it! That may not be close for you though. Something salty and umami-ish would be a good sub.
Can this be made without the anchovies?

Will be trying this recipe tonight. Will sub in Gochujang for scotch bonnets. Had some red peppers in the ‘fridge and will roast those - quartered, de-seeded, parchment lined sheet pan with avocado oil - 375 oven. Remove from oven and put in a covered metal bowl to steam the skins loose. Much better tasting. If you do this on a weekend, the peppers can be stored in the ‘fridge for a few days in a jar with oil of choice.
Following suggestions I added the whole can of anchovies and doubled the miso. Excellent lovely meal and easy preparation
Quite lovely. Some might want to reduce the amount of habanero to a half. The anchovy miso sauce is so nice, it might work with just the beans as a side dish.

I found the sauce very bland and flavorless. The short cooking time did not allow the onions to soften, therefore they were practically raw, and crunchy. My instincts told me to sauté them first but I decided to follow the recipe. I know it’s not the same recipe but I cooked the sauce until the onions were soft and added tomato paste. I found this an improvement but I still wouldn’t  make this again. The salmon was good, though.
I made as written and had your same opinion.  BLAND.  I did not put as much serano pepper, but the sauce was blah and I don't blame the lack of heat

This was absolutely marvelous and a keeper. I only wish the negative reviewers had actually made the recipe as given, instead of substituting this and that and then complaining! Can't wait to make it again.
Nice change of pace for salmon. I wish I had added  some lime or lemon juice at the end just to balance the heaviness of the sauce.
I thought I would love this recipe but found it to be a little too rich and oily for my taste. Made as described except roasted my own peppers instead of using jarred.

To those who said the sauce was bland—pretty sure what you needed to do was add a little more salt. When I first tasted it toward the end it was a bit bland, so I added some salt, tasted again, then a bit more...in the end it was delicious and not bland at all. But it does need salt and the recipe doesn't tell you how much to put, just to taste. I thought this was a great way to cook an OK piece of salmon—would probably hide a superstar king salmon, but perfect for Trader Joe's salmon!

Omg… I have to stop eating long enough to say how absolutely scrumptious this recipe is! I made it almost completely as is… Just used maybe closer to 12 ounces of green beans… Didn’t have quite enough anchovies so added a couple of teaspoons of capers… Otherwise made it exactly as written ( for a change lol!). It’s incredibly delicious! Serving it over brown rice. Love it love it love it!
Made this for my visiting grandson and he loved it.  Added a few pieces of fried tofu to the leftover sauce and made it work a “leftovers”  Still a great tasting dish and a good alternative.
Miso is an added expense that, at 1 TBSP, did not make a difference.  You can skip it.  You will be fine.  I added extra anchoive for the umami.

I used an enamel cast iron skillet. Substituted habenero for scotch bonnet but ended up seasoning with additional salt, pepper, cayenne, garlic powder and smoked paprika. Added spinach before re-adding salmon to pot. Served alongside jollof rice - it was delicious and not too heavy!

A truly great change up of flavors. I opted to use okra instead of green beans- just cut off the tops then into roughly one inch pieces and cooked as instructed. Next time I will double the sauce and okra.

Not sure how this managed to be both bland and terrible tasting at the same time.  The commenter who called out not enough green beans was spot on, but overall this is just  very oddly conceived receipe that sadly does not work.

I made this for the second time tonight, and for the first time I had habaneros. The lone habanero in the sauce really transformed this dish into something spicy, fruity, and wonderful. 

Suggestions for the rest of my carton of habaneros are welcome.

Loved the look and texture of the sauce but maybe it was because I had to sub caribe peppers for scotch bonnet / habanero but the flavour was slightly lacking in depth, although I should have tasted more to adjust salt and pepper. We added hot sauce / red pepper flakes and then it was enjoyable. Overall, nice.
I always find jarred red peppers to be too cloying - does anyone have a good brand they recommend? Or a substitute? I can make my own but it can be time consuming on busy weeknights...
@Maggie  same - the taste of fresh red roasted peppers is far better than jarred. I’ve made them, then stored the peppers for a couple days in the fridge in a jar covered with oil of your choice
Please give clues on using induction cooktop.  Mine is new and I love it but cannot use cast iron.  I will try anyway with stainess steel.
@Kay. You absolutely can use cast iron on an induction cooktop. I use cast iron cookware on mine regularly.
The recipe calls for the fish to be skin side up in the sauce, but the photo is otherwise.  Did I miss something?
The instruction said skin side up (salmon) at the final step. The picture shows skin side down. I don't think it matters.

So very EXCITED about this recipe becuz it has all my favorite ingredients especially the anchovies which I buy in bulk at Trader Joe’s! And. I’ve had MISO in dressings which is another love affair! 

Thanks !!
I substituted anchovy paste for tinned anchovies. delicous.
Hi! Novice cook here.  Could I use another fish?  Halibut?  Swordfish?  Thanks in advance for the advice.
This was excellent. I would double the green beans next time. (I got skinless salmon accidentally but it worked fine).

This recipe is fantastic! I used to think I didn't like anchovies until Melissa Clark snuck them into sauces and now I love using them. I substituted the super hot peppers for a jalapeno and a hot wax pepper and the subtle heat was fine for the 2 of us. I didn't halve the sauce/beans and served with boiled new potatoes. We thought all the flavors were great together!
As a single person, I'm interested in finding out whether portions of this can be frozen for future meals. Anyone know?
Delicious. I used skinless salmon, otherwise I followed the recipe. Next time I'll double the pepper sauce, which was very tasty. Served with white rice and a small green salad.
Delicious!!!  Will definitely make again.  Served over brown rice.  Rich, warm and satisfying with lots of flavor.
Outstanding recipe!  The crispy salmon skin was wonderful.  I substituted a teaspoon of gochujang for the hot pepper and used snap peas along with green beans.
Absolutely fantastic - next time I’ll sauté the salmon fillets outside on the grille in a cast iron pan to save on the smell & mess!

Modified the recipe and worked out great!  My pink lipstick chard stems from home garden substituted for green beans, a couple NM chiles into sauce foregoing the sardine miso addition as who needs that much salt! Was very delicious but even more delicious cold the next day!

Great result. Mostly followed the recipe, but used 12 ounces of green beans rather than 8 - I think that resulted in a better balance. I used a 12" cast iron skillet; no way that salmon would have fit in a 10". I didn't have fresh scotch bonnet or habanero, so used 1 tsp habanero powder instead. The end result was gloriously spicy for me, probably too spicy for many others.

I burned the heck out of my salmon skins and then burned the heck out of my sauce.  Followed directions to the letter, using a habanero.  I like lots of spice and usually heat but the habanero overpowered everything. Nevertheless it was very tasty and I’m looking forward to my leftovers.

No habanero at my market, so I subbed habanero hot sauce and Calabrian chili oil. Could not force myself to overcook the beans, so they maintained some crunch. Served with rice, and Hubby LOVED it! It will stay in permanent rotation. Thank you!
could not at all appreciate the more delicate flavor of the salmon...

Interesting break from our normal simply grilled salmon. Delightful, with tons of flavor.  Not sure I’d use it with expensive Skuna Bay salmon, but we really loved it with TJs salmon.  I discovered, at the last minute, that the jar I thought was roasted red peppers was actually sun-dried tomato bruschetta. Oopsie.  No problem; full steam ahead!  The sun-dried tomatoes blended really well.  Not at all too fishy nor too spicy for our tastes.  Salmon was done perfectly.  We’ll definitely do it again!

I tend to overcook and ruin fish and the salmon turned out well this time. Only problem for me was that the anchovies and miso instantly burned when I added them (the anchovies fried whole and didn't break down), so the sauce was inedible. I used a stainless steel pan on a gas stove and had turned the heat down to medium. I'll try this again but take the pan off heat for a couple minutes before adding the anchovies, miso, and green beans.

I was excited about this recipe and vowed to make it but in the end, we were disappointed. I used a rather small habanero for fear of it being too spicy, although we like a little heat. The result was not bland, but had no real defining favor element for all the effort required to make it. It was a fair amount of prep and there are many more tasty salmon recipes that do not require as much effort nor as many ingredients. The fish itself was tasty, but the sauce we rated “meh.”
Made as directed with King Salmon. It was very good. The salmon stood up to the sauce.
Delicious! I loved the taste of roasted red peppers. I used capers instead of anchovies, used a Holland pepper, because I had that around, and added a little bit of honey.
Is it possible to use a scant amount of cayenne to give it a little heat?  My family doesn't not do heat really at all.

I made this tonight as written and we were really underwhelmed.   All the ingredient flavors were cooked to blah, no pop.  Couldn’t taste the garlic, the pepper.  Not sure quite what would have made a difference…

This was deceptively bad. The anchovies were not a hit and overpowered everything. Why are we making a subtle sweet fish meat taste fishier on purpose? Before the anchovy stage, the sauce was very pleasant.
@Anti-Anchovy Anchovies made me think puttanesca and kalamata olives solved it.
Surprisingly delicious! I used a dried Scotch bonnet and it added a nice fragrance. I added 6 anchovies and it didn’t taste fishy. I also used frozen French green beans.

I made this exactly according to instructions (as I always do the first time) and thought it was borderline vile. The sauce was seriously unpleasant — way too much onion and garlic that didn’t have the benefit of mellowing by browning. It tasted raw, and the green beans felt incongruous. Also made a big mess of the kitchen with all the hot oil and frying fish. Hubby liked it more than me, but he won’t be eating it again.

This is so-so. I do not understand the rave reviews. While I am not anti-anchovy, I agree with Anti-Anchovy's comments. I like a lot of flavor, including spice heat, in my mouth, but this was unbalanced with too much heat and too much saltiness. Whatever you do, please do not waste beautiful salmon on this recipe as I did. (Copper River King.) Use a lesser salmon because the sauce will completely overpower the salmon. Unlike other commentators, I will not make this again.
@Ekaterina This got an "okay" at our house as well.  Second pass with uneaten half - Added some fresh peppers and kalamata olives for the umami that was missing.

re Nancy B.
You could use fish sauce as a substitute for anchovies (2 teaspoons for this recipe) but be aware that fish sauce does contain anchovy extract. Anchovies are too intensely salty for me but fish sauce is a good substitute in my view.

I used a de-seeded habanero and the sauce was very mild and needs a little acid at the end. I recommend cooking salmon separately because simmering the fish in the sauce with the leggy green beans resulted in messy looking fillets that were falling apart in chunks. It was awkward to present.
Great recipe. Very flavorful—will definitely make this again. (Used habanero chili and 8 anchovies)

Amazing recipe. I doubled it and made it with one half scorch bonnet with seeds one deseeded. It was definitely spicy. The flavors grew on me. At first I thought it was just ok but as it sat and cooled down I really enjoyed it. Lemon juice squeezed in at the end doesn’t overpower here since there are a lot of bold flavors.

I made this tonight for dinner - EXCELLENT. Probably one of the best salmon dishes I've made in recent memory. The red pepper sauce is fantastic. I was a little skeptical of breaking out the food processor but it is 150% worth it and I will be making this again next week!

Make this! Wow, this is simple to make yet tastes complex. No more dry, boring salmon. This will be my go-to for using frozen Alaska salmon steaks from Costco. Served over brown rice, this is a healthy meal the whole family loves. I used anchovy paste and it worked a charm.

This was a simple and relatively quick healthy meal to pull off and with a few tweaks it could be fabulous. I made as written and although I was disappointed with the first few bites the flavours grew on me. Next time I’d  simmer the sauce much longer ( I did increase the time a bit); dial up spices and probably switch to a white fish because salmon is not my favourite fish -yes, I know everyone loves salmon-I’m trying to eat more of it.
I made this tonight. It had such depth of flavors. My family loved it. I have never cooked with Anchovies before. But they added so much flavor. It is a keeper.

Made last night exactly as written but only used 1/2 of a seeded habanero.   Result was terrific.   My wife loved it and described it as “very clean”.  We didn’t find it spicy at all and zero fish flavor from the anchovies.  Just great flavor.  Think I will go with the whole habanero (seeded) next time.   This is definite repeat recipe.   And folks.  Like most of the comments reflect, if you let the anchovies dissolve (mash them with a wooden spoon etc) you will NOT tase fish.  Another NYT winner!
I followed this recipe to the letter and it is absolutely delicious!

I've never left a comment before but this is so transcendent & easy to make! My only suggestion would be to *lightly* salt the salmon (as per the instructions). The flavors of the sauce are so unique and delicate, too much salt overrides the dish.
Very good recipe.  Can think of many variations to try.

Easier to make than expected.  Used Dutch oven.  Used red chili flakes instead of fresh chili.  Used soy sauce and anchovy paste instead of anchovies and miso.  Used Trader Joe’s frozen green beans.  Will add this dish in the dinner rotation
when do you add the red peppers?  It doesn't say?

I am an incompetent cook -- I ruin half the recipes I try. But not this one! I simply followed the recipe, and it was among the best things I've ever made. This is a delicious dish -- fit for company. Truly impressive. The only change I made was to use anchovy paste instead of anchovies, and it was perfectly fine. If you think this recipe sounds at all complicated, I can promise you it isn't. If I can do it, anyone can!
My new favourite dish! Absolutely delicious
Sorry, this recipe is a disappointment.  It's spicy hot red pepper sauce totally overpowers the salmon.  Made it once, never again.

I think this is The first time I've believed a five-star recipe to be a total fail. like some others, I found the sauce to be very fishy, and the strong taste of raw onions persisted even after the sauce was cooked down for a long time.
Do not understand the 5-star rating. Terrible. Avoid.

Really good! Could add a lot more beans (or, in fact, almost any other green vegetable).  Easy to adjust the spiciness by adding more chili pepper. Anchovies and miso are surprisingly subtle but really round out the flavours.

Based on the reviews this recipe was loved or despised. We really enjoyed this dish - give it a try. 
Some suggestions based on the negative comments: I drained the oil used to brown the salmon to reduce the fishy taste some reviewers noticed. Strongly recommend skinless salmon (the freshest you can find). I cooked the pepper mixture with the anchovies and miso first to mellow the raw onion flavor and added a tablespoon of tomato paste and a tsp of curry powder, onion powder and garlic powder. Let that cook for 5-10 mins and the flavors meld beautifully. Taste as you go and add whatever flavors you like! I added twice the anount of green beans and 1 cup water after the pepper mixture lost its ‘sharpness’ and simmered until the green beans softened. Then added the fish and covered for a few minutes. Finished with some fresh lemon. Delicious with rice or crusty bread.

Just made this last night. We are fortunate to have very easy access to excellent salmon, always looking for alternative presentations that really work well, and this one really does.  I would suggest removing the skin, cubing the salmon, and adding to the thickening simmering sauce 5-6 minutes prior to serving. Use more salmon than suggested to feed a crowd and add more beans as well, there is plenty of sauce to share.   The cubed salmon cooks rapidly and is much easier to eat. The skin, even if crispy from its initial sear, rarely retains any crisp and soggy salmon skin is not my cup of tea.  The flavors meld well, even better the next day.

I'm not a fan of raw onion and garlic so I sautéed the onion garlic and pepper after wiping out the pan completely when the skin side of the salmon crisped up. Added 1/2 cup water and cooked that for a minute or so. Then removed to a bowl and using my immersion blender puréed it all. Back to the skillet with the green beans. Cooked for a few more minutes and finished the salmon in the roasted pepper sauce. Added chopped fresh dill when done. Delicious

To be clear, by "green mangoes" the recipe no doubt means unripe mangoes, a common ingredient in many Swahili dishes.  Urojo is tart and tangy.  In Zanzibar, much of the pleasure of enjoying mix comes from selecting the savory "bitings" to top it with, such as kachori, cassava chips, and mishkaki (bits of skewered goat or beef often being grilled at a stand next to the mix vendor).
It's different! Love it! You had me at mangos and garam marsala. (I did substitute a low-fat coconut milk though. The saturated fat content of full strength is just too darn much for me)
Are green mangoes a certain variety of mangoes?  Or are they just until pure mangoes (or unripe mangoes of a certain variety)?

Green mangoes are unripe mangoes. Many varieties are used this way around the world, but at least in my part of the US, I most often see underripe Kent (large, predominately green-skinned) mangoes. The key is ensuring that your fruit is HARD. Most of what my market sells as "green mangoes" are, unfortunately, already too soft and sweet. Good luck!

@ Samantha: Green mangoes are a different variety of mango, common in East Africa. They are green when ripe, and are tart and sweet. (You determine ripeness by smell and touch.) They're one of the first things I get when I return as I rarely find them Stateside. Things may be changing. I use to only find the sweet variety of passion fruit in the US. Now, I can get both tart and sweet varieties. 
If you can't find green mangoes, add fresh lime juice to mango to approximate the flavor profile.
@Emma this sounds like a very plausible explanation, but goes against most other comments. I wish the author would follow along and comment on what they actually meant with this ingredient.

Samantha, green mangoes are unripe mangoes.  They are commonly served sliced and sprinkled with Tajin as an appetizer here in Mexico.
I would guess that you could find them at an Asian grocery store in the US.
Bummer to offer a mango-based soup 3 months after peak mango season. Niw I'll have to remember to try this next June.
Wedges of tart raw mangoes brined with green chillies and stored for a week or two. Try it.
Your local Bengali store may carry frozen green mangoes.
I have the same question as Samantha. By "green mangoes," do you mean a variety of mango or a degree of ripeness?

I live in Oklahoma, so the best I could do were slightly green mangos. They weren't tart, they were soft & mostly ripe - but don't stress y'all it was still freaking fantastic! I'm sure the tartness of a different mango makes a complete different soup, but we absolutely LOVED this recipe with what we could scavenge locally. We will be making this unique flavor combo again& again!
wait this is so good! i think next time i'll take yewande's advice and serve it with falafel or mashed potatoes, something to cut the acidity just a bit.
I am making this with my AP World History class to celebrate the Indian Ocean Trade Network from Unit 2. We are going to have to improvise with ripened mangoes.

I've never made or had anything like this soup and I loved it! For once I followed the recipe as written, except lemon for the lime I thought I had but didn't. Can't see anything I would change, except getting limes and trying coconut milk instead of cream.  All the different toppings were fun and made an attractive presentation.
So when does the panko and spice mixture go into the dish?
It's in Step 6
This was amazing!  I used extra potatoes and it took a little longer to soften them, but followed everything else exactly.  Will keep this in my rotation for sure.

My only comment is that this is absolutely delicious. I left out the garlic (my partner is really allergic) and just upped the other spices a bit to compensate. Served with home-made sourdough bread, since the 2020 year made newly-minted bakers of us all...

I first made this recipe as-is (excellent! And the toppings really do make the dish!) But then I followed my stomach and roasted some fish (some really nice halibut that was on sale) and added that to re-heated left-overs - now this dish is truly complete for me :)
The video promoting this soup has toppings not included in the recipe but that look like they would be important for the flavor. Does anyone know what they are? (Thanks in advance!)
Some varieties of mango are green when rioe (like Amelie). You're supposed to use unripe mangoes so hard ones. Think of it like using a green tomato instead of a ripe red one
You can find frozen green mangoes that are tart at your local Indian grocery store. Easy to use in the recipe. I’m going to pick up a bag to try this recipe out.

title: Smashed Chicken Meatballs with Suya and Charred Corn Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Wow! What an interesting dish, unlike anything I’ve had before. Super flavorful! I opted without corn and instead served it over roasted butternut squash and wow with the peanut sauce this was out of this world. I cooked the meatballs in the air fryer for a less messy alternative. Delicious!
@Erika so glad to see this suggestion because I don’t have corn but do have butternut squash from the garden 👍 It’s always a good day when I can postpone going to the grocery store!
@Erika how did you do the smashing in the air fryer? Halfway through? Thanks!
@Erika I’m new to using my air fryer and would love to try it for the meatballs. Any suggestions on heat and timing? Ty!!
awesome recipe. i substituted fresh spinach for frozen, tahini for the miso, and arugula for the kale.  putting this into the rotation (with kuku sabza, among other NYTimes recipes)!

This was quite tasty.  We just did the chicken and corn, skipped the kale and rice at the end. 

There is a lot of peanut sauce - could cut in half and be fine.
@Sam or coulda just used hamburger and really enjoyed them.

in this house we have been using one of the 2 coffee grinders for spices for years. But that one broke and now if i use the regular coffee grinder for spices, afterwards i put raw rice into the grinder and grind it to a fine powder,.. then i wipe it out (unplugged!!) -- it helps get rid of the oils left by the spices.

I have a cuisinart mini prep/spice grinder; the grinder bowl/blade portion can be removed and run through the dishwasher.  
Makes one item work for both coffee and spices

I'm thinking the peanut butter could be substituted for almond butter (or half almond butter, half tahini), but would roasted almonds work for making the suya? Or would that change the vibe too much and this is just a recipe the peanut allergy crowd has to skip altogether?

@TFH so many tweaks offered for vegans and gluten sensitive folks, but seldom mention of peanut allergies. I’d like to hear how others sub for peanut butter, it’s not always a straight line substitution.
@Grandma would sunflower seed butter work?

High impact flavors from a handful of ingredients, this recipe is great. I agree on squash/pumpkin subs for spinach. To avoid uncoupling spice from culture, I’ll add that suja is Nigerian (called chinchinga when I had it in Ghana) and used widely in the region. It’s a gem. I also add it to chickpeas with mint.
@Nicole thanks for the culture connection.

Even as a vegetarian, I will peruse some meat containing recipes to ascertain if it will hold up to protein substitution. I love the spice & flavor profile here and look forward to trying this using Quorn in lieu of chicken.
@Katie I sub chickpeas, and its great! Could also used smoked tofu.
@Katie the NYT recipe “I cant believe its not chicken” works well here.

I found the recipe a bit too back and forth so I’ve simplified it.  Mix the peanut butter concoction in a small saucepan so the heat will help it mix easily. Added a dash of tamari and gojugong to it. Put 2-3 spoons of liquid peanut butter mixture in large bowl with ground chicken, spinach (didn’t have kale), extra ginger, a few dashes of garlic, paprika and onion salt along with a dash of cayenne and mixed with hands. Cooked the meatballs like it said. Added a little honey to warm peanut butter mixture. Drained the meatballs on paper towels. Added frozen corn to the pan and browned some, then added a little chicken stock and lime juice to scrape the pan with the corn and bits. Plate with lettuce, meatballs, corn and drizzle peanut butter mixture over top. Served with a little rice. Leftovers are great for lettuce wraps for lunch!

Flavors were delicious and I liked the result of mixing chopped spinach with the ground chicken. The meatballs stuck to my stainless steel pan and I had to do them in batches, even though the pan is large, 12-14 inches. Maybe they stuck because the oil wasn’t hot enough? I think I will mix the leftovers with ramen noodles and speaking of noodles, the sauce would be yummy mixed with noodles and vegetables like spinach and broccoli. A multipurpose recipe!
Really lovely flavors but a bit more fiddly to prepare than anticipated.

Fantastic -- not easy, there are a lot of steps and it takes time.  Did not try to cook meatballs and corn at the same time.  Took the meat balls out after browning, cooked the corn at high temperture to char, then turned down and pushed the corn to one side, added the meat balls and reheated before serving.  Followed the recipe, except substituting almond butter for peanut butter because that is what I had.  I prefer almond butter, although we have it for a someone who is allergic to peanuts.

I wonder every time I see a recipe that calls for a spice grinder, what percentage of home cooks actually have one?

I don't know any home cooks who have one, and a cursory search doesn't turn up any useful info.

Thoughts, folks?.

@Squirrel Butler an electric coffee grinder works great for spices and they’re not terribly expensive. Just make sure once you start using it for spices that you don’t use it for coffee beans—especially when grinding chiles/peppers!

@Squirrel Butler I resisted getting one for years, figuring the preground stuff would be fine for my needs. Then I started down the slippery slope of Indian cooking and thought “my trusty mortar and pestle will be fine”! I finally caved when I realized that a coffee grinder just for spice would make that job waaaay easier. Glad I came over to the dark side!
@Squirrel Butler I use a coffee grinder I keep for spices only. It works fantastic.
Really enjoyed this. So flavorful! We used ground turkey, fresh spinach and corn, and placed on a bed of arugula. Also subbed cashews for peanuts and it worked great.

Suya is a new to me spice mix and I really enjoyed it! Sauce was delicious. The recipe is relatively easy except I have no idea how big of a pan you need to be able to cook the meat mixture and also the corn. I had to do the meat in batches and then the corn.
I did all the herbs because I was going to the store but I think you could do without Im not sure they added so much that  I would run out to get if I had everything else

@Vicki same…we used a very large cast iron skillet, and there was only a tiny space for the corn at the end…next time, we’ll cook the meatballs, remove, then char the corn! This was perfect on a chilly Friday night!

Made these tonight and really loved the flavor profile! Used ground turkey instead of chicken, and farro in place of rice.  Also, didn’t have peanut butter so used PB Fit powder instead (just add water to reconstitute). I too used the air fryer instead of pan on the stovetop, and to respond to MSG, time in air fryer was 7-8 minutes and no smashing needed.  Just made irregularly shaped meatballs and slightly flattened them before cooking. Will definitely make again!

I made these as a weeknight meal after another long day.  To say that it made the day worth it would be an understatement.  The combo of peanut powder and fresh greens, along with the nuttiness of the dressing was ethereal.  A perfect reason to keep miso around.  I tweaked the recipe to make the meal more like a salad by using fresh spinach and kale, rather than cooked and slightly wilted from frozen.  Other than that adaptation, I made the recipe as is and will definitely make this again!

Made this for dinner and it was so flavorful! I skipped the kale because my husband is picky and subbed fresh baby spinach (chopped up small) for the frozen spinach. I used Trader Joe’s frozen roasted corn (already charred) and served it over riced cauliflower. So good!
one of the better NYT recipes I've ever made. swapped serrano peppers for the habanero.

This was terrible! The meat was way too salty, and the elements did not work together at all. The sauce turned out to have a weird, unappetizing flavor. The textures of the meat, charred corn, greens and sauce were unpleasant together. The worst NYT Cooking recipe I have ever tried, and there won't be a second time.
Amazing!  A perfect blend of textures and flavors.  And quite spicy - yeah! - you can easily buy a tasty suya spice on Amazon if you don’t want to purchase a second coffee grinder 😜
Delicious as written. Amazing peanut sauce with lots of leftovers. Served with pearl couscous and it was a huge hit. Next day the leftovers went into a tortilla for a tasty twist on a quesadilla.

This is really delicious but be aware you should allow 90 minutes. Lots of moving parts if you have to start by roasting the peanuts for the suya spice.  Maybe I’ll plan to do the suya spice the day before.
I used rendered chicken fat instead of vegetable oil. Scrumptious.
Re the corn: TJ has charred corn kernels, for speed. We like the taste of raw corn, so just toss it in the chicken at the end to warm. Sauce is worth the small hassle of spice grinding. Yum.

I have a cooking labor to reward ratio. The whole time I was preparing this, with every step I was thinking “never again.” Then we ate it and I thought, okay, I’ll make it again. 

Only substitutions were ground turkey because that was what they had and arugula because that was what they had (and I will go on record and say I think arugula probably works even better.)
I love this comment! For some reason I can't give it a thumbs-up, so now you know!

Re: spice grinders, Cuisinart has a spice and nut grinder for around $50. I used to use a coffee grinder but it just wasn't big enough when you are preparing spice blends in quantity for Indian cooking and things like that. Works great for chopping peanuts and things like that too.

This was fantastic as is and has been saved to make again (and again). I agree, you could halve the sauce and be okay. And the total time of 45 minutes seemed ambitious - more like 75-90 minutes. Worth the time & effort!
So delicious! Made exactly per the recipe. Very fresh and tasty.

Loved the suya and peanut sauce with the charred corn and kale, but the meatballs were weirdly bland and almost tasted undercooked even though they were well-browned. Honestly, I would only make again without the meatballs! Maybe add other vegetables such as the butternut squash recommended by others. It also definitely needed a lot of fresh lime at serving to perk up the flavor.

Made as written, except used dry ginger in meatballs as had no fresh, and used a combo of a (remarkably tasteless) ancho chili I had, plus red pepper flakes, instead of the Scotch Bonnet. Next time will use a better pepper, fresh ginger, and will try arugula + spinach instead of kale. Definitely will cook again -- everyone LOVED this.

I made this exactly as written, and it was delicious! My husband and I had it 2 nights in a row for dinner, and it was just as good the 2nd night. I made the suya spice in my Cuisinart food processor, adding the whole peanuts and all the spices at the same time and pulsing. It came out perfect, no need for a spice grinder. I used 2 ears of corn, and could have gotten away with one, and I didn't serve it with rice, and didn't miss it.

Try for the largest pan possible. I used a 12” cast iron which was too small, so I had to split them with another pan. 

The corn adds a lot to this recipe as does the mint cilantro lime to cut through the peanuts.
This was delicious! I love a good sauce so the peanut sauce was a star in our house. Skipped the kale but will try next time. The suya spice was great- would make again…

@nicole your comment confuses me because the spinach mixes with the chicken whereas there was a suggestion to substitute corn with the squash/pumpkin. The corn (or squash/pumpkin)  gets cooked next to the meatballs, not in them. ?

I don't own a spice grinder, so I just made the Suya spice in a regular food processor. It didn't come out a super fine powder, but I actually really enjoyed the really small chunks of peanuts in the sauce. I made with sweet potatoes instead of corn and it paired perfectly with the peanut sauce!

IMHO - there are far too many ingredients, and it takes much longer to prepare than stated. I don't think the (costly)miso added anything as it was overpowered by other ingredients.   I used a large cast iron Dutch oven. I had to do 3 meatball batches plus 1 for the corn adding to the time to prepare. They tasted great and I really liked the corn, but if I do it again, I think I would just mix all the ingredients together and bake it like a meatloaf. Oh, and there was WAY too much peanut sauce!
One of the best recipes I've ever made. My husband called it "restaurant quality".
Use TJ's turkey meatballs with some of the sauce, and TJ's roasted corn.  Not the same, but quick and easy.  Oh, and being out of peanuts, either roasted almonds or walnuts in the suya spice.
For the suya, since all the other spices are already ground, could I sub peanut powder, like PB Fit?
I used PB2 (no other ingredients besides peanuts)
Yewande, this recipe is phenomenal! Thank you so much for creating and sharing it! A symphony of flavors in my mouth!

I just put some peanuts in a ziplock bag and walloped them with my rolling pin to make the suya spice. It left some small pieces, but powdered them for the most part. This was delicious, just as written. I had to make my chicken bits in batches, and we used arugula because I couldn't find baby kale, but my husband and I both really enjoyed it! (The kids had corn on the cob and chicken nuggets because I figured the habanero would be too hot for them.)
Was a lovely 'unusual' combination of flavours, but for me - the effort to pull it all together was not repaid with a flavour bomb.  Will not be making this again

This was so so so good! I made one batch of meatballs with the chilis for myself, and one batch without for the rest of my wimpy family (jokes jokes), and both were extremely flavorful. I even made a double portion of the suya spice and the kids are putting it on everything! 

I subbed the kale for more chopped spinach and rice and it was all gone in one night - no leftovers! 

Thanks Yewande for demystifying this West African spice for a particularly picky eater such as myself!
Made this recipe as stated and it was amazing. Such a unique blend of spices and flavors that I wouldn't have thought to put together. I learned so much, thank you!
Looks good can I bake them?

I am not very familiar with cooking with miso but see several varieties in the grocery stores. What is the best type to use?
Thanks so much!
@Lu Duval I would go with a darker miso (red) for this as it’s a stronger umami flavor generally
@Lu Duval I would start with white miso. It is more versatile and can be added to many other recipes to add umami.

@Lu Duval - I concur with Debe - white miso is much more versatile and you will find more recipes for white miso than a darker miso.  Once you find how much umami it adds to your cooking, you will use it more often.
Can I use ground pork?
@Patty yes I generally sub ground pork and chicken interchangeably

Wow, this recipe looks amazing!  I love how the suya spice and peanut sauce bring such bold flavors together. The idea of smashing the meatballs for that seared texture is brilliant, and the charred corn must add such a nice sweetness. Definitely trying this one soon!

Wanted to love this, but it was surprisingly meh.  Textures were dead-on, but flavor was lacking.  Most came from the cilantro and mint.  Definitely needed more salt, and next time I'll keep the seeds in the Habenero.  Wound up putting chili crisp on top to help.  I think it came down to the peanut sauce and I've definitely had tastier.

Was not a fan of the peanut sauce, subbed grated squeezed zucchini in for the spinach, cooked up some green beans along with the charred corn. This recipe is a keeper and I’m grateful I was able to adapt it easily. That suya spice blend is out of this world.

I agree that there was a lot of sauce, but really enjoyed the recipe overall. Definitely will try butternut squash next time as we had corn on hand.  Excellent with a mix of chicken and turkey- loved that it never got dried out as so often poultry does when cooked at higher heat.

Wow! They’re not flavors I would have thought to combine but they were excellent together. Though not “hit you in the tongue” flavors, which might be why some said it was bland, I found them really tasty.
The only change I made — except for forgetting the toppings, I always forget the toppings ::sigh:: — was switching almonds for peanuts to cut down on saturated fats. They worked just fine. (Though peanuts would undoubtedly add a more pronounced flavor.)

This is a unique and delicious recipe! I substituted Impossible beef for the chicken to make it vegetarian and roasted delicata squash for the corn since my garden is overflowing with it. I skipped the rice to avoid the carbs, but my husband said he wanted it with pita bread next time (and there will be a next time!). Be careful when grinding the suya spice - I went a little too far and almost turned it into peanut butter. Very adaptable recipe and quick enough for a weeknight dinner.

This was fantastic! I didn't have any peanuts on hand, so I used a handful of walnuts for the suya. I also ran out of miso so I used soy sauce instead. I warmed the peanut butter in the microwave, and the sauce was incredible. This was an easy, healthy recipe that I'll be making again!
Easy to make, perhaps. But it will take you at least a week to find the ingredients. Forget buying loads of things you will never use again.
Has anyone tried this in burger form?
The ginger is dried, correct?
Yes, in the suya, but fresh ginger in the meatballs. I find this odd as dried ginger is so blah.

This was delicious. I had leftover tahini hoisin sauce from a cauliflower recipe so I subbed that in and used most of the spices in the Suya (adapted for my husband who is not a ginger fan). I had to use frozen corn which took a bit to dry out and served it over spinach with brown rice on the side. Such tender meatballs and great sauce! Next time I am making double the amount of meatballs. I used a cookie scoop to shape and drop the meatballs in my skillet.

This was good but the kale needed a little something tossed with it like a lime dressing or something. I may have over-chared the meatballs so next time will cook at a slightly lower temp and toss some lime juice or something with the kale. Tasty, beautiful looking dish just needs a few tweaks.

This dish is super crushable.  Forgot the spinach, oh well…didn’t miss it!  The combo of suya spice and peanut sauce is really addictive - I recommend getting heavy handed with it when plating.  Easy to kick up or roll back the heat and pretty quick from start to finish.  A1 stuff.

I swapped roasted cashews for the roasted peanuts to make the spice powder—it was delicious. I think you could also swap almond or cashew butter for peanut butter in the sauce to make this work for anyone with peanut or legume allergies. This was such an interesting and flavorful dish—it’s going into the rotation!
Yum yum yum! Used arugula instead of kale and used my food processor to make the suya mix - delish!
used broccoli rabe and didn't have corn. the flavor was great and so different than anything I've had in the past. A lovely heat. I certainly went freestyle with what I had on hand.

Made this in advance by cooking meatballs first, setting them on a paper towel, then cooking the corn, before setting pan off the burner. When ready to serve, I put chicken balls back in pan with corn and heated. Great flavours!
This may be obvious, but the heat in this recipe can be moderated down to almost nothing.  My eaters include people who can't tolerate it.  It's delicious without it

I was so intrigued by the ingredients of the suya, I revised the meatballs to share the dish with someone who is vegetarian.  Next time I’ll try falafel with these seasonings, but today there wasn’t time for the soaking garbanzos would need.  So I devised some double-soy balls, about a quarter dried bean curd that was soaked and then sauteed with soy sauce; the rest, crumbled firm tofu.  They might work with just these ingredients and the seasoning, but this guest eats eggs.  Very good!

Made this exactly as written and it looked just like the picture. It was really delicious and everyone—including my 12 year old an 9 year old—loved it. Very flavorful and different than our usual weeknight dinner, and now that I've got a batch of the suya spice made up, it will be less work next time!
Just perfect! Made my own seasoning blend with the recommended recipe, used a jalapeño, and didn’t have the peanuts. However sauce was out of this world. Outstanding recipe.
was wondering if you could make this on a sheet pan instead of on the stovetop?
@ADC Thinking the same question! Seems like it would be adaptable without too much effort.
Trader Joe’s sells charred corn in the freezer section.  I will try using it.
Might be the most delicious meatballs I’ve ever had
Very good. Wish I had the herbs to add some flavor at the end. Need a better char on the chicken
Can you make this without the miso? I am allergic but can handle small amounts of soy sauce
I made this last night and 100% believe that you will not miss the miso at all. The dish is pretty flavorful and miso might add some depth, but the small amounts won't meaningfully change anything.
When is the kale used?  It’s in the ingredient list but not instructions
@Phil STEP 5 - Spread the greens on a large serving platter and top with meat and corn mixture…
Followed the recipe exactly, except added chopped peanuts and chopped peppers and sriracha  at the end. It was absolutely delicious. The lime squeeze is important.

@Yewande Komolafe or any reader willing to help - confused on Step 2 - it says to use remaining 2 T suya spice, but it seems like the recipe is referring to the peanut sauce made and reserved in Step 1.  Is that correct?  Going to try it that way - I'm sure it would be delicious either way - can't wait!
reread and realized that the 2 T peanut sauce goes in the medium bowl and everything else is added
@Anne No, they do mean 2Tbsp suya spice. You are supposed to only put 1 of the 3 Tbsp suya into the sauce. The other 2 go in the meatball mix. I missed that detail at first too.

If you want to make whisking the sauce a little easier, use very hot water. It'll cool after a while though, and thicken, and you may want to further adjust the thickness of the sauce. Overall, we found this ... OK. Both my wife and I said, "Not really worth the trouble of making again." I've made a couple of other Komolafe recipes and really enjoyed them, but this one...not so much.
Just incredible! We did add just a touch of fish sauce to the chicken and used two 10" cast iron pans insteadvof one. But results were 5 star worthy!

Just had it. A big hit. Not complex (I did just order the spice from Amazon because I wasn't in the mood to mix it). It looks fiddly but it really isn't. Basically you make the sauce (minus the honey and lime) and put some it in with the meatball mix. I'm sure if you forget the meatballs could take the rest. 

It is a really easy meatball recipe because you just plop some meat in, fry it, and flip it.
Was a bit more work than these usually are BUT worth every minute. We did use half of the scotch bonnet without seeds. Had a mild nice heat. This will be a dish we will serve to guests.
Needs chili.

This is a wonderful seasoning. Kudos to the person who mentioned peanut butter powder because preparing this seasoning was so much easier. I used PB2's Organic Pure. 
I noticed other suya spice recipes included a bouillon cube so I added 1/2 t. of MSG and omitted the salt. I added salt separately when I made the Suya Spice Salmon recipe, which I made with orange roughy because I had that on hand. Delicious!!
Can almonds be substituted for the peanuts, for those that can’t have peanuts?

@Gail M.
Why would one even ask? Cooking is freedom. Change what you like :)
Good question! Cashews perhaps? Maybe a bit closer to the flavor of peanuts than almonds.

I couldn't find suya in any of my local grocery stores, so this recipe saved the day! 

I made double the portion and my kids are sprinkling it on everything.
A question please - are the peanuts skinned or unskinned? I.e. do we remove the testa before adding the peanuts to the mix?

@Lindi
I would used skinned peanuts. The skins have a somewhat bitter flavor.

I ordered a jar of Suya Spice from Amazon, JG Foods brand. The ingredients  are Peanut(kulikuli), Ginger, Garlic, African Nutmeg, Clove, Black Pepper and Fennel.It was very peppery to taste, so much so that I gifted it to someone else.

I don't have the resources to grind and mix my own spice mixes. Although I wanted to try the various NYT recipes using this, I  have been dissuaded.
@P Ellen from Chicago Those ingredients are different from those listed in pretty dramatic ways.  Does anyone know which are more authentic?

This is terrific stuff. I thought, 1/4 cup of peanuts sounds way too small a batch; plus the recipe only said “makes 4 servings.” Yah but, how *much* is that? I doubled the recipe and, oh boy, 1/4 cup peanuts is plenty. I wound up with about 1.25 cups of the final mix in a doubled recipe. || 
I also replaced half the paprika with chipotle powder, to keep the smoke but amplify the heat; and kicked in some piquin chile molido at the end. (I likes the spicy.) Love this mix and can’t wait to try it on everything, veggies too. Popcorn!

My Zanzibari husband said this is a pretty good take for a “short-cut” Zanzibari pizza so thank you Yewande! If anyone is interested in project baking the real thing, try following Aromas of Zanzibar https://www.tiktok.com/t/ZP8STU9VX/ -She is adorable and will walk you through the process. While you are at it, try her recipe for kaimati- it’s a small donut hole covered in cardamom syrup and one of the greatest treats on the planet. The best part of the video- she makes the dough at work to rise and then brings it home to fry the kaimati for her kids. https://youtu.be/Py4j1SNSDLk

I buy frozen paratha that you heat/warm/toast on a griddle or skillet. Am I right in assuming to use it for this recipe I would have to thaw it first, then fill, fold and fry? It would seem difficult to fold a toasted paratha. Anyone have any thoughts on this?
@RSB56, the ingredient list specifies that the paratha should be “thawed if frozen “, which I take to mean left at room temperature until thawed.
@RSB56 where can you buy paratha? Used to find it at Trader Joe’s, but no more.
