@mary if you have an Indian grocery store near you there will be many options, especially in freezer section
Interesting and exciting recipe, correct me if I'm wrong but it sounds like an iteration of Mughlai paratha from the Indian subcontinent.
@M which is on brand for Zanzibar.
@M that would make sense, since India and Zanzibar had a strong trade relationship!
If I understand correctly, we are flipping onto uncooked filling? The photos look like there is crust on both sides, but that’s not what the instructions say.
@Diana note step 4: “Without breaking the paratha, fold the edge of the paratha closest to you about ½ inch over the filling’s edge and repeat for the 3 remaining sides until you have a square.”
@Tricia J Still sounds open to me.

Diana, I'm with you!  It was confusing to me also, so I watched a couple of videos.  Sure enough, the filling is NOT completely enclosed - you fold up the edges, leaving the middle exposed (like a French galette), and serve it with the crusty side up.  Sounds delicious and I can't wait to make it!
For those asking…Patel Bros grocery (highly likely one is near you) has both fresh and frozen paratha.

@M OC. 
I live in a Very Small Town in southern Oregon, and if I can’t buy it from our 1 Indian Restaurant, I’m going to have to make it myself!😄

This is version of mkate ya mayai, sometimes called chapati ya mayai, a fabulous Swahili street food. I practically lived on it in college in Nairobi--it's a complete meal and so delicious. I will try to make this (on a flat metal grill outside, over charcoal, with lots of piripiri sauce), but it will never taste as good as it did there.

Garam Masala is interesting seasoning with huge regionalized set of flavors.  Punjabi style is very different from Kerala or Kashmiri versions.  There are many more styles and flavor profiles that reflect India's diversity.  It would be really helpful to know which version or if this was a generic supermarket version which one.

This dish is similar to Uganda’s rolex, which is a rolled chapati filled with eggs, cabbage, onions, tomatoes, and other ingredients you can choose. The main difference is that a Zanzibari pizza is flat, while a Ugandan rolex is rolled.

I think you could use a flour tortilla for this recipe, as well.  Most of the flavor in this recipe is from the filling.

The bread he recommends looks great but is harder to find, at least for me.

I am usually fairly good at following recipes but couldn’t make the 4oz of ground beef cook up to a cup and a half of filling as this recipe suggests it should. I needed 12oz of beef for that. Am I reading the recipe wrong?

I'm confused by the filling stage. If you have baking paper covering the tin, what's the point of th flour? Veggie eggs go onto the paper lined baking tin raw so they cook, then the paratha bread and then the cooked meat filling is what the steps are saying? So how do you fold the paratha bread without making a mess? Can we have a video please, I can't visualise what the author intended. Or use meat and eggs instead of filling constantly to make it easier to understand what goes where.
I'm new here, maybe I missed it. Is there an adjunct post that sources utensils, table settings, and what not? I really want a big supply of those bamboo 2-prong serving forklets!
@Annie Long I think I got mine on Amazon. Just search for bamboo forks—or forklets even better
Wondering if I could do this as a sheet pan recipe.  Anyone try that yet?
This sounds amazing. Anyone make vegetarian friendly?

@Cassie I am going to try and make a version with soy crumbles like light life brand. The consistency is very similar to ground beef! You could also use crumbled tempeh or even potatoes in place of the ground beef. If using tempeh or potatoes I would up the seasoning to taste.

@Cassie
You can also try grated extra firm tofu, and season as directed it really acts like a very good ground beef substitute
Could you use a flour tortilla?
I am also confused : if the center is exposed and not covered by the paratha when you flip the pizza how do you prevent the filling to fall out into the pan?
Huge success with the family. I added red bell peppers to the lamb filling and some dill herb just before cooking. An explosion of tastes ! The "Vache qui Rit" is a must.

All I could taste was uncooked floor. And when opened runny egg came out despite being cooked for the full length of time and the outsides being a lot darker than those shown.  These went straight in the bin!

I have made this twice. The first time, I used a large fry pan and it didn’t seem that the egg cooked all the way through. I also added extra cheese to each one and used a bread called Lavash from Trader Joe’s. The second time, I added the extra shallots and cabbage to the meat along with the egg and pre cooked it. Then I used a panini press/foreman grill and it was way better. We also thought it needed a sauce and opted to make a tamarind chutney that was the perfect complement.

title: Sticky Rice Cakes With Sausage and Greens Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Made recipe exactly and this is a solid dish for anyone who likes a comforting, tasty-yet-mildly-flavored dish that can quickly come together. I found it slightly dryer than I’d like and needing a kick, so I added a mix of pantry hot sauces with some tteokbokki sauce and now we’re talking!

My immediate first thought upon reading the recipe would be to add some gochujang to the sauce (or replace the miso with the gochujang if you are worried about tilting too hard towards the umami side of the scale)
Making this now.  The sausage cooking instructions seem sketchy at best.
Sketchy exactly how? Puzzling comment.
@Holly what’s sketchy about it?
@Holly you mean because of letting the sausage sit undisturbed for 9 minutes? I scratched my head at that too. How did it turn out?

Came out amazing! Note, I made a few modifications for my home’s dietary restrictions and tastes but still a winner.

1. Swapped regular sausage for spicy Beyond Italian Sausage to make vegan/vegetarian. Worked wonderfully! Needed to just add a tiny bit of oil for the onions since there wasn’t pork fat.

2. Used 3/4 cup ketchup, 1/4 gochujang. Felt sacrilegious to make this dish without gochujang.

We enjoyed this dish as a pivot from the more traditional uses of tteok.  The sauce was very sweet and tangy from the ketchup (i used a chipotle flavored one) which masked much of the Italian sausage flavor.  I added a little extra bok choy.  I think I sprinkle of melty mozzarella would be a good addition over the top.

Replaced the sausage with tofu. I cut it into batons, squeezed it dry, then crumbled it into a cast iron skillet with 3T sunflower oil. Followed the instructions otherwise. The sauce is pretty mild. I forgot I have already made this once! I’ll zhuzh it up next time. I’d add vinegar/sugar/salt to the sauce to keep it flavorful (but not spicy). Maybe increase the sauce 1.5x. Added broccolini in place of the bok choy. Both are good, but the recipe is highly adaptable. Almost added some kale, but kept it to the single veg. Don’t sleep on the chili crisp, that’s what shifts it from kid-friendly to adult-tantalizing. Clear out your fridge, and believe in yourself—you got this!

Where's the gochu jang? Personally, aside from the color, ketchup and miso is not much of a substitute for gochu jang. Gochu jang is readily available in pretty much the entire country (via amazon or some other food delivery service). If people have access to chili crisp, more likely than not, they will have access to the korean red pepper paste.
@NorCalDoug. Well this recipe is different from traditional tteokbokki, hence no gochujang

As a Korean who grew up on tteokbokki this is an interesting version. Koreans also make “ketchup tteokbokki” that is child-friendly but I noticed even these ones usually do include a tbsp or so of gochujang in addition to the ketchup. My child doesn’t like spicy so I decided to try this recipe. When I tasted it I thought it lacked a bit of something so ended up adding some agave syrup, msg, and soy sauce. Also added a handful more tteok as it seemed too little for the meat and sauce. This was fine for both my child and myself (better after adding chili crisp) but if it were for me I’d stick to the gochujang version

This recipe is very similar to one we love at a restaurant in Seattle, with some tweaks. They use chorizo for more spice. I also found this sauce to be too ketchup/tomato-forward. I'm sure this version is kid-friendly, as the recipe intro suggests, but I will probably cut the ketchup with something spicy next time!
@Veronica could you name the restaurant please? Always on the lookout for good Seattle restaurants.
This is really good but a bit sweet due to the ketchup.  I used 3/4c ketchup and 1/4c gochujang, which this recipe BEGS for!  Next time I’d try to reduce the ketchup component even more.
I did half the amount of ketchup and it was way too sweet. Next time, I would do tomato paste, and add a little sugar
This was REALLY tasty. We added aprprox 1 Tbsp of gochujang to the sauce, which definitely helped amp up the flavor.
Fantastic!

We had a pack of loose lamb sausage in the freezer, so I used that with a ton of baby bok choy.  Otherwise essentially as written.  Was truly a fabulous comfort food dinner after a long hard day of moving.  Also — sacrilegious though it may be  — got my tteok on Amazon neither refrigerated nor frozen, and it was perfect.  Cooking times for everything (browning the sausage, sauce, bok choy addition) were much shorter than written.
I made this with beyond sausage and it tasted great but it seemed like too much meat relative to the amount of rice cake. I’d add more bok choy and less meat next time, but my husband loved it.
My family enjoyed this dish quite a bit! I did reduce the amount of sausage (2/3 lb) and upped the bok choy. Found that the sauce was pretty saucy, not dry as others have noted.
Really good and easy
This was delicious!  We made two substitutions.  We used turkey Italian sausage and we used unsweetened ketchup along with a tablespoon of Gochujang to the ketchup.  Cucumbers were yummy.

I made this recipe because I wanted to use tteok in a non traditional recipe. The recipe was good, tangy and sweet from the ketchup. If you don’t like ketchup I wouldn’t recommend this dish, as it’s the main flavor. 
Next time I might use a different flavored ketchup, like a spicy ketchup, to make it less sweet.
This was absolutely awful. The ketchup sauce is gnarly. I threw it out.

I was a little hesitant about the flavor combination: Italian sausage + ketchup + sesame/miso = a little odd. But it’s fantastic, fast, and easy! Used hot Italian sausage instead of sweet, plus a tablespoon of gochujang, and the spicy/sweet/sour/savory balance was excellent. This is going in the regular rotation as an easy weeknight staple.

I made this with 1/2 cup gouchujang and 1/2 cup ketchup, but it was still too sweet for me.  Has anyone tried subbing the ketchup with tomato paste or tomato sauce?

I might also suggest doubling the bok choy!
@alefro. I didn’t have ketchup, so used ~1/2 tomato paste w/ ~2+ tbsp brown sugar and a splash of cider vinegar.  I like a sweet profile so this was good and well received by the family.
@Holly What did you end up thinking of it?
It’s nice, but tastes a bit like a hamburger helper.
10 minutes on high undisturbed seems excessive for a pound of sausage.  Am I missing something?
The timings definitely seemed off when I was cooking this (stainless steel on electric stove). I just disregarded the timings and followed the descriptive indicators and it came out fine!
I made this tonight- and OMG- I have been making a brunch of gochujang mouth numbing recipes and hubs needs a break- this is so amazing and deciloius - I would not change a thing

This was good, but even with 1/4 cup less ketchup than the recipe called for, it still tasted a whole lot like ketchup. I also added about a 1/4 tsp of gochugaru with the ketchup mixture and close to a 1/4 cup of water with the bok choy, both of which I think improved the end product.

Eerily, I made something very similar to this last week and then had my phone present this video to me today. Not sure if it’s a case of having seen the video before and only registering it in my subconscious, or my phone spying on my real life and feeding me what it considers relevant content… In any case, the sauce starts similarly with ground pork, diced onion, and chopped garlic, but then I added a hefty helping of gochujang, mixed with soy sauce, fish sauce, sesame oil, sugar, and water. Used rice coins instead of cylinders because that’s what I had, and threw in a whole bunch of chopped bok choy leaves towards the end. I had jokingly called the dish Korean bolognese but now I see that name’s already taken on this app, too! So I guess I’m disappointingly unoriginal. Figured I’d share anyway because it was super tasty.

I was skeptical, but I had a lot of ttoek to use up so I tried it.  pretty good, like a very tomatoey and sweet spaghetti sauce. I did use chorizo sausage instead of sweet Italian sausage. If you hate ketchup I would skip this recipe and make regular tteokbokki instead.  the bok choy is important for the texture (and you've got to eat more vegetables, right?)
Too sweet from ketchup. Would use Gochujang next time
love these recipes that recommend items that my local grocery stores carry- like tteok
“Put the lime in the coconut and drink it all together.  Put the lime in the coconut and drink it all down.  Put the lime in the coconut and call me in the morning.”  Harry Nilsson
@Yespat I didn’t know that was a Harry Nielsen song! Fun !
Limes are used with their peels?
this is my question too!

Yes, and it adds a wonderful aroma. The addition of the peels gets you all of those awesome essential oils they contain and, as long as you follow the instructions to keep the blending quite coarse, will add only very minor (pleasant! necessary!) bitterness. This technique is a riff on Brazilian lemonade (also delicious, and with fewer ingredients, if you want to just dip your toes into this style of beverage).
Any suggestions for adding alcohol to this, say rum or coconut cream or vodka?

@Garth - I would add some G4 Tequila Blanco….
It nice on the palet, smooth & sweet….
Lime and coconut? Let's go with rum.
@Garth white rum, or vodka for a more neutral flavor

Yes with their peels. Wash them thoroughly, since the peel is going right into the blender.
Scrubbing helps remove any wax, dirt, or pesticide residue.
You don’t peel them — the zest and peel add bitterness and depth of flavor to balance the sweetness of the coconut milk.
Make with organic  allulose derived from figs instead  of cane sugar.  Type 1 keto friendly
For those who really know flavors, what is the black pepper doing in this drink?  It seems so odd and unappealing.

Black pepper limeade (without coconut milk) is an Indian drink & delicious, and I've made cookies with lime & black pepper that were great too.  Those flavors are great together, you don't have to crack more on top, can get the flavor without the grit and maybe then you will understand it?

@Nicole. It may seem counterintuitive but pepper on fruits can be quite nice. First time I experienced was black pepper on a simple piece of melon at a five star French restaurant in Paris. It was an amazing flavor profile.  White “pepper” is not equivalent.  Give it a try!
@Sue I love black pepper and balsamic vinegar on strawberries
Yes lime with the peel

My basil is overproducing, and just culled the mint to keep it in check, so made this with almost entirely basil and it was very good, honestly tasted more mint than basil.  Delicious and will make again.  

People asking what to mix it with - rum or cachaca and reduce the sugar a smidge.

So tart and refreshing, and easy! My edits: made with 6 XXL limes (yes, peels and all), full-fat coconut milk, 50/50 herb mix--less than 1 cup each to avoid overpowering the lime, and only about 3/4 cup sugar because I don't like sweet drinks. I agree with others it seems a waste to throw out the solids! It is excellent as is, but I like cocktails and  taste-tested with white tequila and rum, and rum was definitely the winner. Mine was not as bright green as the photo but was still pretty.
Delicious! Turned my husband’s frown upside down.
This was delicious. Made it as written but I think next time I’d add a little less sugar. Maybe 3/4 cup.

This is absolutely delicious! The combination of lime and coconut is refreshing, but the addition of the black pepper and basil gives it an unexpected kick that makes the flavors pop! I don’t even miss the fact that there is no alcohol!
It feels wasteful to throw away the solids. Any suggestions for using those? (Maybe lime bars or ice cream?)
Was thinking the same thing.
@A slice it to prettify a simple glass of water or muddle it into lemonade
A quick whiz in a blender.
The best cranberry relish always uses the whole orange, peel and all. Otherwise I would question the lime peels, too. Looks delicious!
Instead of all that sugar why not just sweetened coconut milk or sweetened cream of coconut?
@Sandy: but aren’t those things sweetened with sugar?

I haven't made this yet, but this recipe doesn't make sense to me. If I lightly pulse 4 cups of ice, how am I'm going to get that through a fine mesh sieve? it's going to have to melt, so why not just use water and then add ice later?
Pulsing the entire lime into this drink adds an extra layer of brightness and depth from the rind.
Where are the amounts of ingredients. Not a Mention of limes and how many
First ingredient: "7 limes, scrubbed."

This is delicious, but next time I will reduce the salt and use a lower-fat or more homogenized coconut milk.  When I served this, I found that the ice soon caused the coconut cream to gel into unappealing lumps, which made it hard to drink and also grossed out some of the folks to whom I served it.
Would cucumber be good in this?

I loved this, however:
1) didn’t add any mint because I didn’t have any
2) didn’t add peppercorns, just some shakes of pepper 
3) it tasted AMAZING day 1 but incredibly bitter day 2. So I’m not sure it actually would stay good for a few days.
I brought this to a taco pot luck party and everyone was raving about it!  Be aware you can’t make it too far in advance or the lime rind oils go bitter. What a great recipe!!
"a little bit of sugar"? It's over 1 cup for 4 portions. 1/4 cup sugar is NOT a little bit for a single drink!
Amazing drink. Won over two skeptics immediately.
What about using a juicer to create this drink? T
@dede why? I can’t see how that would work. Are you going to put all the ingredients through the juicer? Why create a mess to clean up when it is so easy to throw it all in a blender
@dede If you’re thinking about a vitamix or similar, I think it’s because it’s important not to pulverize the limes too much—the drink gets super bitter if the limes are chopped too finely.
delicious - although it immediately split and became cloudy. not sure why, directions were followed very specifically.
@afj! Same thing happened to me—I blended the strained liquid again after straining and it helped everything come together!

As a diabetic who would very much like to make this drink, can someone tell me if I will get a good outcome with artificial sweetener? Otherwise, I can't handle such a large amount of sugar. - Thank you.
@David B. I made this yesterday with Splenda (sucralose), with absolutely no problems :-).   The drink is lovely
I think I would make this just for what I'm imagining to be an amazing aroma. As a limeade lover, this really kicks it to the next level. Just in time for the end of summer!
Ingredients say 7 limes - What size-ish roughly ? Small vs larger would skew the results I’d think ?
Similar to Limonada recipe on this site without the herbs.

@Garth considering the ingredients I intend to add a good light rum. If you have a HOMEMADE, not bought, high quality coconut liqueur, it could definitely make things interesting, just test and reduce sugar in the lemonade if necessary :) Can't wait to try this out!
Amazing! Made as directed and was delicious. Adding rum next time!
Is this a good way to use up those limes that get a little sad in the drawer (sad=dry) or should I stick with the beautiful freshies for this?

Question:  regular or key limes?  Big difference when you’re using 7 limes!   Can’t wait to try it!  I always make impossible lemon pie in a blender with whole limes!  Peels add so much flavor & texture. Thanks.
my former roommate who was Brazilian made caipirinhas this way, and used key lines. incredible.
my friend josh really loved this
I don't understand the purpose of the ice in step 2. Seems to me to blend, strain and then blend with ice or just pour over cracked ice.
I only used 1/2 cup of sugar and it was perfect. Delicious drink on a 90 degree day!

I’ve made this twice this week. It does get noticeably bitter on day 2, but the addition of some simple syrup tamed it. For the second batch I used leftover sweetened coconut cream from a cake project which I mixed with water until I got the volume of 1 can of coconut milk. Since the coconut cream is very sweet, I adjusted the volume of sugar based on the amount of cream I used, but to equal the amount stated in the ingredients. It was just as delicious.

title: Lemon-Pepper Zucchini Pasta With Dill Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: My recommendation is to double the zucchini, even triple for that amount of pasta. Also, I mixed some of the hot pasta water with the grated pecorino and stirred in off the heat because I’m terrified of the stringy cheese effect…haha
@Hungry Ginger good thinking!! Especially When the other half of the cheese sticks to the pan
Delicious and simple — perfect timing since it’s zucchini season. I added garbanzo beans for extra protein.
4 ounces of pecorino should be about one cup, not more than 3.
@Kbrandin I thought the same.  Could you imagine that much cheese?  Thanks for clarifying.

@Kbrandin
It depends on what kind of grater you use.  I made a half recipe, and 2 oz. of pecorino grated with a microplane zester yielded about a cup and a half of fluffy grated cheese.
@Becky Same here. Which is why weight is a better gauge than volume of grated cheese.
Used basil instead of dill. Tagliatelle instead of spaghetti. Delicious!

@Robin Happy to see I was not the only herb adventurer. My wife is not willing to eat dill, but we had a huge plant of tarragon in the garden that was just right. I almost went to the oregano plant, and might try this again to see if that’s better, but the tarragon was great.

Loved the dill-lemon pasta combo. However, with miso, pecorino and pasta water (and residual salt from zucchini!), this was way too salty. Next time I’ll use less miso and pecorino and add more zucchini.

In August, what is a "large" zucchini anyway? I made this "as is" but next time would start the zucchini frying well before starting the pasta, and I would use more pasta water, as it was not very "saucy."
Loved this pasta, but I suggest adding very little or no salt to the pasta water. As the miso and the salted zucchini have enough salt on their own.
Next time, I will add the cheese before the water and add water gradually. Ended up with clumpy cheese soup. Maybe this one needs a video. Loved the flavors
What would be a good protein to add to this? Shrimp maybe?
@Dr. Miss Sure, shrimp sounds good and would work well with this recipe. My son prefers salmon, so we opted for fish.
@Dr. Miss I aim to try this with smoked salmon
@Dr. Miss I served it with plain sautéed salmon.

This was DELICIOUS! And relatively easy to prepare. I subbed parm for pecorino (forever working through a block from Costco) and 4 oz of it looked like 3.5 cups until I squished it down to pick it up, then it seemed to be about a cup. Next time, I will double the zucchini (it got buried under all the pasta!) and maybe try it under the broiler to get some color on it without the oil spatter and having to work in batches
I found the result way too salty. The concept is good. Will use a less salty cheese next time.
I can confirm that shrimp is great in this!
This was delicious, but it needed three times as much zucchini.

For me the proportion of zucchini and pasta was off - a problem with a vague word like “large.” Too much pasta and not enough zucchini. The amount of cheese seems off too. 4 oz does yield a boatload grated (over 3 cups as the recipe) and it became too salty even when I held back a bit, worrying it might be too much. It was. Frying zucchini also needed to be done in 2 batches (even with an apparently not enough amount). It’s a fine supper but I’m not sure if I’d make again.
does anyone have options for replacement of miso?
My question too. Too salty and I don’t eat soy.
@nyper you can use a mixture of tahini and soy. 1.5 tsp of soy with 1 tbs of tahini for every tbs of miso.
@Nancy Peterson it's possible to find miso made from chickpeas instead of soy.

The flavours generally worked really well but I have to agree with some other commenters that it was just a bit too salty. I also felt that some of the ratios were off - there was so much pasta and too little zucchini. I think if I remade it I would halve the amount of pasta used

After making (and definitely enjoyed it!) I’d double the amount of zucchini next time. I also enjoyed the slimmer slices as they got a little crunchier when fried. I also didn’t have dill and linguini, but parsley and bucatini were great substitutes. The miso cheese sauce was lovely!
I made this with 3 cups of cheese and it was way too cheesy. Lost the taste of all the other ingredients. I would use no more than ONE!
Toss with corsely chopped chopped toasted bread crumbs
It would be helpful if the weight of the zucchini was provided. A "large" zucchini can mean anything. In my garden they can grow to two feet long.
We’re avoiding simple carbs / pasta.  I’d rather substitute the zucchini as THE “pasta”.  Does anyone know how to do that?  (Besides buying another special gadget for my tiny kitchen…)

There is a small zoodle maker shaped like an hour glass and made from plastic on Amazon for about $12. Some grocers also carry fresh zoodles in their produce section, but fresh homemade are so much better and take little prep time.
I used bucatini which went perfectly well.  For my protein I heated some peas and tossed them in at the end.  Great crowdpleaser!
any substitute for miso???
@nyperr There is miso made with chickpeas if you are avoiding soy. If you want to avoid miso all together, not sure what would be a good substitute.
did everyone use a full pound of pasta? I've found a lot of recipes that call for a pound end up being dry (could be user error) so I'm considering using maybe 3/4 instead.
@Justina I used half a pound of pasta because I didn’t have a full pound and it worked perfectly :)
I substituted parmesan for pecorino and made these miso meatballs to go with (but added dill): https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1019762-chicken-miso-meatballs
not my favorite. the miso goes better with parsley in my opinion.

Delicious! And simple too. I doubled the zucchini and halved the pasta (because I only had half a pound of pasta available). At first, 1.5 cups of water seemed like a lot. As it sat for a while, the sauce thickened and stuck to the pasta. And I think using this much water cut the saltiness. If the pecorino is grated finely, I think that will prevent a stringy cheese situation. Enjoyed this very much and will definitely make again!

A quick off-the-cuff calculation for calories in one serving.  4 oz pasta = 400 calories, 1 tbsp olive oil = 120 cal, 1/2 tbsp butter = 50 calories, 3/4 tbsp miso = 25 calories, 1 oz pecorino = 110 calories.  So that's around 700 calories without including the zucchini. So, not 1056 calories but not a light meal!

Julie asked "What is a large zucchini in August anyway?" I just weighed the ones I have from my garden this week (Aug 25), and they range from 8.4 oz to 14 oz to 1.1, 1.5, 1.7 lb, and then there are the garden logs -- 2 lb and 2.2 lb. I'd consider any of the ones over a pound to be large!

Great dish
Salted squash, rinsed and dried thoroughly.
Wasn’t salty at all

Wow, made this with my fiancé for a late night dinner and despite our exhaustion from the day we couldn’t help but scarf this down! Really delicious, we added extra of pretty much everything minus pasta lol

Excellent even without the cheese. Came together quickly. I added smoked salmon, dill and arugula which, I know, is a different recipe but still excellent. Would only substitute butter with olive oil.
We added shrimp to the leftovers and ate a 2nd day.
This recipe was delicious as written.  The sauce was silky and smooth with the umami punch of miso. Perfection for using the dill and zucchini from my garden.

Added asparagus cut into about 1-inch pieces after frying the zucchini, removed from pan once crisp-tender (and still nice and bright), and continued as written. It was a nice addition and went well with all of the other flavors.

I agree with the suggestions of more zucchini, and less than 4 ounces (weight) of cheese. I used Parmesan because that was what I had and a combination of chopped basil, oregano, and thyme from my garden.
No notes. Didn’t salt beyond the water and the zucchini and it was soooo savory and perfect for late summer.
adding some (shelled) edamame would make a good protein addition and nice colour

We loved this dish and will definitely make it again. I made a few changes due to what was in my pantry. I used lime instead of lemon, soy sauce and Nori Komi Furikaki instead of miso. I added about 1/4-1/5 tsp red pepper flakes and grated ginger. We also added shrimp to include more protein. I used 6 oz pasta but followed the full directions (except 1 cup pasta water and 3 oz Pecorino). The lime, red pepper flakes and shrimp were game changer!

LOVED it!  The miso+lemon is an awesome flavor, and goes incredibly well with pasta.  And the salting does help the zucchini stand up to the cooking.  This dish punches above its prep time.  

I made it largely as written but with mods per the comments:
-subbed tarragon, basil, and some mint from the garden (my dill’s done) 
-rinsed the zucchini THEN toweled off; salt level in finished dish was perfect. 
-only used enough water to make the sauce.  Miso needs some water to dissolve; the rest is up to how you like it.  
-And added leftover chicken breast just before the final combination, in zucchini-size pieces.  Worked great.  

This got very good household reviews & I agreed:-)

I agree with others - this needs more zucchini.  I halved the pasta for two of us but used the full amount of zucchini and would have liked even more. I used fresh basil because I had it and used the full amount as well ( did not halve). This is yummy (with more zucchini).

Made this tonight. It’s delicious! However, i wish I’d doubled (or even tripled) the zucchini. It’s delicious in this preparation and especially plentiful at this time of year. (And I doubled the garlic, per usual—I love garlic!)
Once the cheese is added the heat should be turned off to avoid clumping.
Could eggplant be used in place of the zucchini?
This sounds delish but any thoughts for a pasta substitute?  I often use a bed of spinach instead but not sure how to work the miso into that option

This is far too salty. For me, it has about 1.25 days salt in one serving, not that I would eat four ounces of pasta at one time. I will make it with about 6 ounces of pasta for four servings, make sure I have plenty of veggies, zucchini or other, and skip adding any extra salt.

Delicious. As others say, too salty as written so maybe rinse the zucchini after salting and before patting. Pecorino is only 1 cup. Doubled the zucchini, which still felt like too little (don’t be shy). Also added 4 ears of corn kernels. Will make again with adjustments. A splash of white wine in the liquid mix would do nice also.
Any idea for a vegan substitute for the pecorino? I was thinking nutritional yeast but I'm not sure exactly what flavor pecorino should add.
super tasty. agree with other comment re 2 zucchini not 1.

I had a couple of ears of fresh local corn, which I cooked with the zucchini. I thought the whole dish was wonderful. I agree that shrimp would be a great protein to add (I didn't) or possibly ground pork.
Used basil, not dill and tahini and soy sauce mixture instead of miso.  Delicious!

It's amazing that this gets 5 stars as there are many issues with the recipe including some confusion noted in other comments. For me the sauce came out like a soup texture, not creamy at all. If you put the pasta and zucchini in, as stated, it's going to tend to overcook everything. If I were to cook it again, I'd leave salt out of the pasta water and ideally use unsalted butter. I'd also recommend reducing the pasta water and as others noted, would double the zucchini.

I followed the recipe exactly except I did not have any fresh dill so I substituted fresh basil from the garden.  Wonderful!  I  would be careful with the salt, though. Miso is pretty salty on its own. Don't salt the pasta water too much.
Re miso, if you can't eat soy, try a chickpea miso (Miso Master is good). I like the taste better anyway.
I used 3 little zucchini and didn't have dill so used a combo of basil and cilantro and it was still very delicious!

I too recommend doubling the zucchini. Could totally see this working with almost any fresh herb but I used the dill. I think doubling that would work out fine. I really liked the dish but a bit fussy with the boiling and the frying and the saucing and the tossing. Read the recipe carefully. There are ways to simplify like just throw the zest and pepper in the sauce with all the other sauce ingredients. No point in pre-mixing. Yummy but probably won’t make it again.

delicious! next time, i'll use two large zucchinis because one wasn't enough. or i'd add shrimp or salmon. also, more pasta water since the pasta was a little too dry. a pound of spaghetti is a lot and it absorbs that sauce quick!

We loved the flavor of this dish! I used fettuccini and added red pepper flakes to the sauce. I used two small to medium zucchini cut into thick half-moons and this filled a large pan and browned up nicely in one batch. I didn't use about 1/4 lb of the cooked pasta and the dish came out beautifully. Zesty and delicious.
When salting vegetables to draw out moisture, should you rinse them after the 10 min? I've always wondered.
@Emily F I would imagine rinsing them would reintroduce the water! Hence the pat method.
Excellent!  We used twice as much zucchini and half as much pasta so it was very flavorful.
who would've thought miso and dill would go so well together! i quadrupled the zucchini and doubled the dill/lemon, using only a tablespoon of oil to fry it up.
WAY more zucchini, WAY less miso and more lemon.

Loved this easy and tasty sauce for pasta. Used scant measurement for miso and no salt in pasta water and that was just right.  Basil and parmesan are what I had on hand and they were great.Agree with others that, next time, I want more zucchini.
Definitely add more zucchini or whatever veggie you have on hand.  I used a giant zucchini and a half head of broccoli to rave reviews.
I forgot the garlic and it was delicious. Reminded me of a superb parmigiana sauce I had in Venice.
I agree with your other readers/cooks.  There wasn't enough zucchini for all that pasta.  And three 1/2 cups of cheese?  We love it and used a lot, but not nearly that much!
Sadly it was a greasy mess in the kitchen and on the plate. Way too salty. Way too rich.

yet another 5 star review! made this last night. not sure how large is large when it comes to zucchini so decided to err on the side of more zucchini. the salting method changes how I feel about zucchini. 

also went a bit heavy on the garlic and butter. cheese happened by volume, but in the future I'll do by weight. this is a total winner in my book.

I agree with the comment to increase the zucchini.  I doubled it and added extra dill and zest.  I would add more next time.  As I ate, I thought some seared mushrooms would also be nice. I bought lemon torchietti pasta on impulse at Costco yesterday and used that, which made a beautiful dish to look at.

This was quite good. I grilled some shrimps in the pan, set them in a bowl before doing the zucchini, then put the cooked zukes aside with them and followed the rest of the recipe. It made for a nice complete meal. My only complaint is the miso seemed a bit overpowering and I’ll cut the amount to 2 tbsp next time.

Loved this recipe. It is so customizable but also delicious and simple on its own. The only change I made was to grill the zucchini which gave it a smoky aspect. I love miso and how it gives a subtle sweet/salty taste. I can see adding salmon, shrimp or chicken to this.

I made this for the first time so I tend to follow the recipe pretty closely amd I found it simple and delicious! However, I, too, think more zuchini is a good idea given the amount os spaghetti suggested!

Delicious but messy. I used a lot less pasta and less cheese. Frying the zucchini is a big spatter mess and you certainly can't get all that zucchini in one layer of a large cast iron skillet. I like the idea of putting the zucchini under the broiler. Perhaps after brushing with oil? Then put a small amount of oil in the pan for before throwing all the ingredients together. Trader Joes shelf stable white miso paste mixes in easily without lumps.

I made this tonight. It tasted good. Used parmasan. A bit too salty. Could have used  more zucchini.
I
Make sure the dish doesn't end up too salty! I'll scale back on salt in my pasta water next time. It was yummy, though!
I added a mushroom medley to the zucchini.

Liked this but not blown away. Stick with half inch slices. Thinner resulted in less zucchini flavor, more zucchini mush. Microplane cheese and add in portions to taste.  Dill got lost without fresh squeeze of lemon and dill garnish.

SO good. Still, could reduce the olive oil, increase the zucchini and cut down on the miso/butter a tiny bit next time. had it as a main but would make it next time with a piece of fish. Also - my 3 year old son loved it!

Made this tonight. Did not salt the zucchini or the pasta water. Browned zucchini in a little oil, removed from pan, then sautéed some chopped onion, broccolini and mushrooms and put that in bowl w zucchini and dill. Made the miso sauce, then added the cooked noodles, then the cheese, and finally stirred all the other veggies in. Not watery, not lumpy, and VERY good. Thanks for all the tips that helped!
This recipe sounded great with all it's little dash of this and that, but somehow when I made it it turned out to have a bitter flavor and I couldn't figure out what I might have done wrong?
You got lemon pith in with the zest.

Top marks! I added more zucchini because it cooks down a lot, and it was delicious. I also threw in some snow peas for more green and a little crunch, some jarred roasted artichokes for a little tang, and added fresh oregano to the spice bowl. My miso was less salty than most brands, but taste it first along with the pecorino/parmesan to watch the sodium levels. Definitely top it with a healthy spray of fresh ground black pepper! This will be in constant summer rotation.
4 ounces of cheese is 1/2 cup … not 3-1/2 cups.

@Cheryl
The volume of grated cheese depends on how and when it is grated. Freshly and finely grated hard cheese has a lot of volume compared to its weight.
You are conflating volume vs weight.
We need to use more zucchinni.
My family loved this. I used 3 zucchini and zested 2 lemons.
My husband and I thought it was for not much return with this one. Would not make again.
Something is very off in the recipe because there’s no way 4oz of Pecorino is equal to 3.5 cups?? (Also 3.5 cups would be insane for this amount pasta?)
Terrific dish for zucchini! We have made it twice in two weeks. We add a pound of shrimp to get some protein. The miso is a great addition, we will be using it in more dishes.

I would triple the zucchini. I thought, otherwise, that it was a very easy weeknight dish. Don’t be like me and not have any fresh herbs in your fridge - this needs and wants a citrusy green kick. I only had parsley and it was good, but dill or basil would have definitely been better.
Did not like the lemon in this but husband did.
Superb!  Miso adds a nice creaminess.  Will use more zucchini next time, likely next week when I make it again!
A simple, delicious way to impress with minimal ingredients! I would add more zucchini and shrimp for some added protein!

This is delicious - made it on the recommendation of a friend.  Doubled the zucchini and cooked on a sheet pan 450F.  I can understand why folks add shrimp, would go along well with the flavor profile.
I doubled the zucchini in this - so tasty!
Great dish!  If you use 1lb of pasta make sure you double all the other ingredients.  You’ll be thrilled you did.

FANTASTIC. I roasted the squash in the oven @425 convection for ease.  

Next time I’ll add 2x as much squash.

I don't know how large the zucchini are in the US, but here in Australia I used two "large" zucchini and it still was not enough for the quantity of pasta. Would use three next time. Otherwise delicious, however test the miso as you add it as some brands can be saltier.
Doubled the zucchini and tore some fresh basil over it at the end, perfection

Made for a crowd and everyone enjoyed! I added mushrooms and increased zucchini, per the comment suggestions which was a great call. I also used basil and omitted amount all of the oil and didn't salt the pasta water to keep the sodium a little lower. Excited to eat the leftovers!
3 cups is WAY too much cheesy - i did about a third of that and it was good.

This was good, and I will make it again - a few notes (mostly for myself). Don't use the mandolin, it makes it way too thin and falls apart when frying. I used two med zucchini and it wasn't enough. You really only need 1 TB of miso paste, also suggest adding some lemon juice to the miso mix to help balance it, and maybe some chili flakes for some zing!

This pasta was perfection. I would recommend using bucatini to soak up the sauce. I loved the zucchini, and although mine got a bit burned, it was still amazing. I didn't add the dill which I thought was a good choice but it probably would have been delicious with the dill. The lemon, cream, zucchini ratio was perfect.

This was excellent.  Took pasta quantity down for 2, using 1-1/2 zucchinis, 1 T butter and 2 T miso but used 3 small garlic cloves!  Reduced pasta water and cheese as well. After browning the zucchini, I sautéed mushrooms, keeping the zucchini warm in the bowl with the zest, dill and pepper. At the end, I squeezed half a lemon before serving and adding the dill and more cheese. Next time, I would up the lemon zest and consider adding lemon paste at the same time of the miso, as we love lemon!

Excellent recipe! I used 2 zucchinis, added a little extra garlic, and mixed some sour cream into the sauce. I broiled the zucchini instead of frying it, which made the whole process much easier. I’ll definitely be making this again.

Double or triple the amount of zucchini.

Mushrooms would also be good in this recipe

Also makes a good cold noodle salad.

title: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Could you use chunks of pork tenderloin instead?
@Up north I was wondering the same, as I have some on hand.
Definitely  not. Just don’t do that. Pork tenderloin is far too lean and you’ll ruin a perfectly nice piece of meat. Either use a fatty cut or move on to another recipe.
@Up north nowhere near fatty enough.  If you want a lower fat substitute then I’d use duck breast but with some duck schmaltz to add the needed fat.  But only if you absolutely must.

Just cooked this with a few modifications, and it turned out delicious.  Harris Teeter didn’t have pork belly, so I got a strip of fatty bacon and lean pork chops instead.  I also used only two large, fist-size black plums, but added two small zucchinis, a vidalia onion, and two small sweet peppers.  As for the sequence, first I made the sauce as suggested in the recipe, then cooked one bacon strip cut in pieces and chopped onion for maybe 5 minutes, then added pork chops sliced in thumb-sized chunks and sliced sweet peppers.  Added a bit of sauce as there wasn’t enough fat in the wok.  Cooked for six minutes, then added two black plums cut in small pieces, cooked for a couple of minutes, then added sliced zucchini and the remaining sauce and cooked 5 minutes longer.  Added cilantro on top before serving.  Next time I might add a bit of honey or brown sugar, but it was really good as described.

Has this actually been tested? Not a fan. 
After browning the pork there is a LOT of fat left in the pan. This needs to be drained off or the rest of the recipe flops - plums don’t caramelize and the sauce doesn’t thicken. 
Also think the red wine vinegar is too much with the plums.
@Ray I agree. Recipe did not work as written. Meat and fat were tough, sauce didn’t caramelize.
@Ray I have found caramelising doesn’t work in cast iron. Use a stainless steel pan.
Substituted pork shoulder country style spareribs prepared as instrcted.  Nice dish with both bold and subtle flavors.

Made this recipe last night and both my husband and I thought it was fabulous!!!  I followed the recipe exactly. I had never cooked pork belly before and since it looked very fatty when raw, I was concerned that there might be a lot of fat left in the pan after browning but this was NOT my experience.  I needed the additional TBS of oil to caramelize the plums. Served over rice, topped with a big handful of arugula. Fast, easy and SO delicious!!
I don't eat pork, beef, lamb or veal. Would chicken work? Or cauliflower? Or...? Thanks.
so many questions about using other ingredients.  Why not just try it and see?
@Marla I’m thinking about making this Vegan by using extra-firm tofu.  Could include Cauliflower too!
I am Plant Based. I substituted the pork for shiitake mushrooms and reduced the fish sauce to 1 Tablespoon. It was delicious.
I roasted a whole pork tenderloin & sliced it before serving. For that amount of pork, I should have 2x the sauce components. Also, slice the plums into 6 pieces as quartered were too large. Delish.

Made this for dinner last week and we loved it. I used a wok, and also opted for pork tenderloin (pork belly was unavailable and I’d taken note of the comments about too much fat). Other than that, made the recipe exactly as written. It was delicious. Salty, sour, sweet, spicy. In other words, umami goodness. I think this would work great, too, with cherries. Maybe sub in duck breast for the pork? Also a combination of chicken and peaches would be nice. In any event, the flavors are great, and we’ll definitely make this again.
what is the serving amount?

Hi Up North You could, but the texture and cooking time will be different. You will wind up with different results. Pork tenderloin will get tough and dry if overcooked and will not get crispy because there is no fat. (Pork belly is about 50% fat).

I didn't have pork belly, so I rendered fat from 3 pieces of thick-cut chopped bacon, removed the bacon then cooked 1lb ground pork in the bacon fat, broken into bit-size chunks . Returned the crispy bacon bits at the end after the sauce has cooked down. Served over rice. Would definitely make again, as it was relatively simple with lots of flavor.

@TW We just made this and the pork belly was essentially inedible for me. It was tough and didn’t add any flavor to the dish. I was thinking that ground pork would be good. I like your adaptation and would try that next time because I loved the sauce and plums, but couldn’t eat the pork belly at all.

Cooked it as written, but used my wok. Others were right, you will need to drain off some of the pork fat before adding the plums. I would change the ratios - more pork, less plums. Liked it, but something was off about the sauce. Might sub rice wine vinegar for the red wine vinegar next time.
Used a mix of pork belly and cutlet to reduce the fat amount. It was a bit tart (but still good) so important to dose vinegar carefully.

I loved this dish. I used pork loin instead of pork belly. Much healthier and took care of the oily/fatty issue many people complained about. Serviced over arugula with roasted potatoes. My wife thought the dressing was a bit too strong, but I tend to like vinegar-based sauces and pickled vegetables. If vinegar isn't your thing, than cutting back or adding more sugar/honey could be considered.
I used pork belly; it seems that that is the whole point of the recipe.  No one found it too fatty; just get some pork belly where the amount of fat is not very disproportionate to the lean meat.
I also am looking for an alternative to pork belly which is just too fatty for me. Maybe pork shoulder?  I think tenderloin would be too lean.

@JillC

Oooh, but check out what Betsy said! Interesting!

@JillC

I’d skip the pork altogether and switch to chicken. You’ll probably need to add some neutral tasting oil to blister the plums. 

I don’t think pork shoulder will work here because it needs slower cooking methods. 

If you do it, please report back! I’ve been sort of off the taste of pork lately, so I’d love to hear back from someone who tries doing this with chicken.

@cocomero pork shoulder was ah-mazing. I cut it into thick slices maybe 2x3 and 1/2 to 3/4 inch thick. I think I did everything else by the book. served with pan-roasted potatoes with Penzey's Forward seasoning, roasted garlic, turmeric, and minced onion, and some of the scallions. So incredibly tasty. Will make it again and again.
@Ray My first thought was to make this with a juicy boneless pork chops, like a Kurobota, or a chop from my local pork butcher. Still fatty, but definitely not pork belly.

Love it! I find it can be hard to find that “sweet spot” when sautéing pork belly for this amount of time. Part of me wants to sear it, and then slow-braise it, but I suppose that misses the point. Thanks for sharing!
Would love tips on making this with trader joe precooked ( but not browned) pork bell which I happen to have in the fridge.

I cooked this exactly as the recipe was written. I found it to be quite delicious and quite frankly I can’t imagine cooking this with chicken as I don’t think chicken really pairs well with fruit.  my pork belly was a little fatty if I make this again, I most likely will omit the canola oil when cooking the plums.

We made this tonight for a quick dinner and loved it!  As warned in other comments, reduced vinegar by half - good call.  For ease of hunting and gathering, used thick cut bacon vs pork belly.  Doubled the garlic, ginger and scallions and omitted all oil as the pork fat was more than sufficient (actually drained some).  We used lovely local peaches and went with rice noodles this time, although rice would be very nice as well, I think.
Could you use chicken instead?

@Ruth — I used chicken when I made this tonight. Boneless, shingles, cut into larger chunks. Seared at high temp with some peanut oil then made pretty much as suggested, except I took the meat out a little early, reduced the sauce a bit, then added the sauce back to the chicken. It was great.

I'm confused about the number of servings.  The recipe says that it serves 4, which makes sense for one pound of meat.  The nutritional information says that it is based on 12 servings -- not too easy to do with 1 pound of meat and 6 plums!  Pat Gericke seems to wonder about this too.

Sautéed 1/2 a yellow onion and chopped garlic before adding the pork. I used more pork (1.5 lbs), sautéed in two batches. Used 5 rather than 6 plumbs as I added a small zucchini. Otherwise did the recipe as directed and it came out fantastic. Definitely worth repeating.

It made a good meal but the recipe needs some tweaks.  Step 4 should take much less time or the sauce will completely dry up, I added vermouth to keep things loose. Next time I’ll have some plum wine on hand and cook longer in step 3 and for less time in step 4. I used half the vinegar and it was perfect for us. The plums I used were very firm, I’m going to try next time with riper plums and see if they leave a richer sauce. The arugula was a perfect accompaniment but we dressed the leaves simply

Agree that it's better to err on the side of less cooking time overall so as not to overcook the pork.  But important to get pork lightly browned and edges crispy in Step 2.  I might experiment with removing the pork for part of the Step 3 process (i.e. when caramelizing the plums).

Recipe says to mix all of the sauce ingredients ahead of time ... I'd go light on the red wine vinegar, because unless your plums are super sweet ... it can get awfully tangy.  
Served with rice and Asian stir fried green beans.  Quite delicious, if a bit tangy.  We used tiny Japanese Shiro plums (yellow) and subbed about 6 for each normal sized plum in the recipe (friend has a tree just loaded with these gorgeous little plums).  
We used a pork tenderloin and added a tiny bit of fat.  Delicious

I used this as a starting point, replacing pork belly with thinly-sliced tenderloin I seared on high, then transferred to a plate while I cooked the other ingredients. I replaced part of the fish sauce with more soy sauce and the red wine vinegar with rice vinegar (2 tablespoons only) and I served everything on Korean glass noodles with a sprinkling of gochugaru. Make sure you taste your plums first and adjust the sugar accordingly.

This looks like a really good recipe.   It as I am on a low sodium diet for health reasons, I thought the nutritional information of a serving was a little low.

Fish sauce, one of my very favorite but hard to incorporate ingredients, has between 1400 and 1500 milligrams of sodium in a tablespoon.  And the recipe says it makes 4 servings.

But the nutritional information says the recipe makes 12 servings.

Please clarify which one is correct.  Thank you.

This recipe is in regular rotation. I sub pork shoulder for the pork belly, rice vinegar for the red wine vinegar, and palm sugar for the brown sugar. I also top with sesame seeds, which makes it look much prettier.

I have made this with pork tenderloin and it was wonderful. I sautéed the tenderloin strips first, removed them from the pan and proceeded with the recipe, then added the pork back at the end to pull it together. Very tasty and less fat.
Did this with tofu rather than pork.  Very good.
Adjust fat and expect less flavor from that cut. It dries out quickly so there’s that.
Or sliced boneless skinless chicken thighs?
Barbara, I think it would be very good with chicken thighs or even chicken breasts.  I'm going to try with boneless pork loin chop and second time with chicken thighs.

I think you could do it with dark meat chicken, but you’d want skin-on, bone-in thighs. This recipe depends on the fat to make it work. No way would boneless white meat or even dark meat  chicken work, nor would a pork tenderloin, as someone else suggested. Both are far too lean.
Chicken would work

This really didn’t work. Meat was tough, fat did not stay tender, caramelization did not happen.  This seemed like an easier option to try than a long slow roast or braise, but the result after following recipe as written was inedible. At very least needs more detail about how much fat to trim, how much oil to pour off, etc. The pan was too crowded and keeping it low to keep meat  tender  meant the sauce never caramelized even after dumping half or more of the fat / liquid. & the meat still came out tough.
Need to cook at high temps to brown. Would decrease red wine vinegar to 1/8 c,  increase brown sugar,  reduce cooking time for plums and switch to a wok vs cast iron.
Could you use Beef as a substitute?
Can someone comment on reduced fat pork recipes?  New cuts dry out and taste like sawdust when used in oldies but goodies?  Ie chops smothered in onions!!

I made the recipe as described, substituting nectarines for the plums as suggested. I've never cooked with pork belly before. My husband and I both LOVED it, but boy was it fatty!! Our stomachs felt rather heavy and nauseous after. I'd have to follow some of these other ideas next time to use a less fatty meat. The sauce is excellent, and worked so great with the meat and the fruit. It all caramelized perfectly.
Are you supposed to set the pork aside when cooking the plums ?

Made it as written except with a pork tenderloin and extra oil instead of pork belly. Delicious!!! Next time I might add some bacon or something to add a bit more fat, but if you don’t overcook, the tenderloin works fine. Definitely a keeper!

Made the glaze in the pan with the plums and pork chops. Amazing! Pork belly is too fatty for my husband and this recipe works brilliantly with bone in chops. Just enough fat to cook the plums, no extra oil, and the glaze coated them beautifully!
Use lime juice and Asian vinegar with soy sauce as substitute for fish sauce
I used pork the supermarket sold as "stir fry," and it was fine.  Also, I did stir once while the plums were cooking.  This was a wonderful meal.

I am a newbie to Pork belly but I bought a slab at the farmer’s market today.and operated on it. We loved it. The recipe worked as written and since it was a 2 pound piece of meat, we have dinner for another night. Next time I will definitely stop by my local H mart in Yonkers and buy it there since no surgery would be necessary. Thank you for the tip!

Just made this and I think it's a good recipe.  I drained about 90% of the pork fat and halved the amount of neutral oil, although I'm not sure about adding any additional oil at all.  The sauce thickened nicely and it was a hit at the table.

This was a hit!! Made the recipe exactly as written although I added 1/4 tsp Chinese five spice to the sauce. I also removed the rind from the pork belly along with a bit of fat and put that in a ceramic cast iron skillet in the oven at 450 for 30 minutes to create cracklin!
Served over basmati rice and a hispi cabbage chiffonade lightly sauteed in olive oil with the cracklin on the side.
One of the best NYT recipes!
How would rice noodles be with this recipe?
@Margo N Rice vermicelli is even better than angel hair pasta here, and so easy to make. Put the vermicelli in a broad bowl and spoon on the pork and enjoy dinner with your family and friends

Gorgeous.  Not a low-fat option, but no one will leave unhappy.  I use eight purple Italian plums rather than six black plums - a little less sweet.  And rice vermicelli is the best pasta complement that I’ve found.

I made this with pork cut off of pork chops, followed the rest as written. It was delicious. The pork didn't brown up as much as it would have with pork belly but there was a lot less fat. Flavor was delicious!

Second time making this and I liked the results a little better! First time it was all a little too mushy and sweet (but still amazing). This time I let the pork belly get a little bit crisper on the edges, used very firm plums, and did 1 tbsp of brown sugar and a heaping tsp of gochugaru. The sweetness was better balanced! Served with watercress.
Delicious! I used half the sugar and the plums still made it very sweet. Pork belly was great, but I think pork butt or ground pork would be good.

This is insanely good! Made exactly as written, except I used Trader Joe’s pork belly, which is only 12 oz. Also, used Italian plums from our tree. Did not need to add any additional oil after the pork belly step. Served over steaming white rice and raw arugula. Note to plant-based folks: I bet extra-firm tofu would be outrageously excellent for this sauce.

And, the perfect dessert would be a slice of that infamous Plum Torte!

Served to company; everyone loved it.  Bought 1.5 lbs of pre-diced pork belly from an Asian supermarket; it was the right amount of meat for 4 hungry people.  Tried to get a package that was not TOO fatty.  Cut plums smaller.  Served with arugula and taglietelle. Next time I'd serve in bowls for easier mixing.  Also 1.5 times the sauce to have enough. There was just the right amount of fat; the plums caramelized nicely in the large cast-iron pan.  Very tasty; quick; will make again!

The recipe says it serves 4--and that seems reasonable--but the nutrition information says it gives 12 servings. I can do the math myself (770 or so calories per serving when divided in 4) but I sure wish the two numbers were consistent...I'm making this tonight, exactly as written, so I'll find out if the calories are even relevant to me in the future!

Hubby and I both loved this and I'll make it again. The odd thing is that there was nearly NO fat in the pan when it was time to add the plums. I had cut a 1-pound piece off a 5-pound pork belly, and cut it off the more lean side, so perhaps had I cut it off the other side, there would have been the amount of fat I expected and others have reported?

Delicious. I think you could sub some other pork but it would not be as good. 

You have to remove the pork from the pan and drain off most of the fat before caramelizing the plums. There is not a pan big enough to fit both and do this properly. 

A squeeze of lime before serving brightened it up nicely.

Made this other night as plums are in season right now and as Molly Baz says “you should be eating as much stone fruit as possible”.. I digress, we made this as instructed. There wasn’t excess fat as other mentioned and it was a solid “weekday meal”. 
The pork belly fat wasn’t able to render and tenderize as much as other methods so I do wonder if this dish would be better served with a ground protein? The sauce is versatile and overall quite tasty. We felt it needed a crunch texture so added peanuts to serve.

I cook pork belly dishes frequently. In many recipes that call for pork belly, such as twice-cooked pork, the belly is simmered in water and then chilled before slicing. Also, if the belly is particularly fatty, most cooks will trim excess fat before simmering.

In my experience, pork belly cooked this way (just taking raw pork belly and fry directly) will be super tough/chewy; I routinely double cook mine, first several hours in the crock pot until the meat is tender, cool it, then cut it up and brown in a skillet.  Thoughts?  Am I missing something?

This was really really good!  

I would check the amount of fat in the pan after crisping the belly and before adding more - it ended up being a bit much.  I would also add way more red pepper.  

but this is definitely going into rotation and will be served to guests.
This was absolutely delicious! I followed the recipe - no substitutions.  Easy and tasty!

Boneless country ribs worked well; nice flavor--and tender--without so much fat.  Sauce is highly seasoned; not sure plums stand up to it.  Served over rice with micro greens on top.  It was quite good but next time I'll use fresh pineapple.
I did drain most of the fat after cooking the pork belly. I substituted rice vinegar for the red wine vinegar. I thought it was one of the best things I’ve ever made.

When I saw that several commenters reported that the the pork belly was tough and rubbery, I was not surprised. So I covered the chunks of pork belly with cold water in a saucepan and brought it to a moderate boil for 10 minutes, then rinsed and covered them in cold water again and simmered them for an hour. I dried them completely before searing in a T. of oil in a stainless steel skillet until crispy. I thought the sauce was skimpy, so I added 2 T. of chicken stock. The result was perfection.

Was so good! Easy to make, and you can set up some of it in advance, making it easier. My husband usually says "it's okay" when he likes it. This one was "really good!" so he must have thought it was amazing. Definitely need to remake this again!
Sensational. Made with a peach. Reminded me of Orange Chicken. Delicious and fast. Used a large stainless steel heavy bottom pot because cleaning caramelized cast iron can be tough.

Doubled the ginger to replace the garlic and parsley instead of scallions (allergic to alliums). My plums were very ripe and almost disintegrated; next time I'll use less ripe fruit. I chunked the pork loin chops I had on hand; less fatty than belly slices, but still needed to remove a little fat. However, the sweet/sour/sticky sauce was wonderful - next time, I'll double it. Served with rice and a rocket & watercress salad; my husband requested mashed potatoes next time.  Yum!

I used half tenderloin, half pork belly to make it a bit less fatty - cooked tenderloin in the pan first until browned (a couple minutes) removed, followed the recipe as is, and then added it back in with the sauce in at the end

title: Bratwurst With Caramelized Onions and Chakalaka Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: To clarify the timing:  25 minutes to make the bratwurst and caramelized onions; 40 minutes to make the chakalaka.  The chakalaka can be made in days in advance, but you still gotta make it to complete this dish.
I live in Wisconsin, Brats are grilled never fried in a pan.  They may be parboiled with onion before grilling.
Where can I find chakalaka?

This is a great recipe. However, the order of cooking ingredients seems wrong to me. Per the recipe, I browned the buns and brats first, set them aside to sauté the onions. Everything was mise en place, so there was no wasted time. However, by the time the onions were sautéed and spiced, the brats and rolls were cold. 
I suggest sauteeing the onions first, stashing them in a warming drawer or 200deg. oven, then browning the rolls and brats. That way, everything is nice and warm when you eat it.
Btw, the spiced onions were delicious. 
Didn’t make the chakalaka as, here in the Northeast, in January, summer veggies are very pretty but tasteless. I’ll wait for my summer garden veggies to make that.
@Helen Ullric the recipe calls for the bratwurst to stay in the pan while you cook the onions. Also, the chakalaka is part of the recipe; don’t say you made the recipe if you did not.

This recipe is delicious! 
Just one note: When I turned up the heat to medium-high and added butter, the butter burned, setting off the smoke detector. I turned the heat down and cooked the sausages a few minutes longer than recommended with a lid covering them for the last few minutes. 
It still turned out amazing, scorched butter and all!

Thank you for this!  Just seeing the recipe brings back happy memories of my time at a wonderful wildlife preserve/lodge in South Africa, at which Eggs Chakalaka quickly became my favorite at the brunch that followed our early-morning wildlife outings.  Now I will try it at home.
It will taste nothing like chakalaka at a SA game reserve.  The authentic recipe does not contain beans, garam masala, carrots, ginger, lime or peppers.
@Ano what’s the original recipe? I don’t like peppers

For a little more authenticity, I’d swap the garam masala for curry powder and bay leaves - that will get you a better flavour profile. Yellow onions are more commonly used, but red onions would probably be delicious! Whatever you have can work honestly, as long as it’s tomatoey and spicy and simple and moreish! It’s not one exact dish, everyone has their own recipe. That said I’ve never heard of adding limes haha - I should try it. Can recommend hollowing out a bread roll and filling it with chakalaka as a snack/lunch while camping.
Very good! With a tip of the hat to Sly and the Family Stone, BOOM, chakalaka! (Sorry, I couldn't resist.)

I loved this!  We have so many seasonal veggies in our garden—tomatoes, peppers, carrots to use-- and I even added shredded cabbage. I also added a can of rinsed garbanzo beans. We don't eat onions or garlic (FODMAPs), but it is still very flavorful with the lime juice, ginger, peppers, garam marsala, and fresh sweet tomatoes.  LOVE this on toast for breakfast in the morning. A great way to take a break from oatmeal this summer. My friend from S. Africa loves this Chakalaka.
South African garam masala is different from that found in India (or the US).  Any good substitutions?

@MJ I would make your own if you have a spice grinder- it is super easy and well worth the effort. Off the top of my head, the recipe I use calls for a cinnamon stick, cloves, coriander and cumin seeds, dried red chile and bay leaves.
It does not exist in an authentic South Africa chakalaka.

I'm not going to wade into the authenticity debate. I was looking for a vegetable stew like a ratatouille. 
Totally delivered on flavor - it is spicy ( I used 1 red jalapeno instead of the Thai peppers) and added more garum masala and some curry powder. Can't wait to use it for breakfast on toast with a fried egg

I spent ten days in Jo-burg and Cape Town and tried every restaurants’ chakalaka! I used curry powder > garam masala, light on the lime juice, and added two bay leaves. I noticed chakalaka had a sweet taste and all the other recipes online called for baked beans so I added some honey as well- delicious!

Made 1/2 the recipe tonight - as directed, with Pinto beans - dry pinto beans, not soaked overnight, stovetop with herbs, bayleaf, garlic, lightly salted…served with Yolanda’s Bratwurst with Caramelized Onions and Chakalaka…so very delicious…we used sweet potatoes chips to dip the Chakalaka - its so good - amazing…!

This was great. I added curry as well as garum masala and skipped the beans. It's great with any meat, on toast with an egg on top or as a zippy sandwich enhancement. 
We all need more of this in our lives
This was my FAVE part of any braii. Best with pap or steamed bread. Mouth is watering!!
Can you use Olive oil or avocado oil instead?
Of course, those are both vegetable oils anyway
What could you substitute for the peppers? It says it’s a flexible recipe, but it doesn’t seem like the peppers can be switched out. I’m not a big fan of bell peppers

According to other recipes, cabbage is common in addition to or instead of the bell pepper. I also enjoyed replacing the bell peppers with mild chiles (poblanos and Big Jims, because that's what I had), since I don't enjoy bell peppers but love chiles.

Made this for dinner tonight. Delicious (if a bit time consuming). One humble suggestion: I used green tomatoes that were just starting to pinken, as I had no ripe ones, and I think they really worked in this dish.

title: Suya Spiced Grilled Chicken Thighs With Nectarines Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: The nectarine salad was good but felt somewhat random. The absolute star is the suya spice, and the chicken is delicious.
"Musky, earthy and complex, this Nigerian staple gains its heat from chiles". But there are no chiles in the suya spice recipe. Hmm.

Per other recipe - 
¼cup roasted unsalted peanuts
1teaspoon ground ginger
1teaspoon smoked paprika
1teaspoon granulated garlic 
½teaspoon granulated onion
½teaspoon ground cayenne
½teaspoon kosher salt (such as Diamond Crystal)
The smoked Paprika is from chili peppers
Paprika is made from ground chile peppers :)

I grilled mangoes instead of nectarines, omitted sugar and increased the rice vinegar. How to grill a mango:
I used 2 Atulfo mangoes, sliced thick sides off the pit and grilled them cut side down. After cooling I cut 3/4” crosshatch just through the flesh to skin, turned the fruit inside out and sliced the chunks off the skin.

The combination of roasted grilled stone fruit, a ginger-forward spice blend, and marinated thighs was beloved by my four dinner guests.  We put the suja on the table and people sprinkled it on as desired.
this the suya spice blen in the tip is missing chilli powder

@Mayeni
The recipe calls for using 1 tsp of suya spice blend, and in that blend is the chili. Most suya spice blends I’ve seen have cayenne and paprika in them.

¼cup roasted unsalted peanuts
1teaspoon ground ginger
1teaspoon smoked paprika
1teaspoon granulated garlic 
½teaspoon granulated onion
½teaspoon ground cayenne
½teaspoon kosher salt (such as Diamond Crystal)
Thank you for the recipe for suya. I hate have to buy a bottle of a spice I will use only once or a few times. Much easier to make what I need when I need it.

Loved the suya spice. We added heat per our individual tastes. The peanuts temper the spiciness so you get beautiful flavor in each bite without your mouth burning. It’s peach season so the sweetness was a beautiful compliment to the suya. This will be in a regular rotation as the suya ingredients are standard in our pantry. More African recipes please. Jollof rice next?

Lots of quibbles about the Suya combo.  These recipes are just “suggestions.”  Tweak it the way you want, but it’s still a great recipe and I’m savoring the outcome. Try it with Romano-bean succotash seasoned with ras el hanout.
This recipe was incredible. I’d make it again and, since I made a large batch of suya spice, I’ll certainly do so. Thanks so much.

Several have commented that some heat should be added to the suya mix.  The recipe calls for smoked paprika, so use a hot smoked paprika.  Look for pimentón picante and adjust heat according to your preference.

This exceeded expectations in every way. Made everything as directed and it was phenomenal. Actually, the only change I did was to add about 1/2 tsp of cayenne pepper to the spice blend. This is one of my new favorite recipes from NYT and I will cook again.
Suspect Suya spice mix is missing cayenne pepper or something similar
We loved this dish. Made as directed and served with coconut rice.

Fantastic! I want to put suya on everything! I used hot smoked paprika in the spice blend, which I highly recommend. It gives a bit of heat, but it's not too spicy for those who don't like too much heat. I marinated boneless chicken thighs for 12 hrs. Grilled peaches and made the salad as directed, and grilled the chicken. Served on a large platter with chicken, peach salad with labneh dollops, and then sprinkled everything with more suya and cilantro.
Made as directed.  The suya powder sprinkled on as a condiment is a must - it takes the recipe from”Yum!” To “WOW!”  The grilled nectarines were amazing!
In this recipe for suya spiced salmon, the suya blend includes cayenne: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026992-crispy-suya-spiced-salmon?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share
We cooked exactly as written. Nothing to change as this is taste perfection!
paprika is made from chilis @Anthony
OK Kate. I will rephrase. There are no hot chiles in the recipe, thus "gains its heat from chiles" is not correct.

There are chilis in the recipe, via the suya spice blend. As described in the tip, cayenne and paprika are included and both are chilis. As far as missing chili powder, most chili powders are a spice blend which include a blend of chilis, but cumin, garlic, onions, etc. Since the the tip describes a blend itself, using chili powder would throw things off. Now, substituting another ground chili, say Allepo, that might be interesting...
Instead of fruit, has anyone tried another veggie in its place?
Sadly Penzey's doesn't carry Suya or Yaji.
So don't be sad: Google Suya. You'll find it online.

Delicious! I marinated the chicken for 8 hours, but I see where a full 24 hours would only add to the deliciousness. My propane tank conked out before I could grill anything, so I cooked the chicken in a cast iron pan with another on top for crispy edges. I cooked the fruit and scallions in the broiler. The nectarine salad was lovely. I like Lucia’s idea to use mango. I was thinking grilled pineapple would also work, or a mix. Don't skip the cilantro.
This is the best recipe I've ever found in the NYT. Exactly perfect.
Wow!  Very flavorful!  We used the suya on the roasted butternut squash that we roasted to go with it.  We’ll be making this again.
This was amazing!  I made the Suya seasoning.  Make sure to serve some on the side.  Great start to my summer grilling recipe repertoire.
what about using PB2 peanut butter powder?  Should that work too?
Does the ginger knob imply the peel stays on and is grated with the ginger?
Recipe calls for ginger that is "scrubbed." That implies scrubbing the peel. The peel is edible, so you do not need to be too fussy.

Didn’t make the side salad but the chicken thighs were delicious, even after only marinating for 40min.

just one note, personal opinion (I made my own) Suya was not that spicy, either it is tame as listed or my tolerance is up there, but regardless, it was delicious.

on the side: I had some peanut sauce I made with thai green curry paste and sambal that I used as dipping sauce and it worked really well.
@João  Would you share that peanut sauce recipe?
Can one use fresh peaches instead of nectarines?
Can you taste the difference between a peach and a nectarine? That's your answer.
Lots of trouble for very little flavor

Out of curiosity I checked the Instacart shopping list to see if they could find suya spice mix for me. Nope. Their "close match" was actually a sweet lemon drink with "suya" in the name. Just another AI fail.

title: Pan-Seared Chicken With Mujdei Green Beans Recipe
author: Yewande Komolafe

comments.commentBody: Borderline inedible - surely 2 tablespoons of salt is a mistake. Teaspoons?
@Noah Where does it say 2 tablespoons of salt?
This has now been clarified in the tips section.
What does store-bought garlic mean here?   Does it mean buy garlic that is already peeled?
And why would that be the preference? Surely peeling garlic at the last minute means it's fresher and retains more of its pungency.

I adore garlic, but this much raw garlic sounds like a recipe for indigestion. Kenji Lopez-Alt recommends letting raw minced or crushed garlic sit with lemon juice for about ten minutes before mixing with other ingredients (e.g., for hummus) to temper its pungency, which I find works well. I'll try that here.

I gave this recipe 5 stars, but I think its reviews are suffering from the American palate’s fear of garlic and the deceiving difficulty of making the mujdei. Here are some of my tips:

1. Don’t fear the garlic! Use the full 3/4 cups. Yes, it’s a lot. But without this quantity, the mujdei won’t emulsify and it won’t take on the thick, opaque, creamy texture that makes it shine. If you’re set on using less garlic, reduce the other mujdei ingredients proportionally.

2. Crush the garlic cloves and soak them in lemon juice for 10-20 minutes to mute the garlic’s bite. Cover the cloves in the juice of 2-3 lemons. The garlic will also neutralize the lemon juice’s bite and you can use it to top your plate in place of lemon wedges.

3. Achieving the creamy oil/garlic emulsion of the mujdei is difficult. You can cheat by using a high powered blender like a Vitamix, which also lets you skip grating/pressing the garlic. Follow the recipe’s instructions and ingredient orders for the mujdei, but blend at high speed instead of whisking.

4. The green bean salad may seem overdressed, but if you want a big punch of flavor, it’s actually great like this.

5. For the chicken, I prefer covering the thighs in my favorite dry rub and making them in the air fryer, which lets you make the beans and chicken at the same time. If you go this route, allow the beans to cool for about 10 min before dressing them. 

I hope these tips help and I’d love to know how they work for you!

The Romanian word 'mujdei' came in the 18-th century from the French expression 'mousse d'ail' (garlic foam), which is not used or known in France for quite a while. Also in Romania almost nobody knows the origin of the word, believing it's an old Romanian word...
